blocks.html:
EXPOSURE IV
TOPOSby A. Grothendieck and J.L. Verdier
0. Introduction

We have seen in II various accuracy properties of categories of the form \(\widetilde{C}\) = category of bundles of sets on \(C\), where \(C\) is a small site, properties that we can be expressed by saying that in many respects, these categories (which we will call topos) inherit familiar properties from the category (Ens) of (small) sets. On the other hand, experience has taught that there is reason to consider various situations in Mathematicsespecially as a technical means to construct the corresponding categories of bundles (of sets), i.e. the corresponding “topos”. It appears that all the really important notions linked to a site (for example its cohomological invariants, studied in V, various other "topological" invariants, such its homotopy invariants studied recently by M. ARTIN and B. MAZUR [1] and the notions studied in the book by J. GIRAUD on non-commutative cohomology) are in fact expressed directly in terms of associated topos. With this in mind, it is appropriate to look at two sites as being essentially equivalent when the associated topos are equivalent categories, and to consider that the data of a site (at least in the case, especially important in practice, where its topology is less fine than its canonical topology) returns to that of a topos \(E\) (namely the associated topos, formed of the sheaves of sets on the site), and a family generating elements of \(E\) (see II 4.9, and 1.2.1 below). This point of view is analogous to that which consists of associating a group with any system of generators and any system of relationships between these generators, and to attach its interest rather to the structure of this group than to the system of generators and relationships which served to generate it (considered as incidental data of the situation). Besides the “comparison lemma” III 5.1. provides numerous examples of pairs of sites \(C\), \(C'\) non-isomorphic, and even non-equivalent as categories, and giving rise to equivalent topos, so that there is reason to consider \(C\) and \(C'\) as essentially equivalent.

In this presentation, we give a characterization of topos by properties simple exactitudes (due to J. GIRAUD), we study the natural notion of topos morphism, inspired by the notion of continuous application of one topological space in another, and we develop in the framework of topos certain familiar constructions in usual sheaf theory (sheaves \(\mathrm{Hom}\), tensor product bundles, supports). Finally, we show (following M. ARTIN) how we can reconstruct a topos from an “open” of it, from the complementary “closed”, and from a certain exact functor on the left which connects them, which, with little difference, can be chosen elsewhere arbitrarily.

We have here a process of joining together topos which, applied to topos coming from spaces ordinary topologies, will generally give a topos which will no longer be of the same type. This is a first indication of the remarkable stability of the notion of topos by various constructions natural, which is missing from the notion of topological space (from which the notion of topos is inspired). For a second remarkable example, let us also point out that of classifying topos relating to a group of a topos (cf. [1] or 5.9 below), inspired by the classic notion of classifying space of a topological group, and the notion of "modular topos" associated with various "module problems" in Algebraic Geometry or in Analytical Geometry [10] [13].

Other topos, such as the extended topos of a diagram (systematically studied in the present Seminar, from Exp. VII) or the crystalline topos of a relative diagram [6] are introduced in a manner natural when we want to develop for abstract algebraic varieties (and more generally diagrams) usable cohomology theories, which replace the classic Betti cohomology of algebraic varieties on the field of complexes.

We can therefore say that the notion of topos, a natural derivative of the fascist point of view in Topology, in turn constitutes a substantial broadening of the notion of topological space(∗), encompassing a large number of situations which previously were not considered to fall within topological intuition. The characteristic feature of such situations is that we have a notion of "location", a notion which is formalized precisely by the notion of site and, in the final analysis, by that of topos (via the topos associated with the site). As the term "topos" itself is intended precisely suggest it, it seems reasonable and legitimate to the authors of this Seminar to consider that the object of Topology is the study of topos (and not only topological spaces).

It seemed useful to us to include in this general presentation on the topos a fairly large number examples, many of which have only distant connections with the initial goal that this seminar, (i.e. the study of flat cohomology). The reader in a hurry, interested exclusively in equal cohomology, can of course omit without disadvantage the reading of these examples, as well moreover that of the greater part of the present presentation, to which it will suffice to refer in the event of need.

footnotes.html: (∗) Cf. [9], or 4.1 and 4.2 below, for the precise relationships between the notion of topos and that of topological space.

blocks.html: 1. Definition and characterization of topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

We call \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, or simply topos if none confusion is to be feared, a category \(E\) such that there exists a site \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(E\) is equivalent to category \(\widetilde{C}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-set bundles on \(C\).

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We always consider \(E\) as having its topology canonical (II 2.5), which therefore makes it a site, and even, by virtue of 1.1.2 d) below, a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (II 3.0.2). Unless expressly stated otherwise, we will not consider any other topology on \(E\) than the one we have just explained.
We saw in II 4.8, 4.11 that a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (I 1.1) satisfying the following conditions:

1. a)

    Finite projective limits are representable in \(E\).

  2. b)

    Direct sums indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) are representable in \(E\). They are disjoint and universal (II 4.5).

  3. c)

    The equivalence relations in \(E\) are effectively universal (I 10.6).

  4. d)

    \(E\) admits a generating family (II 4.9) indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
In fact, we will see that these intrinsic properties characterize the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2 (J. Giraud)

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. The following properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (1.1).

  2. ii)

    \(E\) satisfies conditions a), b), c) and d) of 1.1.2.

  3. iii)

    The \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundles on \(E\) for the canonical topology are representable, and \(E\) has a small generating family (condition 1.1.2 d)).

  4. iv)

    There is a category \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) and a fully faithful functor \(i : E \to \widehat{C}\) (where \(\widehat{C}\) designates the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-sheaves on \(C\)) admitting an adjoint to left \(a\) which is exact left.

  5. i')

    There exists a site \(C \in \mathcal{U}\), such that the projective limits are representable in \(C\) and that the topology of \(C\) is less fine than its canonical topology (II 2.5), such that \(E\) is equivalent to the category \(\widetilde{C}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-set bundles on \(C\).

Additionally:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2.1

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(C\) a subcategory full of \(E\), which we provide with the topology induced (III 3.1) by that of \(E\) (1.1.1). Consider the functor \[ E \longrightarrow \widetilde{C} \] which associates with all \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) the restriction to \(C\) of the functor represented by \(X\). This functor is a equivalence of categories if and only if \(\operatorname{ob} C\) is a generating family of \(E\).

The equivalence i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iv) immediately follows from II 5.5, and we have already recalled above that we have i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). As i') \(\Longrightarrow\) i) trivially, it remains to be proven ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) iii) and iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i'), which will be done in 1.2.4 and 1.2.3 below. The corollary is then obtained by noticing that the functor considered factors into \(E \to \widetilde{E} \to \widetilde{C}\), so that, \(E \to \widetilde{E}\) being an equivalence under (iii), the question comes down to determining when \(\widetilde{E} \to \widetilde{C}\) is an equivalence. We then conclude thanks to the “comparison lemma” III 4.1, cf. demonstration 1.2.3 below.
1.2.3. Proof of iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i').

Let \(\mathscr{X} = (X_i)_{i \in I}\), \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), a small family generating \(E\). As \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, the set of isomorphy classes of finite diagrams in \(E\) whose objects are elements \(X_i\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. Therefore the smallest set \(\mathscr{X}'\) of objects of \(E\), containing the finite projective limits of objects of \(\mathscr{X}\), is a union countable of small sets and is therefore small(∗). Posing \(\mathscr{X}^{(n+1)} = \mathscr{X}^{(n)(1)}\), and \(\mathscr{X} = \bigcup_n \mathscr{X}^{(n)}\), we see that, even if it means increasing the family of generators, we can assume that \(\mathscr{X}\) is stable under finite projective limits. Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(E\) is \(\mathcal{V}\)-small. For any object \(H\) of \(E\), let us denote \(I(H)\) the set \(\coprod_{i \in I} \mathrm{Hom}(X_i, H)\). Let \(H\) be an object of \(E\) and \(H' \hookrightarrow H\) the sub-bundle of \(H\), for the canonical topology, "reunion" of the images of the morphisms \(u : X_i \to H\), \((u,i) \in I(H)\) (II 4.1). Since \(H'\) is a sub-bundle of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle, \(H'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle. It is therefore representable. As the family \(\mathscr{X}\) is generative and for all \(i \in I\), the application \(\mathrm{Hom}(X_i, H') \to \mathrm{Hom}(X_i, H)\) is bijective, the morphism \(H' \hookrightarrow H\) is an isomorphism (II 4.9). As a result (II 6.1) the family \((u : X_i \to H)\), \((u,i) \in I(H)\), is covering for the canonical topology of \(E\).

So any object of \(E\) can be covered, for the canonical topology of \(E\), by objects from \(\mathscr{X}\). Let \(C\) be the full subcategory of \(E\) defined by the objects of \(\mathscr{X}\) and \(u : C \hookrightarrow E\) the inclusion functor. The topology \(\mathscr{C}\) induced on \(C\) by the canonical topology of \(E\) is less fine than the canonical topology of \(C\), and when \(\mathcal{U}\) has an infinite cardinal element, the projective limits finite are representable in \(C\). It follows from III 5.1 that the functor \(F \mapsto F \circ u\) is an equivalence of \(E\) on the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(C\) for the topology \(\mathscr{C}\), cqfd.
1.2.4. Demonstration of ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) iii).
The demonstration has four steps.

Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\), such that \(E\) is an element of \(\mathcal{V}\). Let \(\widetilde{E}\) be the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-sheaves on \(E\) for the canonical topology, and \(J_E : E \to \widetilde{E}\) the canonical functor.

Let \((g_i : G_i \to H)\), \(i \in I \in \mathcal{U}\), an epimorphic family of \(\widetilde{E}\). If the \(G_i\) and the fiber products \(G_i \times_H G_j\) are representable, then the bundle \(H\) is representable.

Indeed, the direct sum \(\coprod_{i \in I} G_i\) is representable in \(\widetilde{E}\) (II 4.1) by a representable beam \(G\) (property b) and II 4.6). The same is true for the direct sum \(K = \coprod_{(i,j) \in I \times I} G_i \times_H G_j\). Additionally, diagram \(K \rightrightarrows G \to H\) is correct and \(K\) is the fiber square of \(G\) above \(H\). (This last property is true in the category of pre-sheaves, therefore true in the category of sheaves (II 4.1)). We deduce, according to c) and II 4.7, that the beam \(H\) is representable.

Let \(X_\alpha\), \(\alpha \in A \in \mathcal{U}\), be a family of generators of \(E\). For everything beam \(H\), let us denote by \(I(H)\) the set \(\coprod_{\alpha \in A} \mathrm{Hom}_E(X_\alpha, H)\). The family \((u : X_\alpha \to H, (u,\alpha) \in I(H))\) is epimorphic in \(\widetilde{E}\). When \(H\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle, \(I(H)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Indeed, any sheaf \(H\) being the goal of an epimorphic family of morphisms whose sources are representable sheaves (I 3.4 and II 4.1), it is enough to show the first assertion when the beam \(H\) is representable. Let then be \(G\) the image of the family \((u : X_\alpha \to H, (u,\alpha) \in I(H))\). The morphism \(G \to H\) is a monomorphism. Consequently, we are in the situation of the first step, because \(X_\alpha \times_G X_\beta\) is isomorphic to \(X_\alpha \times_H X_\beta\) which is representable, and furthermore \(I(H)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\). We deduce that \(G\) is representable. But \(X_\alpha\) being a generating family, \(G \to H\) is an isomorphism. The last assertion is trivial by definition of \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundles.
Any sub-bundle of a representable beam is representable.

Indeed, let \(G \to H\) be a sub-sheaf of a representable sheaf. \(G\) is then a \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam. The family \((u : X_\alpha \to G, (u,\alpha) \in I(G))\) is therefore epimorphic in \(\widetilde{E}\) and indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\). Furthermore, the fiber products \(X_\alpha \times_G X_\beta\) are isomorphic to the fiber products \(X_\alpha \times_H X_\beta\) which are representable. Consequently, we are in the situation of the first not and \(G\) is representable.
All \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle is representable.

Indeed, by virtue of the first and second steps, it is enough to show that fiber products \(X_\alpha \times_H X_\beta\) are representable. However, these fiber products are sub-objects of the products \(X_\alpha \times X_\beta\). We therefore conclude with the third step. This completes the proof of Theorem 1.2.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.3

Of course, for a given \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), there is generally no preferred way of representing it up to equivalence in the form \(\widetilde{C}\), with \(C\) a small site; or, which essentially amounts to the same thing under 1.2.1 when we limit ourselves to the \(C\) whose topology is less fine than the canonical topology, there is no small generating family privileged in \(E\). When we stop imposing on \(C\) the condition that \(C\) be small, there is on the other hand (by virtue of 1.2 iii)) a completely canonical choice of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\) such that \(E\) is equivalent to \(\widetilde{C}\), namely \(E\) itself! This is one of the technical reasons why it is not convenient in practice of working only with small sites: in fact, the most important sites of all, namely the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, are not small! In addition, the topo generator sites which are introduced into many questions in algebraic geometry (or even in topology, cf. 2.5) are not small either; example: the site spread of a diagram (VIII 1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(E'\) be a category (not necessarily a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category), \(F\) a pre-bundle on \(E\) with values in \(E'\). So that \(F\) is a value bundle in \(E'\) (II 6.1), it is necessary and sufficient that \(F\) transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits in \(E\) into limits projectives in \(E'\).

Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) and such that \(E'\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-category. Component \(F\) with the functors \(\mathrm{Hom}(X',-) : E' \to \mathcal{V}\text{-Ens}\) defined by the objects \(X'\) of \(E'\), we are brought back to case where \(E' = \mathcal{V}\text{-Ens}\), where \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe. Suppose that \(F\) transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits into projective limits, then it immediately follows from the definitions that \(F\) is a sheaf, since a family covering \(X_i \to X\) in \(E\), by definition of the canonical topology of \(E\), allows to consider \(X\) as an inductive limit of the diagram \((X_i \times_X X_j \rightrightarrows X_i)\). Suppose \(F\) is a beam, and we prove that it transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits into projective limits. If \(\mathcal{V} \subset \mathcal{U}\), we can assume \(\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{U}\) and it suffices to apply criterion 1.2 iii). To treat the general case, it is necessary work a little more. Let \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\) generated by a generating family indexed by an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), and \(u : C \to E\) the inclusion functor. Let's get \(C\) from the topology induced by the canonical topology of \(E\) (III 4.5). Let us note \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the categories of bundles on \(C\), \(\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles on \(E\) for the canonical topology, \(J_E : E \to \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the canonical functor, \(i_{\mathcal{U},\mathcal{V}} : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the functor of inclusion. The diagram: \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{i_{\mathcal{U},\mathcal{V}}} & \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \\ E \ar[u] \ar[r]_-{J_E} & \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}} \ar[u] } \] where the vertical arrows are induced by the functor \(F \mapsto F \circ u\), is commutative. It results from the explicit construction of the associated beam functor (II 3) and of II 4.1 that the functor \(i_{\mathcal{U},\mathcal{V}}\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits. Furthermore, it follows from III 5.1 and 1.5 that the vertical arrows of (*) are category equivalences. As a result \(J_E : E \to \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) switches to \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits. For any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have: \[ F(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}}(J_E(X), F) . \]
So \(F\) transforms the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits of \(E\) into projective limits.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(E'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(f : E \to E'\) be functor. For \(f\) to admit an adjoint on the right, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits inductive.
This is an immediate consequence of 1.4 for the case \(E' = \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.6

Let \(E,E'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(f : E \to E'\) be a functor. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. i)

    \(f\) switches at \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits.

  2. ii)

    \(f\) admits a deputy on the right.

  3. iii)

    \(f\) is continuous (III 1.1).

The equivalence i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ii) was seen in 1.5. To prove i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iii), let's apply the definition of continuous functors, choosing a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) (so \(E,E'\) are \(\mathcal{V}\)-small sites). It must be expressed that for all \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundle \(F\) on \(E'\), the compound \(F \circ f\) is a sheaf on \(E\), i.e. (1.4) it transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits into limits projectives. For this it is enough that we have i), since by virtue of 1.4 \(F\) itself transforms \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits to projective limits; it is also necessary as we see by taking for \(F\) a representable functor.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.7

With notations of 1.6, so that \(f\) is the inverse image functor \(u^*\) for a topos morphism (3.1) \(u : E' \to E\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) is exact on the left and transforms epimorphic families into epimorphic families.

The necessity is trivial by definition (NB any right-exact functor transforms epimorphism to epimorphism). Sufficiency follows from III 2.6, which implies that \(f\) is continuous under the conditions indicated, therefore commutes to the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits under 1.6.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.8

With notations of 1.5, we can show that \(f\) admits an adjoint to left if and only if it commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits. In other words, a functor covariant \(E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) is representable if and only if it commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits projectives(∗). We only indicate the principle of the demonstration, which is done in two steps:

  1. a)

    Standard arguments [5, n° 195, § 3] show that if \(F\) commutes to \(\varprojlim\), it is prorepresentable by a strict projective system \((T_i)_{i \in I}\), where \(I\) is an ordered set filter, not necessarily small. We can assume that if \(i > j\), then \(T_i \to T_j\) is not a isomorphism, and under this hypothesis, \(F\) is representable if and only if \(I\) is small (which actually implies that the projective system is essentially constant).

  2. b)

    To prove that \(I\) is small, knowing that for any object \(X\) of \(E\), the set \(F(X) = \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(T_i, X)\) is, all you need is a small cogenerator family \((X_j)_{j \in J}\) (i.e. which is generator for the opposite category \(E^\circ\)). But we show that in a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) There is still a small cogeneration family.

  3. c)

    To prove this last point, we note by standard arguments [Toh] that all object \(X\) of \(E\) admits a monomorphism in an "injective" object; then that for any family generator \((L_\alpha)\) of \(E\), if we thus immerse each \(L_\alpha\) in an injective object \(I_\alpha\), the family \((I_\alpha)\) is a cogenerator.

footnotes.html: (∗) We reason here about the object classes of \(E\) up to isomorphism.
(∗) A more general statement is found in I 8.12.8, 8.12.9, the sketch of the demonstration which follows a) to c) corresponding to the demonstration given in loc. cit.

blocks.html: 2. Examples of topos

We have brought together in this issue quite a large number of typical examples of topos, which the reader will have already had the opportunity to encounter elsewhere, and which are intended for him facilitate access to the “yoga” of the topos. For other examples (from algebraic geometry) of topologies on sites, giving rise to as many topos, he will be able to consult SGA 3 IV 6, and (for the topos spreads out) presentation VII of this seminar. As we will hardly refer subsequently to the present number only for questions of notation or terminology, we leave it to the reader to do as an exercise in verifying the statements with which we have attached these examples for its instruction general. All the examples in this issue will be specified in 4, where we will examine their dependence functorial in relation to the data, and in the following issues by way of illustration of the notions general rules relating to topos.
2.1. Topos associated with a topological space

Let \(X\) be a small topological space, \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) the category of open spaces of \(X\), provided with the canonical topology (III 2.9.1). We will designate by \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) the topos of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\). This topos is equivalent to the category of topological spaces spread above \(X\), in associating with any such space \(X'\) on \(X\) the beam \(U \mapsto \Gamma(X'/U) = \mathrm{Hom}_X(U,X')\) on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) [TF]. We cannot help but sometimes note, through abuse of language, by the same letter \(X\) the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) defined by the topological space \(X\).

It is obviously the previous example which served mainly as a guide and intuitive support for the development of topos theory. Please note, however, that the topos deduced from the topological spaces are of a very particular nature, due in particular to the fact that they have been described by sites \(C = \mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) where all morphisms are monomorphisms (therefore whose category underlying is reduced to a preordered set). From this results in particular that the beams represented by the objects of \(C\) are sub-beams of the final beam, and consequently that the sub-bundles of the final beam form a generating family of the topos envisaged. This property is not not shared by most topos which are introduced naturally into algebraic geometry or algebra, cf. examples below. It is more or less characteristic of the topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (see 7.1.9 below).

We easily verify that the application \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\), which associates with all open \(X\) the beam that it represents, is a bijection of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) with all the sub-objects of the final object of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), this bijection even being an isomorphism for order structures natural, i.e. inducing an isomorphism of the corresponding categories. This suggests that it must be possible to reconstitute up to homeomorphism the topological space \(X\), when we know \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) close equivalence. We will see below (4.2) that this is indeed the case, with a slight restriction on \(X\).
2.2. Punctual or final topos, and empty or initial topos

When \(X\) is a topological space reduced to a single point, the functor \[ F \mapsto F(X) : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens} \] is an equivalence of categories. This shows in particular that the category \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We have seen on examples in Exp II that this \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos is typical from the point of view correctness properties, in that the verification of many properties (in particular properties of accuracy) of the general topos is reduced to this particular topos. The interpretation we give here in terms of the point topological space justifies the abuse of language consisting of calling topos punctual a topos equivalent to the category \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) (although, as a category, it is not at all equivalent to the one-off category!). It's the terminology that matches intuition correct geometry of the role played by these topos. We sometimes call, also by abuse of language, final topos a punctual topos, cf. 4.3; we will say "the final topos" for the topos \((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\).

When \(X\) is reduced to the empty topological space, therefore \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) to the point category, then a presheaf \(F\) on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) is a sheaf if and only if its value in the unique object \(\varnothing\) of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) is a set reduced to one point. It follows that \(\mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) is isomorphic to the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets reduced to a point, a category which is equivalent to the category punctual. From this we conclude in particular that the punctual category (as well as any \(\mathcal{U}\)-category equivalent to this one) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. It is sometimes called, through abuse of language, the empty topos or initial topos (see 4.4); we take care that it is not equivalent to the empty category.
2.3. Topos associated with an operator space

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(G\) be a discrete group operating on \(X\) by homeomorphisms. We then defined in [Toh. 5.1] the category of \(G\)-beams on \(X\), or as we will also say, beams on \((X,G)\); these are the bundles (of ensembles) on \(X\), equipped with compatible \(G\) operations with those of \(G\) on \(X\). We immediately see, using Giraud's criterion 1.2 iii), that this category is a topos (NB \(\mathcal{U}\) is implied in all this), which we will simply note \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\). When \(G\) is reduced to the unit group, we find example 2.1; when \(X\) is reduced to the point space, we finds the topos of the sets on which \(G\) operates on the left (or \(G\)-sets), also called topos discrete group classifier\(G\), and denoted \(B_G\). We easily check that the only sub-object of the object final of the topos \(B_G\) is \(e\) or the empty bundle \(\varnothing\), in particular, if \(G\) is not reduced to the group unit, the sub-objects of the final object of the classifying topos \(B_G\) do not form a family generating \(B_G\). So \(B_G\) is not equivalent to a topos of the type \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) considered in 2.1.

The notion of \(G\)-beam was introduced in loc. cit. to develop the theory cohomology of \(G\)-abelian bundles. Interpreting the latter as the abelian sheaves of the topos \((X,G)\), said theory is included in that of Exp. V, developed within the framework of the topos generals.

We can propose more generally to attach an appropriate topos to a topological space \(X\), provided with a topological group \(G\) (not necessarily discrete) of automorphisms, which would give birth of an adequate cohomological theory. Likewise in the context of differentiable varieties, or real or complex analytical or diagrams. It is indeed possible, cf. 2.5 below.
2.4. Classifying topos of a Group

Let \(E\) be a topos, and \(G\) be a Group of \(E\). Let \((E,G)\) be the category of objects of \(E\) on which \(G\) operates. We immediately see, thanks to Giraud's criterion, that it is a topos. We call it topos classifier of the Group \(G\), and we note it \(B_G\). When \(E\) is the point topos (2.2) i.e. when \(G\) is an ordinary group, we find the classifying topos of 2.3.

The terminology adopted here is justified by the fact that the topos \(B_G\) plays a universal role for the classification of "torsors" (or homogeneous main bundles) under \(G\), or more generally under the \(G_{E'} = f^*(G)\), where \(E'\) is a topos "above \(E\)" i.e. with a morphism \(f : E' \to E\) (see 3.1 below). This role, explained in [3 Chap V] or in 5.9 below, shows that \(B_G\) plays, in the context of the topos, the same role as the classic classifying spaces of topological groups in homotopic theory of topological spaces. The latter can be viewed (see 2.5) as a weakened version of the first, obtained by retaining the topos classifying only the "type of homotopy" of the said topos, in a suitable sense which there is no need to specify here.
2.5. “Big site” and “big topos” of a topological space. Classifying topos of a topological group(∗)

Let \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Esp}\) or simply \((\mathrm{Esp})\) be the category of topological spaces \(\in \mathcal{U}\). We knows that in \((\mathrm{Esp})\) finite projective limits are representable. Let us consider on \((\mathrm{Esp})\) the pretopology (I 1.3) for which \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) is the set of surjective families of immersions open \(u_i : X_i \to X\). We will consider \((\mathrm{Esp})\) as a site using the generated topology by the previous pretopology. For any object \(X\) of \((\mathrm{Esp})\), consider the category \[ (\mathrm{Esp})_{/X} \] objects from \((\mathrm{Esp})\) above \(X\), i.e. topological spaces above \(X\), such as a site, thanks to the topology induced by that of \((\mathrm{Esp})\) via the forgetting functor \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X} \to (\mathrm{Esp})\) (III 5.2 4)). This site is called the big site associated with \(X\). We will pay attention that it is not \(\in \mathcal{U}\); this is also not a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site within the meaning of II 3.0.2, so precautions are necessary for it apply the usual results. To overcome this drawback, we can choose a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\), so that \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) becomes a \(\mathcal{V}\)-site, and we can work with the associated \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\), which may be noted \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) and will be called the gros topos of \(X\). If we are reluctant to enlarge \(\mathcal{U}\), we can choose a cardinal \(c\) increasing the cardinals of \(X\) and all spaces topologies that we intend to involve in reasoning (most often, \(\mathrm{Sup}(\mathrm{card}\, X, \mathrm{card}\, \mathbf{R})\) will be sufficient!), and we replace \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) with the subcategory \((\mathrm{Esp})'_{/X}\) formed from \(X'\) on \(X\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, X' \le c\), provided with the induced topology, and we note \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) the topos of the beams on this site. To fix the ideas, suppose that it is the first definition that has been adopted.

The advantage of the large topos of \(X\) over the small one is that the site which defines it contains \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) as full subcategory; as the topology of this site is clearly less fine than the canonical, we see that the canonical functor of \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) in \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\), associating to all space \(X'\) on \(X\) the beam it represents, is fully faithful. As a result, a space \(X'\) on \(X\) is known up to \(X\)-isomorphism when we know the sheaf \((\in \mathrm{TOP}(X))\) that it defines; so the notion of beam on (the large site of) \(X\) can be considered as a generalization of that of topological space above \(X\), with the help of which all constructions of the theory of sheaves make sense for topological spaces on \(X\).

Thus, when \(G\) is a group object of the category \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) of topological spaces above \(X\), we can associate it with the classifying topos \(B_G\) (2.4), hence cohomology groups classifying, classifying homotopy groups etc. (defined as the corresponding invariants of the \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos \(B_G\)). In particular, when \(X\) is a point space, \(G\) identifies with a topological group ordinary. We can verify, using the usual local conditions ensuring that the cohomology singularity of Cartesian products \(G^n\) coincides with cohomology in the sense of sheaves (for constant coefficients, say), for example if \(G\) is locally contractible, that the cohomology of the topos classifier of \(G\) is canonically isomorphic to that of the classifying space of \(G\) in the sense of topologists.

The introduction of classifying topos (via “big sites”) has on classifying spaces the advantage of providing a richer theory, since they notably provide invariants cohomologies useful for coefficients more general than constant or locally coefficients constant. Furthermore, the definition considered here obviously adapts to other usual contexts: differentiable varieties, analytical varieties or spaces (real or complex, your choice), diagrams. This point of view makes it possible in particular to make the link between the study of classes characteristic of the point of view traditional and from the "arithmetic" point of view, considering the "classical groups" as coming from of schemes defined on the ring of integers; cf. [7] for indications in this direction. Likewise, the general results of J. GIRAUD [3] on the classification of Group extensions, developed in the very general and very flexible framework of topos, can thanks to "big topos" specialize in results on the classification of extensions of topological groups, or of real or complex Lie groups, which do not seemed hardly known to topologists except in the case of extensions with abelian core [11].
2.6. Topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\)

Let \(C\) be a small category. Then the category \(\widehat{C}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundles on \(C\) is obviously a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, since it is of the form \(\widetilde{C}\), where we provide \(C\) with the chaotic topology. We will give some details below on the relationships between \(C\) and \(\widehat{C}\). Let us only note here that a topos \(E\) equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\) is of a rather special nature, due to the fact thatit admits a small generating family \((X_i)\) formed of projective connected objects, i.e. objects \(X\) such as the functor \(Y \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) transforms epimorphisms into epimorphisms and sums into sums: it suffices in effect of taking in \(\widehat{C}\) the generating family formed by the functors represented by the \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\). Note also that if in a topos \(E\) we have a covering family \(X_i \to X\), with \(X_i\) which are projective and related, then any other covering family of \(X\) is increased (I 4.3.2, 4.3.3) by the previous. Consequently, in a topos \(E\) of the form \(\widehat{C}\) every object \(X\) admits a covering family increasing all others. A topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (2.1), with \(X\) a topological space whose points are closed, only has the previous property if \(X\) is discrete.

When the category \(C\) has a single object, \(C\) identifies with a monoid \(G\). A pre-beam on \(C\) is then identified with a set on which \(G\) operates on the right (since it is a functor \(G^\circ \to (\mathrm{Ens})\)), and \(\widehat{C}\) is the topos of monoid sets of right-hand operators, which can also be denoted \(B_{G^\circ}\) (the topos of sets with monoid of operators \(G^\circ\) (the monoid opposite to \(G\)). When \(G\) is a group, using the isomorphism \(g \mapsto g^{-1}\) of \(G\) on \(G^\circ\), we find the classifying topos \(B_G\) from 2.3.
2.7. Classifying topos of a pro-group

Let \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\) be a projective system of groups, with \(G_i, I \in \mathcal{U}\). We assume the strict projective system, i.e. surjective \(G_j \to G_i\) transition morphisms. If \(E\) is a set, we call operation of \(\underline{G}\) on \(E\) (on the left, let's say) the following structure: a) a family \((E_i)_{i \in I}\) of parts of \(E\), of meeting \(E\); b) for any \(i \in I\), an operation of the group \(G_i\) on set \(E_i\); we further assume that these data are subject to the following condition: for \(j \ge i\), \(E_i\) is the subset of \(E_j\) formed from the fixed elements under the core group of \(G_j \to G_i\). It is also said that \(\underline{G}\) operates on \(E\) (on the left) if we have given ourselves an operation of \(\underline{G}\) on \(E\) (on the left). The sets \(\in \mathcal{U}\) with an operation of \(\underline{G}\) clearly form a category. We immediately see, thanks according to Giraud's criterion, that this category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We note it \(B_{\underline{G}}\) and we call it topos classifier of \(\underline{G}\). When \(I\) admits an initial object \(i_o\), posing \(G = G_{i_o}\), we find the topos classifier of 2.3.

Another important example is where the groups \(G_i\) are finite, so that \(G = \varprojlim G_i\) is a totally discontinuous compact topological group, or profinite group. A operation of \(\underline{G}\) on \(E\) then amounts to an operation of \(G\) on \(E\) which is continuous, or what amounts at the same time, such that the stabilizer of any point of \(E\) is an open subgroup of \(G\). The topos classifying \(B_{\underline{G}}\) will also be noted \(B_G\), where, of course, \(G\) must be considered as having its profined topology.

It is easy to verify, using the remarks of 2.6, that the topos \(B_{\underline{G}}\) defined by a strict projective system \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\) of groups is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\) only if this projective system is essentially constant; in the case of a profinite group, this means that this group is actually finished.

The following geometric interpretation of the classifying topos \(B_G\) of a discrete group \(G\) is useful, to give a correct geometric intuition of these topos. (Cf. also, in the same sense, 4.5, 5.8, 5.9 and 7.2 below.) Let \(X\) be a connected, locally connected and locally topological space simply connected, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(G\) its fundamental group in \(x\). (NB it is known that up to isomorphism, any discrete group \(G\) can be obtained thus.) Then the Galois theory of coatings of \(X\) provides an equivalence between the category \(B_G\) of \(G\)-assemblies, and the category of spread coverings of \(X\), i.e. spaces \(X'\) on \(X\) which are locally \(X\)-isomorphic to \(X\)-spaces of the form \(X \times I\), where \(I\) is a discrete space. (Compare SGA 1 V 4.5). The latter can also be interpreted as the category of locally constant sheaves on \(X\), i.e. the category of objects locally constants (IX 2.0) of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\).

When \(G\) is a profinite group, we have an analogous geometric interpretation of \(B_G\), as the category of \(X\)-schemes which are sums of finished coverings of a connected \(X\) scheme, provided of a geometric point \(x\) and an isomorphism \(G \simeq \pi_1(X,x)\). It is also known that any group profini can be obtained as the fundamental group of a suitable connected diagram (spectrum of a body if we want). Finally, we also encounter pro-groups (not necessarily profinis nor essentially constants) for the classification of coverings of related and locally related spaces which are not not locally simply related, or the classification of spreading coverings not necessarily finished or ind-finite non-normal connected schemes. For the latter case, cf. SGA 3 X 6.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.7.5

Define for a topos \(E\) the notion of connectivity, locality connectivity(∗), simple connectivity and simple local connectivity. Define the notion of constant object and locally constant of \(E\) (cf. IX 2.0). Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a morphism of topos (3.1), with \(E'\) simply connected (for example \(E'\) the point topos (2.2)), and \(E\) connected and locally connected. Define a strict pro-group \(\pi_1(E,f) = (G_i)_{i \in I} = \underline{G}\) (called fundamental pro-group of \(E\) in \(f\)) and a equivalence of categories of \(B_{\underline{G}}\) with the category of locally constant objects of \(E\).(∗) Show that when \(E\) is locally simply connected, \(\pi_1(E,f)\) is essentially constant and is identified therefore to an ordinary discrete group \(\pi_1(E,f)\), which is called the fundamental group of \(E\) in \(f\). Show that any strict pro-group \(\underline{G}\) is isomorphic (like pro-group) to the fundamental pro-group of a topos connected and locally connected suitable in a suitable \(f\), with \(E'\) the punctual topos (take \(E = B_{\underline{G}}\), and \(f : E' \to E\) defined by the forget functor \(f^* : E \to E' = (\mathrm{Ens})\)). When \(\underline{G}\) is essentially constant, i.e. isomorphic (like pro-group) to an ordinary discrete group, prove that we can take above \(E\) locally simply connected (take again \(E = B_{\underline{G}}\)).
2.8. Example of a false topos

Let \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\) be a strict pro-group, where \(I\) is ordered filtering and where \(i > j\) implies that \(G_i \to G_j\) is not an isomorphism. Suppose we have \(\mathrm{card}(I) \notin \mathcal{U}\). Let's consider the category of sets \(E \in \mathcal{U}\) on which \(\underline{G}\) operates on the left (2.7.1). This is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and we see as in 2.7.1 that this category satisfies conditions a), b), c) of 1.1.2. However this is not a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, because we see that it does not admit a generating family which is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. We see just as it is not a \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos for any universe \(\mathcal{V}\).

footnotes.html: (∗) The introduction of these sites and topos is due to Mr. GIRAUD, who also highlighted their advantages over the "small" traditional site.
(∗) We can take inspiration from SGA 3 X 6.

blocks.html: 3. Topos morphisms

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We call morphism of \(E\) in \(E'\), or sometimes (by misuse of language) continuous application of \(E\) in \(E'\), a triple \(u = (u_*, u^*, \varphi)\), formed from functors \[ u_* : E \to E' , \qquad u^* : E' \to E \] and an isomorphism \(\varphi\) "addition" of bifunctors in \(X' \in \operatorname{Ob} E'\), \(Y \in \operatorname{Ob} E\): \[ \varphi : \mathrm{Hom}_E(u^*(X'),Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(X', u_*(Y)) , \] the functor \(u^*\) being further subject to the condition of being exact on the left, i.e. of commuting to finite projective limits. The functor \(u_*\) is called the direct image functor for the morphism of topos \(u\), the functor \(u^*\) is called the inverse image functor for the topos morphism \(u\), the isomorphism \(\varphi\) is called the adjunction isomorphism, for \(u\).

Unless expressly stated to the contrary, we will subsequently designate, for a topos morphism \(u : E \to E'\), by \(u_*\) and \(u^*\) the corresponding direct image and inverse image functors(∗). We note that, \(u_*\) being adjoint to the right of \(u^*\) and \(u^*\) being adjoint to the left of \(u_*\) by the isomorphism of addition \(\varphi\), each of the two functors \(u_*\), \(u^*\) determines the other up to unique isomorphism, according to the well-known sorites of adjoint functors [14]. In practice, depending on the case, it may be more convenient to define a topos morphism \(u : E \to E'\) either by the data of \(u^* : E' \to E\), or by the data from \(u_* : E \to E'\); in the first case, we simply have to check that the given functor \(u^*\) admits an adjunct on the right, and that it is exact on the left. In the second, that the given functor \(u_*\) admits an adjunct on the right which is exact on the left. In either case, we deduce from the data partial, thanks to the choice of an adjoint functor and an addition isomorphism, a topos morphism \(u : E \to E'\), and the latter will be "unique up to unique isomorphism" in terms of the data \(u^*\) resp. \(u_*\), in a fairly clear sense, and which will also be fully explained below (3.2.1).

If \(u : E \to E'\) is a topos morphism, it results from the properties of the adjoint functors (I 2.11) that the direct image functor \(u_* : E \to E'\) commutes at projective limits, and the functor \(u^* : E' \to E\) commutes at inductive limits(∗). As we further assume that the latter is exact at left i.e. commutes to finite projective limits, we therefore see in particular that \(u^*\) is exact. We can therefore say that it is the inverse image functor \(u^*\), in the pair \((u_*, u^*)\), which has the most remarkable accuracy properties. These properties ensure that for any type of structure algebraic \(\Sigma\) whose data can be described in terms of data of arrows between the sets base and sets deduced from them by repeated application of projective limit operations finite and any inductive limits, and for any "object of \(E'\) provided with a structure of species \(\Sigma\)" (notion which has meaning thanks to the internal accuracy properties of the topos \(E'\) (II 4.1)), its image by \(u^*\) is equipped with the same structures. Rather than enter into the uninviting task of giving precise meaning to this statement and to justify it formally, we advise the reader to explain it and to convince of its validity for types of structure such as that of group, ring, module over a ring, comodule over a ring, bigebre over a ring, torsor under a group. (In these examples, the first three species of structure are defined in terms of projective limits finite exclusively, while the other notions implicitly imply constructions making also calls for inductive limits.) Moreover, the functor \(u^*\) "commutes" at all operations usual functorial statements made in terms of such structures, more precisely to all operations which can be expressed in terms of \(\varinjlim\), and finite \(\varprojlim\): constructions of free objects (groups or free modules, e.g.) generated by an object, tensor products (see § 12 below) etc.

As for the direct image functor \(u_* : E \to E'\), which commutes to projective limits, it consequently "respects" any algebraic structure on an object (or a family of objects) of \(E\), definable in terms of projective boundaries exclusively (such as group, ring or module structures on a ring, among the previous examples). On the other hand, the functor \(u_*\) is generally not exact right i.e. it does not generally commute at finite inductive limits, and even does not transform into general epimorphism in epimorphism (it is moreover this lack of accuracy of the functor \(u_*\) which is the source of these cohomological properties, which will be studied (from the point of view of homological algebra commutative) in the following discussion). Consequently, it does not extend, in general, into a functor on objects of the comodule type, or bigebra, or torsor under a group, and does not generally commute to operations such as "free generated module", tensor product of modules, etc.

In practice, when we are in the presence of a \(f : E \to F\) functor of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos in a other, it is necessary to explain all the accuracy properties, including the possible existence of adjoint functors on the left or on the right (see footnote 26), to arrive at a understanding of the "geometric nature" of \(f\), understanding which will generally be a guide essential for a correct geometric intuition of the situation. So, if it turns out that \(f\) commutes to arbitrary inductive limits and to finite projective limits, it is appropriate to write \(f\) in the form \[ f = u^* , \] where \[ u : F \to E \] is a topos morphism, i.e. to interpret \(f\) as an "inverse image" functor by an "application continue" of topos. When \(f\) commutes to any projective limits, therefore it admits an adjoint to the left, and if the latter (which a priori commutes at any inductive limits) commutes additionally at finite projective limits, it is appropriate to write \(f\) in the form \[ f = v_* , \] where \[ v : E \to F \] is a topos morphism. In certain cases, it may happen that \(f\) satisfies both one and the other of the two properties considered (see 4.10 for an example). In this case, it is appropriate to introduce simultaneously the morphisms of topos \[ u : F \to E \quad \text{et} \quad v : E \to F , \] which give rise to a sequence of three adjoint functors (I 5.3): \[ v^* , \qquad v_* = u^* = f , \qquad u_* . \] It is then appropriate to carefully distinguish between the topos morphisms \(u\) and \(v\), under penalty of losing the geometric intuition of the situation.

We will note in this regard that when between two topos \(E,F\) we have a sequence of three functors assistants \[ e , f , g \qquad (e,g : F \rightrightarrows E, \ f : E \to F) , \] then \(f\) commutes to any inductive limits and projective limits, so it can always be put in the form \(u^*\), where \(u : F \to E\) is a topos morphism. So \(g\) is therefore written \(g = u_*\). By against, of course, \(e\) can only be written in the form \(v^*\) (and then \(f\) in the form \(v_*\)) only if it commutes from more at finite projective limits. This will obviously be the case if he himself is the deputy to the right of a fourth functor \(d\). Without conditions of this nature, one will often write \[ e = u_! \] for the left adjoint of an inverse image functor \(f = u^*\), when such a left adjoint exists, this notation being suggested by the example of an open immersion \(u : X \to Y\) of topological spaces. From even, if \(e\) is of the form \(v^*\), i.e. \(f\) of the form \(v_*\), we sometimes note \(g = v^!\) the adjoint to the right of a direct image functor \(v_*\), when this adjoint exists(∗).

Let \(E\), \(E'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and \[ u = (u_*, u^*, \varphi) , \qquad v = (v_*, v^*, \psi) : E \rightrightarrows E' \] two topos morphisms from \(E\) into \(E'\). We call morphism of \(u\) in \(v\) any morphism of \(u_*\) in \(v_*\) (in the sense of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,E')\) of functors of \(E\) in \(E'\)). The morphisms of topos morphisms are composed in an obvious way, and in this way we define a category, which is in makes a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (I 7.8) noted \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E') , \] called category of morphisms (or continuous applications) of \(E\) in \(E'\). We then define obvious way a functor \[ u \mapsto u_* : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,E') , \] functor which is fully faithful by definition of the arrows in the first member (but which is not injective on objects).

We will pay attention that, if \(u\) and \(v\) are given as above, the theory of functors adjoints provides a canonical bijection \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u_*, v_*) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(v^*, u^*) ; \] in particular, we obtain a contrafunctor on \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E')\): \[ u \mapsto u^* : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E')^\circ \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,E') . \] In accordance with geometric intuition, according to which the direct image functor \(u_*\) "goes in the same meaning" that the application continues which gives rise to it, it is therefore necessary to define the direction of the arrows for morphisms between topos morphisms in terms of direct image functors, and not in terms of inverse image functors (although it is the latter which, as we have seen, have the properties accuracy characteristics of the notion of topos morphism).

Having defined the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E')\) of the morphisms of the topos \(E\) in the topos \(E'\), the notion of isomorphy between two morphisms \(u,v\) of \(E\) in \(E'\) is also defined. In practice, it there is no need to essentially distinguish between two isomorphic topos morphisms, at least when we have a canonical isomorphism between the two (just as there is often no need to distinguish between two elements of a category, when we give ourselves a canonical isomorphism between them). Let us point out in this regard that most often, when we deal with diagrams of morphisms of topos and questions of commutativity of such diagrams (notion which makes sense thanks to (3.3)), it is only a question of commutativity up to ("canonical") isomorphism; by abuse of language, we then treat these diagrams as effectively commutative diagrams.

We can think of justifying this abuse of language by introducing the whole \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E')/\mathrm{Isom}\) of the morphisms of \(E\) in \(E'\) up to isomorphism, and calling morphism such an isomorphy class, instead of following definition 3.2. But this faces disadvantages very serious which arise, each time we try to identify two isomorphic objects of a category, without having a canonical isomorphism between them. Experience proves that such a point of view is impractical, and that we must keep the "fine" notion 3.1 of the notion of morphism between morphisms of topos, even if it means being obliged, sometimes, to fight with compatibilities between canonical isomorphisms(∗).

Let \(E\), \(E'\), \(E''\) be three \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and consider morphisms of topos \[ u : E \to E' , \qquad v : E' \to E'' . \] The theory of adjoint functors then gives us an addition isomorphism between the functors compounds \(v_* u_*\) and \(u^* v^*\), in terms of the addition isomorphisms for the pairs \((u_*,u^*)\) and \((v_*,v^*)\). On the other hand, the functor \(u^* v^*\) is left-exact, as composed of two left-exact functors. left. As a result, we find a morphism of \(E\) in \(E''\), which we call compound of morphisms \(u\) and \(v\), and that we note \(vu\): \[ vu : E \to E'' . \] We then verify trivially that the composition of the morphisms is associative, and that for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), there is a morphism of \(E\) in itself which is a two-sided unit for composition: this is the morphism \((\mathrm{id}_E, \mathrm{id}_E, \varphi)\), where \(\varphi\) is the obvious adjunction isomorphism of \(\mathrm{id}_E\) with himself. Let then \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\). We define a category \[ (\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Top}) , \] whose objects are the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos which are \(\in \mathcal{V}\), the arrows are the morphisms between such \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and the composition of the arrows being that which we have just explained.

Actually, the composition application \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E') \times \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E'') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E'') \] is the map induced on objects by a “composition of morphisms functor”: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E') \times \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E'') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E'') , \] whose effect on the arrows is the usual "convolution product" operation for morphisms between functors (here direct image functors). These composition functors satisfy a property of (strict) associativity, for four topos \(E, E', E'', E'''\), specifying the associativity of the composition of the topos morphisms. We can also say, in language that is beginning to become familiar [2] [9], that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos are the objects (or 0-arrows) of a 2-category, of which the 1-arrows are the morphisms of topos, and the 2-arrows are the morphisms of morphisms of topos.

This is the fact that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (elements of a universe \(\mathcal{V}\)) form a 2-category, and no longer only an ordinary category like ordinary topological spaces, which constitutes from the point of view technique the most important difference between the theory of topos and that of topological spaces. This fact is the source of certain technical complications to which we have already alluded, but also of essentially new facts compared to the traditional topology.

The fact that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (elements of a universe \(\mathcal{V}\)) form a 2-category (3.3.2) allows in particular to define the notion of equivalence of two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), \(E'\): we will say that \(E\) and \(E'\) are equivalent if there exist topos morphisms \(u : E \to E'\) and \(v : E' \to E\), such as the compounds \(uv\) and \(vu\) are isomorphic respectively to the identical morphism of \(E\) and \(E'\); we then say that the morphisms \(u\) and \(v\) are quasi-inverse equivalences of each other.

We immediately see that for the morphism of topos \(u : E \to E'\) to be an equivalence, it It is necessary and sufficient that \(u_*\) be an equivalence, or what amounts to the same thing, that \(u^*\) be an equivalence. (Use the fact that a functor \(f : E \to E'\) between two topos which is an equivalence is both of the form \(u_*\) and of the form \(v^*\), with \(u\) and \(v\) topos morphisms); and so that \(E\) and \(E'\) are equivalent within the meaning of the preceding paragraph, it is necessary and sufficient that they are equivalent as categories (i.e. as objects of the 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-cat})\)). As expected, this shows that the notions of equivalence introduced do not depend on the choice of the universe \(\mathcal{V}\) of 3.3.1.

Practically, there is most often no need to essentially distinguish between \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos equivalent, just as there is often no need to essentially distinguish between two categories equivalent, - provided however that we have an explicit equivalence from one to the other, or at all minus an equivalence defined up to unique isomorphism. It is the notion of equivalence of topos which here replaces the traditional notion of homeomorphy between two topological spaces. See example 4.2 below for the precise relationships between these two notions.

footnotes.html: (∗) It is sometimes also necessary to write \(u^{-1}\) instead of \(u^*\), cf.

(∗) Moreover (1.5 and 1.6), for a functor \(u_* : E \to E'\) resp. \(u^* : E' \to E\) given, this functor admits an adjoint on the left (resp. on the right) if and only if it commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits (resp. to \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits).
(∗) See below, in the case of abelian sheaves.
(∗) For examples of such battles (victorious, it seems) we refer the reader to the book by Mrs. M. HAKIM on relative diagrams [9].

blocks.html: 4. Examples of topos morphisms
Here we take the examples from 2, using the notion of topos morphism. The Comments from 2.0 also apply to this issue. The universe \(\mathcal{U}\) will generally be implied.
<h3 id="IV.4.1">4.1. The Top(X) topos for a variable topological space

Either a continuous application \[ f : X \to Y \] of topological spaces, we will canonically associate a topos morphism with it \[ \mathrm{Top}(f) \ \text{ou} \ f : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y) , \] with the notations of 2.1. When defining \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) as \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\), the most convenient description of \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\) is by the direct image functor of sheaves \[ f_* : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y) , \] defined by the formula \[ f_*(F) = F \circ f^{-1} , \] where \[ f^{-1} : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X) \] is the obvious functor \(U \mapsto f^{-1}(U)\). We have already noted that this functor is continuous and exact on the left, therefore defines a functor \(f_*\) above, admitting a right adjoint \(f^*\) which is exact on the left (III 1.9.1). Of course, strictly speaking, the morphism of Topos \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\) depends on the choice of the adjoint to right \(f^*\) of \(f_*\), therefore is only defined up to canonical isomorphism. We will subsequently dispense with expressly report phenomena of this kind.

When we adopt the "spread spaces" point of view to define \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), it is the image functor reverse \[ f^* : \mathrm{Top}(Y) \to \mathrm{Top}(X) \] which is the most convenient to define the morphism of Topos \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\), by simply setting \[ f^*(Y') = X \times_Y Y' \] for any spread space \(Y'\) over \(Y\); it is obvious that the fiber product is indeed a space spread over \(X\), and that the functor \(f^*\) thus obtained is left exact and commutes to any \(\varinjlim\), and defined by following a topos morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\). For the compatibility of the two definitions obtained, we refer to [TF].

When we have two continuous composable applications \[ X \xrightarrow{\ f\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Z , \] we find a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Top}(gf) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Top}(g)\mathrm{Top}(f) \] of topos morphisms. These transitivity isomorphisms, for three continuous applications composable \(f,g,h\) satisfy a compatibility relation which we dispense with writing here, and which is none other than that envisaged in SGA 1 VI 7.4 B) (for \(\mathscr{E} = (\mathrm{Esp})^\circ\)). It can be expressed in saying that for \(X\) variable in category \((\mathrm{Esp})\), \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Top}(X) \] is a "pseudo-functor" \[ (4.1.1.1) \qquad (\mathcal{U}\text{-esp}) \to (\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-top}) , \] or also, in the terminology of 2-categories, that we have a non-strict 2-category functor [9]. In In practice, we will most often indulge in the abuse of language consisting of identifying \(\mathrm{Top}(gf)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(g)\mathrm{Top}(f)\), that is to say to reason as if (4.1.1.1) was a real category functor ordinary. We will allow ourselves similar abuses of language in the other examples which will be treated. below.

The preceding considerations immediately extend to the case of topos associated with topological spaces with groups of operators (2.3). If \(f = (f^{\mathrm{es}}, f^{\mathrm{gr}})\), \[ f : (X,G) \to (Y,H) \] is a morphism of operator spaces (where \[ f^{\mathrm{es}} : X \to Y \ \text{et} \ f^{\mathrm{gr}} : G \to H \] are respectively continuous maps and group morphisms, compatible in one sense obvious), we associate it with a topos morphism \[ \mathrm{Top}(f) \ \text{ou} \ f : \mathrm{Top}(X,G) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y,H) , \] the definition of which is left to the reader. When the groups \(G\) and \(H\) are the unit groups, we find the definition of 4.1.1; when on the other hand it is the spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) which are reduced to a point, we finds as inverse image functor \(f^*\) the functor "restriction of the group of operators" \[ f^* : B_H \to B_G , \] associating with all \(H\)-set the \(G\)-set that it defines thanks to \(f : G \to H\). We will find this example in still other forms in 4.5 and 4.6.1.

Given a continuous map \(f : X \to Y\) of topological spaces, we associate it also a morphism on the corresponding "big topos" (2.5) \[ \mathrm{TOP}(f) \ \text{ou} \ f : \mathrm{TOP}(X) \to \mathrm{TOP}(Y) , \] most conveniently defined by the inverse image functor \[ f^* : \mathrm{TOP}(Y) \to \mathrm{TOP}(X) , \] which is none other than the restriction functor. This morphism \(\mathrm{TOP}(f)\) is a special case of the morphism called "inclusion" for an induced topos, which will be studied in 5. We thus see that, subject to the same reservations that in 4.1.1, the topos \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) can be considered as a functor in \(X\), for \(X\) variable in \((\mathrm{Esp})\).
4.2. Loyalty properties of \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Top}(X)\)

We propose to specify to what extent a topological space \(X\) can be reconstitute in terms of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), and for this purpose it is appropriate to describe, for two spaces \(X\) and \(Y\), the category of morphisms of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) in \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) (3.2), so as to be able to specify the fidelity properties of the "functor" \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Top}(X)\). We limit ourselves to stating the results to which this is achieved by referring the reader to [9] for details. The reader who wants to do the exercise of verification itself will be able to consult the exer. 7.8.

Recall that a topological space \(X\) is said to be sober if any closed irreducible part of \(X\) has exactly one wildcard point. Note that almost all spaces used in practice are sober; this is particularly the case with a separate space, more generally with a space in which all the points are closed, or the space underlying a diagram. If \(X\) is a topological space, we associate it with (loc. cit. or EGA \(0_{\mathrm{I}}\), reissue) a sober topological space \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) and a continuous application \[ (4.2.1.1) \qquad \varphi : X \to X_{\mathrm{sob}} \] which is universal for continuous applications of \(X\) in sober spaces; in other words, we constructs a left adjoint functor \(X \mapsto X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) of the inclusion functor \((\mathrm{Esp}_{\mathrm{sob}}) \to (\mathrm{Esp})\) of the category of sober spaces in that of "any" topological spaces (the quotation marks recalling that there is a universe!). The explicit construction is done by taking as points of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) the parts closed irreducibles of \(X\), as open sets of the form \(U'\), where \(U\) is an open of \(X\) and where \(U' \subset X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) designates the set of closed irreducible parts of \(X\) which meet \(U\). The app (4.2.1.1) is obtained by associating to all \(x \in X\) the adhesion of \(\{x\}\). The space \(X\) is sober if and only if the previous application is bijective, therefore a homeomorphism.

We see that the functor \[ \varphi^{-1} : \mathrm{Ouv}(X_{\mathrm{sob}}) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X) \] induced by \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, which implies that the topos morphism \[ \mathrm{Top}(\varphi) : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X_{\mathrm{sob}}) \] defined by \(\varphi\) is also an isomorphism. This explains a priori why \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) must be introduced necessarily in the question of reconstituting \(X\) from \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\): as the latter only depends of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) up to isomorphism, the question can only have an affirmative answer if \(X\) is sober. We will specify below (7.1) how \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) can actually be reconstituted in terms of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), by interpreting its points as "points" of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (or as functors fibers).

On any topological space, it is necessary to introduce the order relation \(\le\) for which we have \[ x \le y \iff \overline{\{x\}} \subset \overline{\{y\}} \quad \text{i.e.} \quad x \in \overline{\{y\}} \] (which we further express by saying that \(x\) is a specialization of \(y\), or that \(y\) is a generization of \(x\)). For a space of the form \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), this is none other than the inclusion relation between closed parts irreducible values of \(X\).

This established, it is necessary to introduce, on all the applications of a space \(X\) in a other \(Y\), the so-called "specialization" order relation, deduced from that of \(Y\), namely \[ f \le g \iff f(x) \le g(x) \quad \text{pour tout } x \in X . \] With these conventions, we have the following result:

4.2.3

Let \(X\), \(Y\) be two topological spaces, with \(Y\) sober, and let \(f\) and \(g\) two continuous applications of \(X\) into \(Y\). Then there is at most one morphism of \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\) in \(\mathrm{Top}(g)\), and for there to be one, it is necessary and sufficient that \(g\) be a specialization of \(f\). Finally, any morphism of topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y)\) is isomorphic to a morphism of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\), where \(f : X \to Y\) is a continuous application (only determined using the first assertion).

If we define the category \(\mathrm{cat}(I)\) associated with an ordered set \(I\) by declaring that for \(i \ge j\), there is exactly one arrow of \(i\) in \(j\), we can summarize the previous result by saying that we have an equivalence of canonical categories \[ \mathrm{cat}(\mathrm{Hom}_{(\mathrm{esp})}(X,Y)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathrm{Top}(X),\mathrm{Top}(Y)) \] (where the second member is defined in 3.2).
We formally conclude from these results:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2.4
  1. a)

    Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous application. So \(\mathrm{Top}(f) : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y)\) is an equivalence of topos if and only if \(f_{\mathrm{sob}} : X_{\mathrm{sob}} \to Y_{\mathrm{sob}}\) is a homeomorphism (so, when \(X\) and \(Y\) are sober, if and only if \(f\) is a homeomorphism).

  2. b)

    Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. So that \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) are equivalent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) and \(Y_{\mathrm{sob}}\) are homeomorphic (therefore, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are sober, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) and \(Y\) be homeomorphic).
4.3. Morphisms in the final topos: constant objects of a topos, section functors

Let us designate by \(P\) (initial of "point") the typical final topos, i.e. \(P = (\mathrm{Ens})\) (2.2). Let \(E\) be a any topos, we will see that up to a unique isomorphism, there exists a unique morphism of topos \[ (4.3.1) \qquad f : E \to P \] more precisely, that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,P)\) is equivalent to the punctual category: for two objects in this category, there is therefore a unique arrow from one to the other, and it is an isomorphism. (This justifies to a certain extent the name "final topos"). For this, let us recall (3.2.1) that \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,P)\) is equivalent to the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(P,E)^\circ\) formed by the functors \[ f^* : P = (\mathrm{Ens}) \to E \] which commute to \(\varinjlim\) and are left-exact. Let \(e\) be a point set, then all set \(X\) is written canonically as "sum of \(X\) copies of \(e\)", from which it follows that the category of functors \(g : (\mathrm{Ens}) \to E\) which commute to \(\varinjlim\) is equivalent to the category \(E\), associating with all \(g\) the object \(g(e)\) from \(E\). We reconstitute \(g\) in terms of \(T = g(e)\), up to a unique isomorphism, by \(g(I) = T \times I\), where we set \(T \times I = \coprod_{i \in I} T_i\), with \(T_i = T\) for all \(i \in I\). That the functor in \(I\) thus defined by \(T\) commutes well to \(\varinjlim\) results from the fact that it is manifestly adjoint to the right of the functor \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) from \(E\) in \((\mathrm{Ens})\). That said, for \(g\) to be exact on the left, it is obviously necessary that \(g(e) = T\) be a final object of \(E\) (since \(e\) is a final object of \((\mathrm{Ens})\)), and it follows easily due to the fact that in \(E\) "the sums are universal" (1.1.2 b)) that this condition is also sufficient. We therefore find that the category of functors \(f^*\) inverse images is equivalent to the category of final objects of \(E\), which is obviously itself equivalent to the final category.

From the above, we see that the choice of a morphism (4.3.1) is essentially equivalent to that of a final object of \(E\), i.e. \(e_E\). In terms of this, we then have canonical isomorphisms of functors \[ (4.3.2) \qquad f^*(I) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } e_E \times I = \text{somme de } I \text{ copies de } e_E \qquad \text{pour } I \in \operatorname{ob}(\mathrm{Ens}), \] and \[ (4.3.3) \qquad f_*(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(e_E,X) \qquad \text{pour } X \in \operatorname{Ob} E . \]

The two previous functors will subsequently play an important role. For a set \(I\), we call constant object of value \(I\) in \(E\) (or, when \(E\) is realized as a category \(\widetilde{C}\) in terms of a site \(C\), constant beam of value \(I\) on \(C\)), the object \(f^*(I) = e_E \times I\) of (4.3.2). We will also often note \(I_E\), where \(I_C\) when \(E\) is defined by the site \(C\). The fact that \(I \mapsto I_E\) is the inverse image functor of a topos morphism specifies its accuracy properties, which imply in particular that this functor respects all the usual species of algebraic structure, transforming a group into a group object of \(E\) etc (3.1.2). When we have for example a Group \(G\) of \(E\), we will say that it is a Constant group (or, where appropriate, a constant group bundle) if it is isomorphic to a group of the form \(G_{oE}\), where \(G_o\) is an ordinary group. Same terminology for any other species of "algebraic" structure, in the sense specified (more or less) in 3.1.2.

We will pay attention that the functor \(I \mapsto I_E\) is not necessarily fully faithful (nor even faithful: take for \(E\) the "empty topos" (2.2)), therefore a constant object of \(E\) does not determine in general with unique isomorphism near the set \(I\) which gives rise to it. We say that \(E\) is 0-acyclic, or connected-non-empty, if the functor \(I \mapsto I_E\) is fully faithful. It comes to the same thing, according to the general properties of adjoint functors, to say that the addition morphism \[ I \to f_*(f^*(I)) = f_*(I_E) = \mathrm{Hom}(e_E, I_E) \] is an isomorphism (so that the functor (4.3.3) allows us to recover the "value" of a constant object from \(E\)). We easily verify that it is necessary and it is sufficient for this that \(e_E\) is not the initial object \(\varnothing_E\) of \(E\), i.e. that \(E\) is not an "empty topos" (which expresses the fidelity of the functor \(I \mapsto I_E\)(∗)), and that \(e_E\) is a connected object of \(E\), i.e. is not the sum of two objects of \(E\) which are not "empty" (i.e. which are not initial objects of \(E\)).

The functor (4.3.3) is also often called sections functor and denoted \(\Gamma_E\) or \(\Gamma(E,-)\) or simply \(\Gamma\): \[ (4.3.6.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(e_E,X) = \Gamma_E(X) = \Gamma(E,X) = \Gamma(X) . \] It is a functor commuting to arbitrary projective limits, but not right-exact in general, of which derived functors (on abelian group objects) will be studied in the next presentation.
4.4. Morphisms of “empty topos”

Let \(\varnothing_{\mathrm{top}}\) be an empty topos, which therefore corresponds to a category of beams \(\widetilde{\varnothing}\) equivalent to the final category (2.2). Let \(E\) be a topos. The category of functors of \(E\) in \(\widetilde{\varnothing}\) is obviously equivalent to the point category, and any such functor commutes to inductive limits and projectives (without any merit elsewhere), so can be considered as an inverse image functor \(f\) for a topos morphism \(\varnothing_{\mathrm{top}} \to E\). This results in the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\varnothing_{\mathrm{top}},E)\) being equivalent to the point category, and in particular that there exists with unique isomorphism up to one and one only morphism of topos \[ (4.4.1) \qquad \varnothing_{\mathrm{top}} \to E . \] This justifies to some extent the "initial topos" terminology introduced in 2.2.

We can also determine the morphisms of topos \[ (4.4.2) \qquad E \to \varnothing_{\mathrm{top}} ; \] we immediately verify that there exists such a morphism if and only if the initial object of \(E\) is also an object final, i.e. if and only if \(E\) itself is an "empty topos", and in this case the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,\varnothing_{\mathrm{top}})\) is still equivalent to the point category. The unique morphism (4.4.2) (modulo isomorphy) is then an equivalence of topos.
4.5. The topos classifying \(B_G\) for G Variable group

Let \(E\) be a topos, and \[ f : G \to H \] a morphism of Groups in \(E\). We deduce a functor “restriction of the Group of operators” \[ f^* : B_H \to B_G , \] where the notations are those of 2.4. It is trivial that this functor commutes to inductive limits and to projective limits, a fortiori it can be interpreted as an inverse image functor associated with a topos morphism \[ B_f \ \text{ou} \ f : B_G \to B_H . \] We easily explain the corresponding direct image functor \[ f_* : B_G \to B_H \] by the formula \[ f_*(X) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_G(H_s,X) , \] where \(X\) is an object of \(X\) with \(G\) operating on the left, where \(H_s\) is \(H\) considered as having the operations to left by \(G\) deduced from \(F\), and where \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_G\) designates the sub-object that we guess from the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\) defined below (10. ); we operate \(H\) on the left on \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_G(H_s,X)\) thanks to the operations rights of \(H\) on \(H_s\) by translations to the right.

As the inverse image functor \(f^*\) commutes to any \(\varinjlim\) (and not only to \(\varinjlim\) finite), it is itself the left adjoint of a functor \[ f_! : B_G \to B_H , \] so that we have a sequence of three adjoint functors as in 3.1.3: \[ f_! , \qquad f^* , \qquad f_* . \] We easily explain \(f_!\) by the formula \[ f_!(X) = H \times^G X , \] where the second member designates the "contracted product", deduced from the operations of \(G\) on \(X\) (on the left) and on \(H\) (right via right translations and \(f\)), defined as the quotient of \(H \times X\) by \(G\) operating by the formula \[ g.(h,x) = (hg^{-1},gx) . \] The functor \(f_!\), being a left adjoint, obviously commutes to inductive limits, but it is not in general exact on the left (i.e. it cannot be considered in turn as an inverse image functor by a topos morphism \(B_H \to B_G\)). In fact, we easily verify that it can only be left exact if \(f : G \to H\) is an isomorphism. Likewise, the functor \(f_*\), which commutes to projective limits, is not not generally accurate to the right, and a fortiori does not generally admit an adjunct to the right. At least when \(E\) is the point topos i.e. \(G\) and \(H\) are ordinary groups, \(f_*\) is only right-exact if \(f\) is an isomorphism.

When we have a second group morphism \(g : H \to K\), we find as in 4.1.1 a transitivity up to canonical isomorphism, so that, subject to the usual reservation, we can consider that the classifying topos \(B_G\) depends functionally on the Group \(G\). We leave it to the reader to generalize functorial behavior for the case where we simultaneously vary \(G\) and the topos \(E\).
4.5.2. The topos \(B_{\underline{G}}\) for a variable pro-group \(\underline{G}\)

We leave it to the reader to specify the covariant nature of the topos \(B_{\underline{G}}\) (2.7) in relation to to \(\underline{G}\), copying the presentation we give in 4.5.1.
