Please note, however, that in the case of a morphism \(f : \underline{G} \to \underline{H}\) of pro-groups which are not essentially constant, the morphism of corresponding topos \(B_{\underline{G}} \to B_{\underline{H}}\) does not generally allow the definition of a functor \(f_!\) (of which the adjoint on the right is the restriction functor \(f^*\) of the pro-group of operators). Supposing, for simplify the statement, that \(\underline{G}\) and \(\underline{H}\) are defined by profinite groups \(G\) and \(H\), we easily see that \(f_!\) exists if and only if the image of the considered morphism \(f : G \to H\) has finite index in \(H\), and in this case \(f_!\) is given by the same formula as in 4.5.
4.6. The topos \(\widehat{C}\) for C category variable

Either \[ f : C \to C' \] a functor from one category \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) into another \(C'\), hence a functor \[ f^* : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C} , \qquad f^*(F) = F \circ f . \] It is trivial that this functor commutes to projective limits and inductive limits, a fortiori it can be considered as the inverse image functor for a topos morphism \[ \widehat{f} \ \text{ou} \ f : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}' . \] The corresponding direct image functor \[ f_* : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}' \] is none other than the functor also noted \(f_*\) in I 5.1. Moreover (as was to be expected from the fact that \(f^*\) also commutes to any \(\varprojlim\)) \(f_*\) also admits an adjoint on the left \[ f_! : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}' \] (which was also noted \(f_!\) in I 5.1). We therefore obtain a sequence of three adjoint functors \[ f_! , \qquad f^* , \qquad f_* , \] the first being an extension of \(f : C \to C'\) to the categories of pre-bundles (for the usual embedding of \(C\), \(C'\) into categories \(\widehat{C}\), \(\widehat{C}'\)). We will pay attention that the functor \(f_!\) is not generally exact on the left, nor \(f_*\) exact on the right, which therefore removes any ambiguity on the direction of the variance of the topos \(\widehat{C}\) associated with the variable category \(C\): this topos is a functor covariant in \(C\), subject to the usual reservation coming from transitivity isomorphisms (cf. 4.1.1). When the set of objects of \(C\) and \(C'\) is reduced to one element, such that \(C\) and \(C'\) identify with monoids \(G\), \(G'\), the corresponding topos are the classifying topos \(B_{G^\circ}\) and \(B_{G'^\circ}\), and we finds the variance of these for \(G\) variable already encountered from other points of view in 4.1.2 and 4.5 (where the restriction to groups instead of monoids was not essential).

We can specify the 2-functional dependence of the topos \(\widehat{C}\) with respect to \(C\), by introducing for two categories \(C\), \(C' \in \mathcal{U}\) a canonical functor \[ (4.6.2.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\widehat{C},\widehat{C}'). \] It remains to define this functor on the arrows, and for this we note that \(f \mapsto f^*\) allows us to identify (at equivalence of categories) the second member has a subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\widehat{C}',\widehat{C})^\circ\) (3.2.1). Now if \(f,g : C \to C'\) are two functors, any morphism \(u : f \to g\) of functors defines a morphism \(F \circ g \to F \circ f\) of functors into \(F \in \operatorname{ob} \widehat{C}'\) (\(F\) being a contrafunctor), which is therefore a morphism \(g^* \to f^*\) and subsequently defines a morphism \(\widehat{f} \to \widehat{g}\) as announced.

When \(C\) is the point category, \(\widehat{C}\) is the point topos (2.2) denoted \(P\), and (4.6.2.1) is interpreted as a natural functor \[ (4.6.2.2) \qquad C' \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(P,\widehat{C}') \overset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \underline{\mathrm{Points}}(\widehat{C}') \] of \(C'\) in the "category of points" of \(\widehat{C}'\), which will be studied in 6. This functor is not necessarily an equivalence of categories (7.3.3), a fortiori (4.6.2.1) is not necessarily an equivalence of categories.

On the other hand, the functor (4.6.2.1) is always fully faithful. To see this, note that if \(f,g : E \to E'\) are two topos morphisms such that \(f_!\) and \(g_!\) are defined (3.1.3), it results from the theory of adjoint functors that we have canonical isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{Hom}(f_!,g_!) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(g^*,f^*) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(f_*,g_*) \overset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \mathrm{Hom}(f,g) . \] Applying this to functors of the form \(\widehat{f}\), \(\widehat{g}\) associated with \(f,g : C \to C'\), we find the result announced, taking into account that the natural application of extension \(\mathrm{Hom}(f,g) \to \mathrm{Hom}(f_!,g_!)\) is bijective (I 7.8).

We can ask ourselves when the functor (4.6.2.1) is an equivalence of categories, i.e. when it is essentially surjective, which is a special case of the question of determining all topos morphisms \(\widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}'\). More generally, if \(C\) is a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\) and \(E\) a topos, we can propose to determine the morphisms of topos \[ f : \widehat{C} \to E . \] The category of these morphisms is equivalent to the category opposite that of functors \(f^* : E \to \widehat{C}\) commuting to arbitrary inductive limits and finite projective limits. Interpreter functors \(E \to \widehat{C}\) like functors \(E \times C^\circ \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) = (\mathrm{Ens})\), or even like functors functors \(F : C^\circ \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E, (\mathrm{Ens}))\), the considered accuracy property is expressed by the condition that for any object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor \(F(X) : E \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) commutes to arbitrary inductive limits and to finite projective limits, (or, as we will say in 6, \(F(X)\) is a "fiber functor" on \(E\)). He comes back the same as saying that \(F(X)\) is the inverse image functor for a topos morphism \(P \to E\), so that we finally find an equivalence of canonical categories \[ (4.6.3.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\widehat{C},E) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C, \underline{\mathrm{Points}}(E)) , \] where we designate to abbreviate by \[ \underline{\mathrm{Points}}(E) = \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(P,E) \] the category of topos points \(E\).

When \(E\) is of the form \(\widehat{C}'\), we immediately see from the definitions that the compound of (4.6.2.1) and the previous equivalence (4.6.3.1) is the functor \[ (4.6.3.2) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,\underline{\mathrm{Points}}(\widehat{C}')) \] defined by \(F \mapsto i \circ F\), where \(i : C' \to \underline{\mathrm{Points}}(\widehat{C}')\) is the canonical embedding (4.6.2.2). It follows as soon as for (4.6.2.1) to be an equivalence, it is necessary and sufficient that \(C\) be empty or that (4.6.2.2) is essentially surjective(hence an equivalence of categories). This last condition on \(C'\) is satisfied in some interesting cases, and notably when \(C'\) is the defined single-object category by a group \(G\) (7.2.5).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.6.4

The fact of having associated a topos \(\widehat{C}\) with an arbitrary category \(C\) suggests that a category \(C\) admits invariants of a topological nature (cohomology groups, homotopy groups, etc.), just like a topos. Cohomology groups of \(\widehat{C}\) with coefficients in an abelian group object \(F\) (in the general sense studied in V) are none other than the values of the functors derived \(\varprojlim^{(n)}\) from functor \(\varprojlim\), already familiar to topologists. J.L. Verdier and (independently) D.G. Quillen have verified that when we limit ourselves to constant coefficients, or more generally locally constant, these cohomology groups identify with the cohomology groups of the semi-simplicial set \(\mathrm{Nerf}(C)\) canonically associated with \(C\) [5, n° 212, prop. 4.1] and what's more, up to isomorphy in the "category homotopic" of [2], any semi-simplical set can be obtained using a category \(C\). By means of a suitable notion of homotopy type for topos, which we do not specify here, we can say that the semi-simplicial homotopy types of topologists are none other than the homotopy types of topos of the special form \(\widehat{C}\), more generally topos \(E\) where any object admits an overlap which refines all others (2.6)(∗). In the absence of this condition on \(E\), we can at best express our type of homotopy by a suitable projective system of semi-simplicial sets [1].
4.7. The topos \(\widetilde{C}\) for a variable C site (cocontinuous functors)

Either \[ f : C \to C' \] a cocontinuous functor (III 2.1) between sites \(\in \mathcal{U}\), i.e. a functor such as the functor \(\widehat{f}_*\) or \(f_* : F \mapsto F \circ f\) of \(\widehat{C}\) in \(\widehat{C}'\) (4.6.1) applies \(\widetilde{C}\) in \(\widetilde{C}'\), i.e. induces a functor \[ (4.7.1) \qquad \widetilde{f}_* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}' \] making the functor diagram commutative \[ (4.7.2) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]^-{\widetilde{f}_*} \ar[d]_-{i} & \widetilde{C}' \ar[d]^-{i'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]_-{\widehat{f}_*} & \widehat{C}' } \] where \(i\), \(i'\) are the inclusion functors. We then saw (III 2.3) that the functor \(\widetilde{f}_*\) admits an adjoint to left \(\widetilde{f}^*\), and that the latter is exact on the left. In other words, \(\widetilde{f}_*\) is the direct image functor associated with a topos morphism \[ \widetilde{f} \ \text{ou} \ f : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}' , \] the corresponding inverse image functor being of course \(\widetilde{f}^*\). Taking the deputies to the left of functors in play, the commutative diagram (4.7.2) also gives a commutative diagram with near isomorphism \[ (4.7.3) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} & \widetilde{C}' \ar[l]_-{\widetilde{f}^*} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[u]^-{a} & \widehat{C}' \ar[l]^-{\widehat{f}^*} \ar[u]_-{a'} } \] where \(a\) and \(a'\) are the "associated bundles" functors, diagram which immediately gives the formula (III 2.3) \[ \widetilde{f}^* = a \, \widehat{f}^* i' . \]

The transitivity property for topos morphisms \(\widehat{f} : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}'\) implies the same property for topos morphisms \(\widetilde{f} : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) associated with cocontinuous functors, so that we can say that the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) varies functorially in \(C\) in a covariant way, when we take as "morphisms" of sites cocontinuous functors.

In all cases encountered so far, the cocontinuous functor \(f\) used is also continuous, that is to say (III 1.1) "extends" into a functor \[ \widetilde{f}_! : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}' \] commuting at inductive limits, and which is adjoint to the left of \(\widetilde{f}^*\), so that we have a sequence of three adjoint functors \[ \widetilde{f}_! , \qquad \widetilde{f}^* , \qquad \widetilde{f}_* . \] Please note that the functor \(\widetilde{f}_!\) is not generally exact on the left, nor \(\widetilde{f}_*\) exact on the right, which removes any ambiguity on the direction of the variance of the topos \(\widetilde{C}\), for \(C\) variable by functors whose we only assume that they are cocontinuous, or even continuous and cocontinuous.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.7.4

Given a topos morphism \[ F : E \to E' , \] for there to exist an adjoint functor \(F_!\) to the left of \(F^*\), it is necessary and sufficient that we can "realize" (at equivalence) \(E\) and \(E'\) in the form \(\widetilde{C}\) and \(\widetilde{C}'\), for two sites \(C, C' \in \mathcal{U}\), and that we can find a continuous and cocontinuous functor \(f : C \to C'\) such that \(F\) identifies with \(\widetilde{f}\). It is obviously sufficient, and for necessity, it is enough to take for \(C\) and \(C'\) small full subcategories generators of \(E\) and \(E'\) respectively, such that \[ F_!(\operatorname{ob} C) \subset \operatorname{ob} C' , \] provided with the topologies induced by those of \(E\) and \(E'\), and to take for \(f\) the functor induced by \(F_!\) (see 1.2.1). Recall (1.8) that the existence of \(F_!\) also means that \(F^*\) commutes at projective limits, or, which amounts here to the same thing since this functor is exact on the left, it commutes to the products.

If we ask ourselves what are the morphisms of topos \(F : E \to E'\) which can be realized by a cocontinuous (not necessarily continuous) functor of sites, we also see that the answer is following: the set of objects \(X\) of \(E\) such as the functor \(X' \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X,F^*(X'))\) of \(E'\) in \((\mathrm{Ens})\) commutes to projective limits (or, which amounts to the same thing, to products) must be a family generator of \(E\).
4.8. The topos morphism \(\widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C}\) for a C site.

Let \(C\) be a small site, with which the two topos \(\widetilde{C}\) and \(\widehat{C}\) are therefore associated (the second does not not dependent on the topology set to \(C\)). We defined in II 3.4 the “associated beam” functor \[ a : \widehat{C} \to \widetilde{C} , \] and established that it is left-exact and commutes at inductive limits. It is therefore the inverse image functor associated with a topos morphism \[ p : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C} , \qquad p^* = a , \] the corresponding direct image functor being the canonical inclusion \[ i = p_* : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C} . \] It is well known that this last functor is not in general right-exact (its functors derived on the abelian objects give rise to the pre-sheaves of cohomology \(\mathscr{H}^n(F)\) of V 2), and that \(a\) does not does not generally commute to any \(\varprojlim\), which removes any ambiguity about the direction of the "natural" morphism of topos between \(\widehat{C}\) and \(\widetilde{C}\).

When we have a cocontinuous functor \[ f : C \to C' \] of sites, we deduce a diagram of topos morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]^-{\widetilde{f}} \ar[d]_-{p} & \widetilde{C}' \ar[d]^-{p'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]_-{\widehat{f}} & \widehat{C}' } \] which is commutative up to canonical isomorphism: this is in fact what the commutativity of functor diagram (4.7.2). We can therefore say that the morphism of topos \(p : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C}\) is functorial in \(C\), when we vary \(C\) by cocontinuous functors between sites.
4.9. Effect of a continuous site functor. Site morphisms

If \[ f : C \to C' \] is a continuous functor of \(C\) in \(C'\), i.e. such that the functor \(\widehat{f}^* : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C}\) applies \(\widetilde{C}'\) in \(\widetilde{C}\), therefore induces a functor \[ f_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C} , \] we saw (III 1.2) that the latter admits an adjoint on the left \[ f^s : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}' , \] which moreover "extends" \(f\) in an obvious sense. Please note that in general \(f^s\) is not exact right (even if \(f\) is moreover cocontinuous), nor \(f_s\) commutes at the inductive limits, so that the data from \(f\) does not allow, without other hypothesis, to describe a topos morphism in one sense or in the other between \(\widetilde{C}\) and \(\widetilde{C}'\). The case where \(f\) is cocontinuous, i.e. where \(f_s\) commutes at the inductive limits and can therefore be regarded as an inverse image functor for a topos morphism \(\widetilde{f} : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\), a was discussed in 4.7. We will examine the case where the functor \(f^s\) is left exact, so maybe considered as an inverse image functor for a topos morphism in reverse: \[ (4.9.1.1) \qquad \mathrm{Top}(f) = g : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C} . \] We will pay attention that we have taken care not to note this morphism by the letter \(\widetilde{f}\) or \(f\), to avoid confusions with the situation of 4.7, following the general recommendations of 3.1.3. They say sometimes that the functor \(f : C \to C'\) is a morphism of sites of \(C'\) in \(C\) (note, no \(C\) in \(C'\)) if it is continuous and if the functor \(f^s\) is left exact, in other words if there exists a morphism of topos (4.9.1.1) such that the corresponding inverse image functor \[ g^* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}' \] extends the functor \(f\), i.e. makes the functor diagram commutative up to isomorphism \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon} & C' \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon'} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{g^*} & \widetilde{C}' } \] where \(\varepsilon\), \(\varepsilon'\) are the canonical functors of II 4.4.0.

Practically, we recognize that a continuous functor \(f : C \to C'\) is a morphism of sites of \(C'\) in \(C\), by the fact that in \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable, and that \(f\) commutes there (III 1.3 5)). Under the condition indicated on \(C\) (almost always verified in practice), and assuming moreover that the topology of \(C'\) is less fine than the canonical topology (almost always verified also), the previous sufficient condition (\(f\) exact on the left) so that \(f\) is a morphism of sites of \(C'\) in \(C\) is also necessary.

In the spirit of the above, if \(C\) and \(C'\) are two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites, it is appropriate to define the category of site morphisms \(\underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(C',C)\) of \(C\) in \(C'\) as the full subcategory of the opposite category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C')^\circ\) of the category of functors of \(C\) in \(C'\), formed of the functors which want to be morphisms of sites (from \(C'\) into \(C\)). In this way, we obtain a functor canonical (defined up to unique isomorphism) \[ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(C',C) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\widetilde{C}',\widetilde{C}) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.9.4

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, then the functor \(f \mapsto f^*|C = f^* \circ \varepsilon_C\), associating with any topos morphism \(f : E \to \widetilde{C}\) the "restriction" to \(C\) of associated inverse image functor \(f^* : \widetilde{C} \to E\), induces an equivalence of categories \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,\widetilde{C}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(E,C) \ (\hookrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,E)^\circ) . \] When in \(C\) the finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable, the corresponding fully faithful functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,\widetilde{C}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,E)^\circ \] has as essential image the set of functors \(g : C \to E\) which are left-exact and continuous, or again which are exact on the left and transform covering family into covering family.

The last assertion follows from the first thanks to 4.9.2. On the other hand, we deduce from III 1.2 iv) and from IV 1.2 iii) that the functor \(G \to G \circ \varepsilon\) induces an equivalence of the category of functors continuous of \(\widetilde{C}\) in \(E\), and the category of continuous functors of \(C\) in \(E\), a quasi-inverse functor being obtained by \(g \to g^s\) in loc notations. cit. On the other hand, by very definition, \(g\) is a morphism of sites if and only if \(g^s\) is the inverse image functor associated with a morphism of topos \(E \to \widetilde{C}\), hence the conclusion thanks to 3.2.1, the "or again" coming from III 1.6.

We will especially remember from 4.9.4 that (when in \(C\) the finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable) "it comes to the same thing" to give ourselves a topos morphism \(f : E \to \widetilde{C}\), or a functor \(g : C \to E\) which is exact left and transforms covering families into covering families.

Using developments 4.9.1 and 4.9.3, we see as usual that for a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\) variable, via the notion of morphism of sites and morphism of morphisms of sites that we come from to explain, the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) depends functionally (or more precisely, 2-functionally) of the site \(C\). We note that thanks to the terminology introduced, \(\widetilde{C}\) depends covariantly on the site \(C\).

It is immediate that (contrary to what happens for the notion of cocontinuous functor, cf. 4.7.4) any topos morphism \(F : E \to E'\) can be achieved using a site morphism \(f : C \to C'\) (i.e. a continuous functor \(f : C' \to C\) such that...): simply choose from \(E\) and \(E'\) small full generating subcategories \(C\) and \(C'\) respectively, provided with the topologies induced by those of \(E\) and \(E'\), such that we have \[ F^*(\operatorname{ob} C') \subset \operatorname{ob} C , \] and to take for \(f\) the functor induced by \(F^*\). This explains why most of the morphisms of topos that we encounter in practice are effectively described using site morphisms (rather than using cocontinuous functors as in 4.7).
<h3 id="IV.4.10">4.10. Relations between the small and the large topos associated with a topological space

We use the ratings of 2.5. In particular, \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\). We let's depart from the convention of 2.1, by designating by \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) the topos of \(\mathcal{V}\)-beams (and not of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams) on \(X\). Thus, we will reason with \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos and not \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. (NB it would be possible to keep the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, by adopting the appropriate convention for the definition \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\), of so that it is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, cf. 2.5). This established, we will define TWO topos morphisms \[ (4.10.1) \qquad \begin{cases} f : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{TOP}(X) \\ g : \mathrm{TOP}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X) , \quad gf \simeq \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Top}(X)} , \end{cases} \] giving rise to a sequence of three adjoint functors \[ (4.10.1.1) \qquad g^* = f_! , \qquad g_* = f^* , \qquad g^! = f_* , \] the notations being those of 3.1.3. We will successively define the first two functors of this continuation, the third then being defined as an adjunct to the right of the second.

The functor \[ g_* = f^* : \mathrm{TOP}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X) \] is defined simply as the restriction functor of \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\) at the site \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) of the open ones of \(X\), which transforms bundles into bundles, as follows immediately from the definition. Designating with underlined letters of the beams on the big site of \(X\), we designate, for such a beam \(\underline{F}\), by \(\underline{F}_X\) its restriction to the site \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\), hence a functor \[ (4.10.2.1) \qquad \mathrm{Restr} : \underline{F} \mapsto \underline{F}_X : \mathrm{TOP}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X) . \] It is obvious that this functor commutes to \(\mathcal{V}\)-projective limits, since these are calculated argument by argument (one could also invoke the existence of the left adjoint, constructed in 4.10.4 below.) I say it also commutes at \(\mathcal{V}\)-inductive limits. To be convinced of this, we will give a very convenient interpretation of the "large" beams on \(X\), i.e. objects of \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\), in terms of ordinary sheaves (NB these are \(\mathcal{V}\)-sheaves) on the topological spaces \(X'\) above \(X\).

For a large beam \(\underline{F}\) on \(X\), and for any space \(X'\) on \(X\) (implied: \(X' \in \mathcal{U}\)), we define in an obvious way, as in 4.10.2, the "small" beam \(\underline{F}_{X'}\), restriction of \(\underline{F}\) to \(X'\). If \[ u : X'' \to X' \] is a morphism of \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\), we then clearly define a morphism \(u_*(\underline{F}_{X''}) \to \underline{F}_{X'}\), or what amounts to the same thing, a "transition morphism" \[ (4.10.3.1) \qquad \varphi_u : u^*(\underline{F}_{X'}) \to \underline{F}_{X''} . \] These morphisms, for \(u\) variable, satisfy an obvious transitivity condition for a compound \[ X''' \xrightarrow{\ u\ } X'' \xrightarrow{\ v\ } X' \] of morphisms in \((\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\), which we leave to the reader to explain. In this way we obtain a natural functor, which goes from the category \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) of large beams to \(X\), in the category of systems \[ (\underline{F}_{X'}) \ (X' \in \operatorname{ob} (\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}) , \qquad (\varphi_u) \ (u \in \mathrm{Fl}\,(\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}) , \] satisfying the transitivity condition envisaged, and such more than for any morphism \(u : X'' \to X'\) which is an open immersion (or, more generally, a spreading), the transition morphism \(\varphi_u\) or an isomorphism. Like the \(u^* : \mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X'')\) functors used for the description of \(\varphi_u\) commute to \(\mathcal{V}\)-inductive limits, it immediately follows that in the previous description of the objects of \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) in terms of "small" beams \(\underline{F}_{X'}\), the \(\mathcal{V}\)-inductive limits are calculated argument by argument, i.e. functors of the form \(\underline{F} \mapsto \underline{F}_{X'}\), commute to \(\mathcal{V}\)-inductive limits.

In particular, this is the case with the functor \(\underline{F} \mapsto \underline{F}_X\) considered in 4.10.2. He therefore admits an assistant on the right (1.5). It remains to construct an adjunct on the left (whose existence also results from a priori of 1.8), and to check that the latter is exact on the left. This will complete the definition of morphisms of announced topos (4.10.1) in terms of functors (4.10.1.1).

The functor \[ g^* = f_! : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{TOP}(X) \] is obtained by associating with any small beam \(F\) on \(X\) the system of its inverse images \((F_{X'})\), \(X' \in \operatorname{ob} (\mathrm{Esp})_{/X}\), by the structural morphisms \(X' \to X\), the transition morphism \(\varphi_u\) for a \(u : X'' \to X'\) being the transitivity isomorphism for the inverse images of beams (4.1.1). We see as soon as we thus obtain a functor \[ \mathrm{Prol} : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{TOP}(X) , \] fully faithful, the essential image of which is formed by the large beams \(\underline{F}\) on \(X\) for which all the Transitivity morphisms \(\varphi_u\) of 4.10.3 are isomorphisms. We leave it to the reader to define an addition isomorphism between this canonical extension functor and the functor restriction of 4.10.2, proving that the latter is added to the right of the first. It is immediate in terms of description 4.10.3 of category \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 4.10.5
  1. a)

    Build 4.10.4 at the same time shows that \(g^* = f_!\) is fully faithful (or, which amounts to the same thing, that \(g_* = f^*\) is a functor for passing to a category of fractions, or finally that \(g^! = f_*\) is fully faithful). The big beams on \(X\) which belong to the image essential of the functor \(g^* = f_! = \mathrm{Prol}\) deserve the name of large spread sheaves on \(X\), since they form a category equivalent to that of ordinary sheaves on \(X\), or even to that of spaces spread over \(X\). We also easily see that if \(X'\) is a topological space above \(X\), then the large beam on \(X\) that it represents is slack in the previous sense if and only if \(X'\) is a spread space on \(X\).

  2. b)

    We can still express the full fidelity of \(f_*\) by writing that the morphism of addition \(f^* f_* \to \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Top}(X)}\) is an isomorphism, i.e. that \(g_* f_* \simeq \mathrm{id}\), i.e. that \(gf \simeq \mathrm{id}\). Thus \(g\) makes \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) a topos on \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), admitting a "section" \(f\) on \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\).

  3. c)

    The fact that the functor \(g^* = f_!\) is fully faithful, exact and that it commutes to \(\mathcal{V}\)-inductive limits partially justifies the very convenient point of view (due to J. GIRAUD) following which it is harmless, in practically all questions of beam theory on \(X\), of replace the usual beams or "small" beams with the associated "large" beams. It is in particular thus of cohomological questions, since \(g_*\) being exact, the functors \(R^i g_*\) (V ) are null for \(i > 0\), so (V) that for any large bundle \(\underline{F}\) on \(X\) we have canonical isomorphisms \[ H^i(\mathrm{TOP}(X),\underline{F}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^i(\mathrm{Top}(X),\underline{F}_X) = H^i(X,\underline{F}_X) . \] Applying this to a beam of the form \(\mathrm{Prol}(F)\), where \(F\) is a small beam on \(X\), we conclude (since \(\mathrm{Prol}(F)_X \simeq F\) canonically) a canonical isomorphism \[ H^i(X,F) = H^i(\mathrm{TOP}(X), \mathrm{Prol}(F)) . \] Thus, the cohomological invariants of \(X\), calculated via the small or large topos of \(X\), are essentially identical. The same result is also valid in non-commutative cohomology.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.10.6

Let \(S\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site whose topology is less fine than the canonical topology, \(\underline{M}\) a part of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, S\) satisfying the following conditions:

  1. a)

    The morphisms of \(\underline{M}\) are quatable (I 10.3), and \(\underline{M}\) is stable by change of base.

  2. b)

    \(\underline{M}\) contains the identical arrows and is compositionally stable.

  3. c)

    An arrow \(u : X \to Y\) such that there exists a covering family \(Y_i \to Y\), with the \(X \times_Y Y_i \to Y_i\) in \(\underline{M}\), is itself an element of \(\underline{M}\).

  4. d)

    For all \(X \in \operatorname{ob} S\), any covering family of \(X\) is refined by a covering family \((f_i : X_i \to X)\), with the \(f_i \in \underline{M}\).

For all \(X \in \operatorname{ob} S\), consider the site \(S(X)\) ("small site of \(X\)") whose category underlying is the full subcategory of \(S_{/X}\) formed of objects whose structural morphism is in \(\underline{M}\), provided with the topology induced (III 3.1) by that of \(S\).

  1. 1°)

    For any arrow \(u : X \to Y\) of \(S\), show that the change of base by \(u\) from \(S(Y)\) in \(S(X)\) is a continuous functor, hence a functor commuting at inductive limits (III) \[ S(u)^* : S(Y)^{\sim} \to S(X)^{\sim} . \]

  2. 2°)

    Define an equivalence between the topos \(S^{\sim}\) and the category of systems \[ (F_X) \ (X \in \operatorname{ob} S) , \qquad (\varphi_u) \ (u \in \mathrm{Fl}\, S) \] formed of objects \(F_X \in \operatorname{ob} S(X)^{\sim}\), and for any arrow \(u : X \to Y\) in \(S\), of a morphism \(\varphi_u : S(u)^*(F_Y) \to F_X\), these systems being subject to a transitivity condition for a compound \(v \circ u\) of arrows of \(S\), and on the condition that \(u \in \underline{M}\) implies that \(\varphi_u\) is an isomorphism.

  3. 3°)

    Define “restrictions” and “extension” functors \[ \mathrm{Res}_X : (S_{/X})^{\sim} \to S(X)^{\sim} , \qquad \mathrm{Prol}_X : S(X)^{\sim} \to (S_{/X})^{\sim} \] Show that \(\mathrm{Res}_X\) commutes at small inductive and projective limits and that \(\mathrm{Prol}_X\) isfully faithful, its essential image being formed of the sheaves \(F\) such that \(\varphi_u\) is an isomorphism for any arrow \(u\) of \(S_{/X}\).

  4. 4°)

    Define an addition morphism making \(\mathrm{Res}_X\) the right adjoint of \(\mathrm{Prol}_X\). Conclude that there exists a topos morphism \[ f : S(X)^{\sim} \to (S_{/X})^{\sim} \] making \(S(X)^{\sim}\) a subtopic of \((S_{/X})^{\sim}\) and such that \[ f_! = \mathrm{Prol}_X , \qquad f^* = \mathrm{Res}_X . \] Show that \(\mathrm{Prol}_X\) transforms abelian sheaves into abelian sheaves.

  5. 5°)

    Show that if \(S(X)\) admits fiber products, \(\mathrm{Prol}_X\) is exact. In deduce that there then exists a topos morphism \(g : (S_{/X})^{\sim} \to S(X)^{\sim}\) which is a retraction to left of \(f\), i.e. such that \[ g^* = \mathrm{Prol}_X , \qquad g_* = \mathrm{Res}_X . \] (For an example where \(S(X)\) does not admit fiber products, take for \(S\) the category of diagrams provided of the flat topology, for \(\underline{M}\) smooth morphisms, for \(X\) a Noetherian diagram of dimension \(> 0\)).

  6. 6°)

    Show that for any abelian sheaf \(F\) of \((S_{/X})^{\sim}\) we have a isomorphism \[ H^q(X,F) \simeq H^q(X,\mathrm{Res}_X F) \qquad \forall q . \] Show, using for example hypercoverings, that for any abelian sheaf \(G\) of \(S(X)^{\sim}\) we have an isomorphism \[ H^q(X,G) \simeq H^q(X,\mathrm{Prol}_X G) \qquad \forall q . \]

  7. 7°)

    Buy a chocolate medal for the editor.

footnotes.html: (∗) or the fact that this functor is conservative (I 6.3).
(∗) and, even more generally, topos which are "locally \(\infty\)-related" in an obvious sense that we leave to the reader to specify.

blocks.html: 5. Induced topos

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\). Then the category \(E_{/X}\) of objects of \(E\) above \(X\) is a topos, as it follows for example immediately from Giraud's criterion 1.2 ii): We can also, thanks to 1.2.1, realize \(E\) as a beam category \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a generating subcategory of \(E\) which can be chosen such that \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\); then we know that \(E_{/X} = \widetilde{C}_{/X}\) is equivalent to \((C_{/X})^{\sim}\) (III 5.4), so it is a topos.
We call the topos \(E_{/X}\) the induced topos on the object \(X\) of \(E\).

We are going to define a canonical topos morphism \[ (5.2.1) \qquad j_X : E_{/X} \to E , \] which is called inclusion morphism of the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) in the ambient topos \(E\), or better (cf. 5.7), localization morphism from \(E\) to \(X\). This morphism corresponds to a sequence of three adjoint functors (see 3.1.3) \[ (5.2.2) \qquad j_{X!} , \qquad j_X^* , \qquad j_{X*} , \] which can be explained as follows:

1. a)

    The functor \[ j_{X!} : E_{/X} \to E \] is the functor "forgetting the structural arrow" of \(E_{/X}\) in \(E\).

  2. b)

    The functor \[ j_X^* : E \to E_{/X} \] is defined by \[ j_X^*(Z) = (X \times Z, \mathrm{pr}_1) , \] where \(\mathrm{pr}_1 : X \times Z \to X\) is the first projection. We can also interpret it as the functor change of baserelative to the morphism \[ X \to e_E , \] where \(e_E\) is the final object of \(E\). It is trivial, by definition of the base change functor, that \(j_X^*\) is well added to the right of \(j_{X!}\). It commutes in particular at projective limits. It also switches to inductive limits, by virtue of the special accuracy properties of topos (II 4).

  3. c)

    From this last fact it follows (1.6) that \(j_X^*\) admits an adjoint on the left \[ j_{X*} : E_{/X} \to E . \] For an object \(X'\) above \(X\), the object \(j_{X*}(X')\) is also sometimes denoted by one of the symbols \(\prod_{X/e_E}(X'/X)\), or \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{X/e_E}(X,X')\), or \(\mathrm{Res}_{X/e_E}(X'/X)\) ("Weil restriction"), one or more of which the other is undoubtedly already familiar to the learned reader of our modest work.

We will pay attention that the functor \(j_{X!}\) commutes to the fibered products, and transforms monomorphisms into monomorphisms, but it is not generally exact on the left. He is not that if \(X\) is a final object of \(E\) (since \(X = j_{X!}(X, \mathrm{id}_X)\), and \((X, \mathrm{id}_X)\) is a final object of \(E_{/X}\)), i.e. if and only if \(j_X\) is in fact an equivalence of topos (so if all functors (5.2.2) are equivalences).(∗)
Similarly, the functor \(j_{X*}\) is not generally right-exact, and does not transform necessarily epimorphisms into epimorphisms.
We conclude from these observations that the direction of the topos morphism connecting \(E_{/X}\) to \(E\), for an object \(X\) of the topos \(E\), is determined without possible ambiguity.

The \(j_X^*\) functor is often called the location functor or restriction functor. This last terminology is justified by identifying the objects of \(E\) resp. on \(E_{/X}\) to beams on \(E\) resp. on \(E_{/X}\) (1.2 iii)), and noting that with this identification, the addition formula between \(j_{X!}\) (functor forgotten) and \(j_X^*\) is interpreted by saying that \(j_X^*(F)\) is the beam restriction to \(E_{/X}\) of the beam \(F\) on \(E\) (taking "restriction" in the generalized sense: composed with the "inclusion" functor \(j_{X!} : E_{/X} \to E\). In accordance with familiar notations in other contexts, we will also often write, indifferently: \[ (5.4.1) \qquad j_X^*(F) = F|X = F_X = \text{restriction de } F \text{ à } X . \]

Similarly, when \(E\) is of the form \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a (small) site, and \(X\) is of the form \(\varepsilon(S)\) with \(S \in \operatorname{ob} C\), so that we have an equivalence of categories recalled in 5.1 \[ E_{/X} \approx \widetilde{C}_{/\varepsilon(S)} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (C_{/S})^{\sim} \] (\(C_{/S}\) being equipped with the topology induced by that of \(C\)), the functor \(j_X^*\) is simply identified with "restriction" functor of a variable beam \(F\) on \(C\) to category \(C_{/S}\).

These reflections make it possible to foresee the important role that the induced topos will play and localization morphisms (5.2.1) in all questions where we are led to reason by "location on \(E\)" (see 8), that is to say practically in all questions involving sites or topos.

Either \[ f : X \to Y \] an arrow of \(E\), which therefore makes it possible to interpret \(X\) (or more correctly, \((X,f)\)) as an object of \(E_{/Y}\). It is obvious that we have a canonical isomorphism \[ (5.5.1) \qquad (E_{/Y})_{/X} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } E_{/X} \] (transitivity of induced topos). Applying the construction of 5.1 to \(E_{/Y}\) instead of \(E\), we therefore find a canonical topos morphism \[ (5.5.2) \qquad \mathrm{loc}(f) \ \text{ou} \ f : E_{/X} \to E_{/Y} , \] also called location morphism (associated with \(f\)). When \(Y\) is the final object of \(E\), we find essentially (5.2.1). The location morphism for \(f\) is associated with a sequence of three functors assistants \[ (5.5.3) \qquad f_! , \qquad f^* , \qquad f_* , \] which can be interpreted respectively as a forgetting functor, as a restriction functor, and as a functor noted either (\(X'\) being the argument) \(\prod_{X/Y}(X'/X)\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{X/Y}(X,X')\) or \(\mathrm{Res}_{X/Y}(X'/X)\).

The transitivity of forgetting functors implies that for two composable morphisms \[ X \xrightarrow{\ f\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Z \] from \(E\), we have a canonical isomorphism of topos morphisms \[ \mathrm{loc}(gf) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{loc}(g)\mathrm{loc}(f) : E_{/X} \to E_{/Y} \to E_{/Z} . \] Moreover, for three composable morphisms, we have the usual compatibility relation for the previous transitivity isomorphisms. This therefore allows us to consider, through the abuse of language usual, that \[ X \mapsto E_{/X} \] is a covariant functor of \(E\) in the category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-top})\) (3.3.1). More precisely, we find a 2-functor (not strict in general) of \(E\) in the 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-top})\).
Before continuing the generalities on induced topos (5.10), let us give some examples informative.

Let \(X\) be a topological space, hence a topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (2.1). Let \(X'\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), which it will be convenient to interpret as a space spread over \(X\), \(p : X' \to X\). The category \(\mathrm{Top}(X)_{/X'}\) is then identified with the category of spaces spread \(X''\) on \(X\), provided with a \(X\)-morphism \(X'' \to X'\). We know that such a morphism makes \(X''\) a space spread over \(X'\), and in this way we find an equivalence of canonical topos \[ \mathrm{Top}(X)_{/X'} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Top}(X') . \] The location morphism is therefore identified with a topos morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\), and we note as soon as the latter is none other than the morphism \[ \mathrm{Top}(p) : \mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X) \] associated with the structural continuous map \(p : X' \to X\). Intuitively, the location morphism is not therefore other than the translation, in topos language, of the spreading morphism \(p : X' \to X\). This explains to both the merits of the terminology "location morphism", and encourages caution in the use of the term "inclusion morphism", this seems especially appropriate in the case where \(p\) is a open immersion. It will therefore be prudent to reserve in 5.1 the term "morphism of inclusion" in the case where the morphism \(X \to e_E\) is a monomorphism, i.e. where \(X\) is identified with a subobject of the final object of \(E\).

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(G\) be a Group of \(E\), \(H\) be a subgroup of \(G\), \[ X = G/H \] the homogeneous quotient space, which we look at as an object of \(E\) with left operator group \(G\), i.e. as an object of the classifying topos \(B_G\) (2.4). We will determine the induced topos \[ (B_G)_{/X} \qquad (X = G/H) , \] by defining an equivalence of topos \[ (5.8.1) \qquad c : B_H \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (B_G)_{/X} , \] such that we have commutativity up to canonical isomorphism in the diagram \[ (5.8.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ B_H \ar[r]^-{c} \ar[dr]_-{B_i} & (B_G)_{/X} \ar[d]^-{j_X} \\ & B_G } \] where \(j_X\) is the location morphism in \(B_G\), and where \[ B_i : B_H \to B_G \] is the morphism deduced from the inclusion \(i : H \to G\) (4.5).

To define (5.8.1), we will simply indicate the description of the inverse image functors \(c^*\) and direct image \(c_*\), leaving it to the reader to verify that these are indeed equivalences quasi-inverses of each other, giving rise to the commutative diagram (5.8.2) of topos morphisms. Either \(e\) the sub-object of \(E\) underlying \(X\), image of the section of \(X\) (on a final object \(e_E\) chosen from \(E\)) deduced from the unit section of \(G\). If \(X'\) is an object of \(B_G\) above \(G\), then we denote by \(c^*(X')\) the image inverse of \(e\) in \(X'\), \[ c^*(X') = X' \times_X e , \] which is stable by the left operation of \(H\) on \(X'\), restriction of the given operation of \(G\) on \(X'\). This defines a functor \[ c^* : (B_G)_{/X} \to B_H . \] On the other hand, if \(X'\) is an object of \(B_H\), then the morphism of \(X'\) into the final object \(e_H\) of \(B_H\) (i.e. the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\) with trivial operation of \(H\)) gives, by application of the functor \(i_!\) (4.5) a morphism of \(B_G\) \[ i_!(X') \to i_!(e_H) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X , \] which allows us to interpret \(i_!(X')\) as an object of \((B_G)_{/X}\), hence the functor sought \[ c_* : X' \mapsto (i_!(X') \to X) : B_H \to B_G . \]

We therefore see, thanks to (5.8.2) that if \(G\) is a Group of a topos, \(H\) a sub-Group, the functor "restriction of operators from \(G\) to \(H\)" can be interpreted as a location functor. In particular, taking for \(H\) the unit subgroup, we see that the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)" is interpreted as a location functor. We conclude, more generally, that if \((X,G)\) is an object of \(E\) with operation of \(G\) (i.e. an object of the topos classifying \(B_G\)), then the functor "forgetting the operations from \(G\)", \[ E' = (B_G)_{/(X,G)} \to E_{/X} \] can be interpreted as a location functor, relative to a suitable \(E_{G,(X,G)}\) object of \(E'\) covering the final object. Just take \(E_{G,(X,G)} = E_G \times (X,G)\), hence the Cartesian diagram (where \(E_G = G_s = G\) with operation of \(G\) by translation to the left) \[ (5.8.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ E_G \ar[d] & E_{G,(X,G)} = Z \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ e_G & (X,G) \ar[l] } \] and to note that by transitivity of the induced topos, the induced topos \(E' = ((B_G)_{X/G})/Z\) is equivalent to the topos \(((B_G)_{/E_G})_{/Z}\); as \((B_G)_{/E_G}\) is equivalent to \(E\) by the functor \(c^*\) of (5.8.1) (with \(H = e\)), it suffices to check that this equivalence transforms \(Z\) into the object \(X\) of \(E\) (which is trivial) to deduce the desired equivalence of categories \[ E'_{/Z} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } E_{/X} . \] We leave it to the reader to verify that the compound of this with the location functor \[ E' = (B_G)_{/(X,G)} \to E'_{/Z} \] is the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)". We thus see that the Cartesian diagram above gives, by passage to induced topos, a diagram, commutative up to canonical isomorphism (5.6), of morphisms of topos: \[ (5.8.3.2) \qquad \xymatrix{ (B_G)_{/E_G} \approx E \ar[d]_-{j} & (B_G)_{/Z} \approx E'_{/Z} \approx E_{/X} \ar[l]_-{i} \ar[d]^-{j'} \\ B_G & (B_G)_{/(X,G)} = E' \ar[l]^-{f} } \] where the inverse image functors associated with the vertical arrows \(j\), \(j'\) are the "forgetting of the operations of \(G\)", and where the inverse image functor \(i^*\) identifies with the location functor \(E \to E_{/X}\). This diagram is also "2-Cartesian" in the sense 5.11 below.

We leave it to the reader to extend the preceding reflections to the case of a pro-group \(\underline{G} = (G_i)\), in the special case where \(H\) is defined as the "core" of \(\underline{G} \to G_{i_o}\). (In the case of pro-finite groups, this means that we limit ourselves to the subgroups \(H\) of \(G\) open, i.e. closed and of finite index.)

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 5.9

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(G\) a Group of \(E\), \(B_G\) the classifying topos of \(G\) (2.4), \[ \pi : B_G \to E \] the topos morphism deduced from the homomorphism of \(G\) in the Unit Group \(e\) of \(E\) (taking into account that \(B_e \approx E\)).

  1. a)

    Either \[ E_G = G_s \] the object of \(B_G\) whose underlying \(E\)-object is \(G\), the operations of \(G\) being defined by translation to left. Consider \(\pi^*(G)\) as a Group of \(B_G\) (this is the Group \(G\) of \(E\) with the operations trivialities of \(G\) above). Show that the morphism of \(E\) \[ G_s \times G \to G_s \] defined by the right translations of \(G\) on \(G_s\), is compatible with the operations of \(G\) on \(G_s\) and on \(G = \pi^*(G)\), and defines a morphism of \(B_G\) \[ E_G \times \pi^*(G) \to E_G . \] Show that this morphism makes \(E_G\) an object of \(B_G\) with an operation to the right of \(B_G\)-Group \(\pi^*(G)\), and that the latter makes \(E_G\) a torsor under \(\pi^*(G)\) (i.e. the final object of \(B_G\) can be cover by objects \(X_i\) such that for all \(i\), the restriction of \(E_G\) to \(X_i\) is isomorphic to the bundle at trivial operators \((\pi^*(G)_{X_i})_d\)).

  2. b)

    Either \[ q : E' \to E \] a topos on \(E\). For any topos \(B\) on \(E\), define the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(E',B)\) of morphisms of topos of \(E'\) in \(B\) compatible with the structural morphisms \(E' \to E\) and \(B \to E\) at given isomorphism close. Taking \(B = B_G\), define by the formula \(f \mapsto f^*(E_G)\) an equivalence of categories \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(E',B_G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Tors}}(E,q^*(G)) , \] where the second member designates the category of \(q^*(G)\)-torsors (right) on \(E\).

  3. c)

    Let \(H\) be a Subgroup of \(G\), so that \(\pi^*(H)\) is a Subgroup of \(p^*(G)\), and therefore operates on \(E_G\) by restriction of the Operator Group, hence a quotient object \[ X = E_G/\pi^*(H) , \] which is none other than the object \(G/H\) equipped with the usual left operation of \(G\). Let \(e_G\) be the final object of \(B_G\) (i.e. the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\) with trivial operation - there is no choice - of \(G\)), and consider in \(B_G\) the morphism diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & E_G \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ e_G & & X = E_G/\pi^*(H) } \] hence, passing to the topos induced by \(B_G\) on these objects, a diagram of topos morphisms, commutative up to canonical isomorphism (5.6). Show that this diagram is equivalent to the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ E = B_e \ar[r] \ar[d] & B_H \ar[dl] \\ B_G & } \] whose three arrows are the arrows associated by 4.5 with the morphisms of Groups \(e \to H \to G\) of \(E\). (Thus, the classifying topos \(B_H\) is intuitively interpreted as a homogeneous space above \(B_G\), of group \(\pi^*(G)\), associated with the torsor (= homogeneous main bundle) universal \(E_G\) on \(B_G\)).

  4. d)

    Resume example 5.7 in the case where \(X'\) is a flat covering (locally trivial, cf. 2.7.4) of \(X\), interpreting it in terms of the considerations of the present exercise.
5.10. Inverse images of induced topos

Let \[ f : E' \to E \] a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(X\) an object of \(E\), and \(X' = f^*(X)\) its inverse image in \(E\). We will then define a topos morphism diagram \[ (5.10.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ E_{/X} \ar[d]_-{j_X} & E'_{/X'} \ar[l]_-{f_{/X}} \ar[d]^-{j_{X'}} \\ E & E' \ar[l]^-{f} } \] where \(j_X\) and \(j_{X'}\) are the location morphisms (5.1), and where \(f_{/X}\) is defined below, diagram which will be commutative up to canonical isomorphism. Here we will define \(f_{/X}\) using the image functor reverse \[ (5.10.2) \qquad (f_{/X})^* : E_{/X} \to E'_{/X'} , \] for which we will simply take the functor "induced" by \(f^*\), in an obvious sense. Like the inclusion functors \(j_{X'!} : E'_{/X'} \to E'\) and \(j_{X!} : E_{/X} \to E\) commute to \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits and to fibered products, and that they are conservative, it immediately follows that the functor \((f_{/X})^*\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits and fiber products just like the functor \(f^*\) which induces it; as more \((f_{/X})^*\) obviously transforms final object \(X\) into final object \(X'\), it is left-exact, so is associated with a topos morphism \[ (5.10.3) \qquad f_{/X} : E'_{/X'} \to E_{/X} , \] defined up to a single isomorphism (3.1.1). On the other hand, the diagram deduced from (5.10.1) by passing to inverse image functors is commutative (up to canonical isomorphism) by construction and thanks to the fact that \(f^*\) commutes to the products \(X \times Y\), therefore (5.10.1) itself is commutative, to a unique isomorphism near inducing the canonical isomorphism on the reciprocal image functors of the two compounds \(f \circ j_{X'}\), and \(j_X \circ f_{/X}\) (3.2.1).

When \(f : E' \to E\) is associated with a continuous map \(Y' \to Y\) of topological spaces (4.1), so that \(X\) is identified with a space spread above \(Y\), and \(X'\) with the fiber product \(X \times_Y Y'\), then, identifying the induced topos \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)_{/X}\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(Y')_{/X'}\) to \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(X')\) respectively (5.7), we note that the morphism \(f_{/X}\) that we have just constructed is (up to a single isomorphism) the morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\) deduced from the canonical continuous map \(X' \to X\). We can therefore say, at the In light of this example, the topos \(E'_{/X'}\) plays the role of a fiber product of \(E_{/X}\) and \(E'\) on \(E\). This intuition is clarified using the following result:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.11

The notations being those of 5.10, the diagram (5.10.1), provided with the compatibility isomorphism \(\alpha : f \circ j_{X'} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } j_X \circ f_{/X}\), is "2-Cartesian"; in a way specifies, for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F\), if we associate with any morphism of topos \(g : F \to E'_{/X'}\), the triple \((f_{/X} \circ g, j_{X'} \circ g, \alpha * g) = (g_1, g_2, \beta)\), where \(g_1 : F \to E_{/X}\) and \(g_2 : F \to E'\) are topos morphisms, and \(\beta : j_X \circ g_1 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f \circ g_2\) is an isomorphism of topos morphisms of \(F\) in \(E\), we find an equivalence of categories of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E'_{/X'})\) with the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E_{/X}) \times^{2}_{\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E)} \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E')\) of all triples \((g_1, g_2, \beta)\) as above.
(NB. We put a 2 above the sign of the Cartesian product to remind you that it is not a ordinary fiber product of categories, but of a "2-fiber product", in the sense explained in the statement.)
To prove 5.11, we explain the topos morphisms using inverse image functors associates. For this we will need auxiliary results, given in 5.11.1 to 5.12 below.

Let us consider generally the situation where we give ourselves two categories \(E\), \(E'\), an object \(X\) of \(E\) which is assumed to be quarable i.e. such that the functor \[ j : A \mapsto A \times X : E \to E_{/X} \] is defined, finally an exact functor on the left \[ (*) \qquad \varphi : E_{/X} \to E' . \] As \(E_{/X}\) has a final object, namely \((X,\mathrm{id}_X) = X\), it is therefore the same for \(E'\), which admits the final object \[ e' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varphi(X) . \] Assuming chosen the final object \(e'\) of \(E'\), we go to all \(\varphi\) as above to associate a couple \[ (**) \qquad (\psi , u) , \quad \text{avec } \psi = \varphi \circ j : E \to E', \text{ et } u \in \mathrm{Hom}(e',\psi(X)) . \] To define \(u\), we note that \[ \psi(X) = X \times X \] has a canonical section \(\delta_X\) on the final object \(X\) of \(E_{/X}\), namely the diagonal section, and we define \[ u = \varphi(\delta_X) : \varphi(X) = e' \to \varphi(X \times X) = \psi(X) . \] I say that knowledge of the couple (**) allows us to reconstruct the functor \(\varphi\) from (*) to isomorphism almost unique. For this, for any object \[ p : X' \to X \] of \(E_{/X}\), consider the following Cartesian diagram of \(E_{/X}\): \[ \xymatrix{ X' \ar[r] \ar[d] & j(X') = X' \times X \ar[d]^-{j(p)} \\ X \ar[r]_-{\delta_X} & j(X) = X \times X } \] where the first horizontal arrow is the graph morphism \(\Gamma_p = (\mathrm{id}_X, p)\). Applying the exact functor to left \(\varphi\) to this diagram, and using the definition of \(\psi\) as \(\varphi \circ j\), we find a diagram Cartesian \[ \xymatrix{ \varphi(X',p) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \psi(X') \ar[d]^-{\psi(p)} \\ e' \ar[r] & \psi(X) } \] in other words we find a canonical isomorphism \[ (5.11.1.1) \qquad \varphi(X',p) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \psi(X') \times_{\psi(X)} e' . \] We immediately see that it is functorial in the object \((X',p)\) of \(E_{/X}\), which explains how we reconstruct up to isomorphism the functor \(\varphi : E_{/X} \to E'\) using the pair \((\psi,u)\) of (**). Note also that the functor \(\psi\) is left-exact; more generally, \(\psi\) commutes to any type of projective limits to which commutes \(\varphi\), because \(j\) commutes at projective limits.

Conversely, now assuming that in \(E\) and \(E'\) the finite projective limits are representable, and starting from a couple \[ (\psi,u) , \quad \psi : E \to E' , \quad u \in \mathrm{Hom}(e',\psi(X)) , \] where the functor \(\psi\) is left-exact, we define by formula (5.11.1.1) a functor \(\varphi : E_{/X} \to E'\). We immediately checks that this functor is exact on the left, more generally, that it commutes at all types of limits projectives representable in \(E\) to which commutes \(\psi\). This immediately follows from the Cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \varprojlim_I^E X'_i \ar[d] & \varprojlim_I^{E/X} X'_i \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ \varprojlim_I^E X & X \ar[l]^-{\delta} } \] connecting the projective limits calculated in \(E\) or in \(E_{/X}\) (where \(\delta\) denotes the diagonal morphism in the projective limit of the constant functor with value \(X\)), by applying to it the functor \(\psi\), hence a diagram Cartesian square in \(E'\), and by composing the latter with the Cartesian square deduced from \(\psi(X) \leftarrow e'\), making appear a compound Cartesian square which expresses the compatibility of \(\varphi\) with the projective limit considered.

We reconstruct up to isomorphism the couple \((\psi,u)\) using \(\varphi\), noting that we have a canonical isomorphism \[ (5.11.1.2) \qquad \psi(X') = \varphi(j(X')) , \] deduced from (5.11.1.1) by replacing \((X',p)\) by \(j(X') = (X' \times X, \mathrm{pr}_2)\), and noting that \(\psi(X' \times X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \psi(X') \times \psi(X)\). The previous isomorphism is clearly functorial in \(X'\), i.e. it gives an isomorphism \[ \psi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varphi \circ j , \] and we also verify that through this isomorphism, \(u : e' \to \psi(X)\) is identified with \(\varphi(\delta_X)\). We thus have got the gist of

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.11.2

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two categories where the finite projective limits are representable, \(X\) an object of \(E\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E')\) the category of left-exact functors \(\psi\) of \(E\) in \(E'\), and \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E_{/X},E')\) the analogous category of left-exact functors \(\varphi\) of \(E_{/X}\) in \(E'\). We have then an equivalence between the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E_{/X},E')\) and the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E')_{/\Gamma}\) of the couples \((\psi,u)\), with \(\psi \in \operatorname{ob} \underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E')\) and \(u : e' \to \psi(X)\) (where \(e'\) is the final object of \(E'\)), whose definition is explained in 5.11.1, as well as that of a quasi-inverse functor. If \(\varphi\) and \((\psi,u)\) match, then \(\varphi\) commutes to a determined type of projective limits if and only if this is the case for \(\psi\). If in \(E\) and \(E'\) the base change functors commute to a determined type of inductive limits, then so that \(\varphi\) commutes at the inductive limits of said type, it is necessary and sufficient that it be thus of \(\psi\).

We leave it to the reader to explain the category structure \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E')_{/\Gamma}\) corresponding to the pairs \((\psi,u)\). Defining the functor \(\Gamma : \underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E') \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) by \(\Gamma(\psi) = \mathrm{Hom}(e',\psi(X))\), we can interpret \(u\) in the pair \((\psi,u)\) as an element of \(\Gamma(\psi)\) i.e. a homomorphism \(\psi \to \Gamma\) in the category of pre-sheaves on \(\underline{\mathrm{Sex}}(E,E')^\circ\), which justifies the notation \({}_{/\Gamma}\) used in the statement of 5.11.1. It remains, to prove 5.11.2, to explain in 5.11.1 the functional character of the constructions envisaged for \(\varphi\) resp. \((\psi,u)\) variable, which is essentially trivial and left to the reader, and finally to verify the assertion concerning the switching properties at inductive limits, which is also trivial using the explicit formulas (5.11.1.1) and (5.11.1.2) relating these functors.
We find in particular, when \(E\) and \(E'\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and by interpreting the morphisms of topos using the associated inverse image functors:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.12

Let \(E\) and \(E'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(X\) an object of \(E\), and consider on \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E)\) the contravariant functor \[ \gamma : f \mapsto \Gamma(E',f^*(X)) = \mathrm{Hom}(e',f^*(X)) : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E)^\circ \to (\mathrm{Ens}) . \] We then have an equivalence of categories \[ (5.12.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E_{/X}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E)_{/\gamma} , \] where the second member is the full subcategory of \(\widehat{\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}}(E',E)_{/\gamma}\) formed of the pairs \((f, u)\), with \(f \in \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E)\) and \(u \in \gamma(f)\) i.e. \(u \in \Gamma(E',f^*(X))\). This functor is obtained by associating to any morphism of topos \(h : E' \to E_{/X}\) the pair \((f,u)\) formed of the compound \[ f = j_X \circ h : E' \to E_{/X} \to E , \] and morphism \[ u : e' = h^*(X,\mathrm{id}_X) \to f^*(X) = h^*(j_X^*(X)) = h^*(X \times X) \] deduced from the diagonal morphism \(\delta_X : X \to X \times X\) by applying \(h^*\).

Using 5.12, the demonstration of 5.11 becomes more or less obvious, and is reduced essentially this (which will take the place of a "formal" demonstration which would not be more instructive): giving oneself a morphism of topos \(g : F \to E'_{/X'}\), "comes back to the same" as giving oneself a morphism of topos \(g_2 : F \to E'\) and a section \(u\) of \(g_2^*(X')\); or \(g_2^*(X') = g_2^*(f^*(X)) = (fg_2)^*(X)\), so the data of \(u\) is also equivalent to the data of a raising of the morphism of topos \(fg_2 : F \to E\) into a topos morphism \(g_1 : F \to E_{/X}\). This clearly expresses that the datum of a topos morphism \(g : F \to E'_{/X'}\), is essentially the same as giving a triple \((g_1,g_2,\alpha)\) as in 5.11.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.13

We will see (§ 15) that for any diagram \[ \xymatrix{ E_1 \ar[d] & \\ E & E_2 \ar[l] } \] of topos, there exists a 2-fibre product in the sense of the 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Top})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(\in \mathcal{V}\) (neither not dependent, up to equivalence, on the choice of a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\)). For other examples natural as 5.11 of "topos fiber products", see the last chapter of GIRAUD's book [3].

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 5.14
  1. a)

    Let \(F\), \(G\) be two topos above a topos \(E\). Set category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(F,G)\) of pairs \((f,\alpha)\), where \(f : F \to G\) is a morphism of topos and \(\alpha : p \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } q \circ f\) is an isomorphism of topos morphisms from \(E\) into \(G\) (\(p : F \to E\) and \(q : G \to E\) being the morphisms of structural topos). Define mating functors \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(G,H) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(F,H) , \] and associativity isomorphisms.

  2. b)

    Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two objects of a topos \(E\). Define a natural functor of the discrete category defined by the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\), in the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(E_{/X},E_{/Y})\). Compatibility with the compositions of morphisms in \(E\) and the couplings considered in a).

  3. c)

    Prove that the functor considered in b) is an equivalence of categories. In particular, an object \(X\) of a topos \(E\) is reconstituted up to a unique isomorphism, when we know "at \(E\)-near equivalence" the topos induces \(E_{/X}\) as topos above \(E\).

footnotes.html: (∗) For a switching property of \(j_{X!}\) when changing topos, cf. XVII 5.1.2.

pages.title: 6. Points of a topos and fiber functors

blocks.html: 6. Points of a topos and fiber functors

Let \(P\) be the \(\mathcal{U}\)-type point topos (2.2) \[ P = (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) . \] Given the quite different intuition which attaches on the one hand to the(∗) symbol \(P\), representing a geometric object in the nature of a point, and on the other hand to \((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\), representing a "large category", which we will interpret as "the category of beams on \(P\)", it is appropriate depending on the context to use one or the other symbol exclusively, although from a strict logical point of view they designate one and the same object.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We call point of \(E\) any topos morphism \(P \to E\) from point topos \(P\) (6.0) in \(E\). We call category of points of \(E\), and we note \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) or \(\underline{\mathrm{Pt}}(E)\), category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(P,E)\) (3.2). If \(p : P \to E\) is a point of \(E\), associated with the image functor inverse \(p^* : E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\), and if \(F\) is an object of \(E\), the set \(p^*(F)\) is called fiber of \(E\) in \(p\), and noted \(F_p\).
Of course, when \(F\) is a group object (resp. ring, resp. ...) of \(E\), its fiber \(F_p\) in \(p\) is a group (resp. a ring, resp. ...) (3.1.2).

By virtue of 3.2.1, the point category of \(E\) is equivalent, via the functor \(p \mapsto p^*\), to the category opposite to that of functors \[ \varphi : E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \] which commute at \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits and which are left-exact. It therefore essentially comes down to even to give oneself a point of \(E\), or such a functor \(\varphi\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.2

We call fiber-functor from \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) any functor \(\varphi : E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) which commutes at \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits and which is left-exact. We call category of fiber functors on \(E\), and we note \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(E)\), the subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}))\) formed from fiber functors on \(E\).

By virtue of 6.1.2, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(E)\) of fiber functors on \(E\) is therefore equivalent to the opposite of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) of the points of \(E\), via the functor \(p \mapsto p^*\) of the latter in the first: \[ (6.2.1.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \approx \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(E)^\circ . \] So that a functor \[ \varphi : E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \] is a fiber functor, it must and is sufficient for it to be the fiber functor \(F \mapsto F_p\) associated with a point \(p\) of \(E\) (6.1). In other words, the functor (6.2.1.1) is surjective. Let us also point out that a functor \(\varphi\) is a fiber functor if and only if it is left exact and if it transforms covering families into families surjectives (1.7). This last condition is also expressed by saying that \(\varphi\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-sums and transforms epimorphisms into epimorphisms.

Let \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) be a site. We call point of the site \(C\) any point of the topos \(\widetilde{C}\), category of points of the site \(C\) category \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(C) = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widetilde{C}) . \] Consider on the other hand the functor \[ (6.3.1) \qquad \varphi \mapsto \varphi|C = \varphi \circ \varepsilon_C : \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(\widetilde{C}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})) , \] which is fully faithful and whose essential image is the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}),C)^\circ\) (4.9.4), which we will also note \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C)\). A functor \[ \psi : C \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \] is called a fiber functor on the site \(C\) if it is in the essential image of (6.3.1), which we come to explain. Such a functor is therefore continuous (a fortiori (III 1.6), it transforms covering families into covering families, i.e. in surjective families), and it is exact on the left. When within \(C\) the limits finite projectives are representable, (condition verified in almost all of the cases that we encounter in practice.) the previous properties characterize the fiber functors on \(C\), which are then the functors \(\psi : C \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) which are left-exact and which transform covering families into families surjectives (4.9.4).

We will remember that the functor \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|C\) is an equivalence of categories \[ \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(\widetilde{C}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C) , \] so that it essentially amounts to the same thing to give a fiber functor on the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) (or even a point in this topos), or a fiber functor on the site \(C\). Given a fiber functor \(\psi\) on \(C\), therefore coming from an isomorphism near a fiber functor \(\varphi\) on \(\widetilde{C}\), we reconstitute the latter using \(\psi\), up to canonical isomorphism, by the formula \[ (6.3.2) \qquad \varphi(F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/F}} \psi(X) , \] where \(C_{/F}\) is the category of objects \(X\) of \(C\) with a morphism \(X \to F\) (in \(\widehat{C}\)), i.e. an element of \(F(X)\). Formula (6.3.2) is an immediate consequence of the fact that \(\varphi\) commutes at the inductive limits and that we have a canonical functorial isomorphism in \(F\) (II 4.1.1): \[ F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/F}} \varepsilon_C(X) . \] More generally, when \(G\) is a presheaf on \(C\), we have a canonical functorial isomorphism in \(G\): \[ (6.3.3) \qquad \varphi(aG) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/G}} \psi(X) , \] where \(aG\) is the beam associated with \(G\) (II 4.1.1). Indeed, we know that we have the formula \[ aG \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/G}} \varepsilon_C(X) . \]

We refer to I 6.1 for the notion of conservative family of functors \[ \varphi_i : E \to E_i \qquad i \in I . \] Note that when \(E\) and the \(E_i\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and the \(\varphi_i\) functors are image functors inverses \(f_i^*\) associated with topos morphisms \[ f_i : E_i \to E , \] then all the correctness properties postulated in I 6.2, I 6.3 and I 6.4 are verified, provided in I 6.2 (v) to assume that \(D\) is finite. It then amounts to the same thing whether \((f_i^*)_{i \in I}\) is conservative, or conservative for monomorphisms (I 6.1), or conservative for epimorphisms, or finally that it be faithful.
When the family of functors \(f_i^*\) is conservative, we will also sometimes say that the family of topos morphisms \((f_i)_{i \in I}\) is conservative. In particular:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.4.1

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \((p_i : P \to E)_{i \in I}\) be a family of points of \(E\). We say that this family of points is conservative if the family of associated fiber functors \(F \mapsto F_{p_i}\) of \(E\) in \((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) is conservative (6.4.0). We say that the topos \(E\) has sufficient points when it admits a conservative family of points (i.e. when the family of all the points of the topos is conservative).

Please note that all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos used so far have sufficient points. We can however “on purpose” construct topos which do not have enough points (7.2.6 e) and 7.4); we will note that such a topos is necessarily not "empty" in the geometric sense of 2.2. A topos admitting enough points admits a conservative family of points indexed by a \(I \in \mathcal{U}\) (6.5). Note however on this subject that if \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, the set of point isomorphy classes of \(E\) is not not necessarily \(\mathcal{U}\)-small (7.3). However, it is encountered in many cases in practice. Finally, for an interesting existence theorem (due to P. Deligne) of sufficient points, covering all cases encountered in algebraic geometry,

When \((p_i)_{i \in I}\) is a conservative family of points of the topos \(E\), we can apply the remarks of I 6.2, which imply in particular that two arrows \(u,v : F \rightrightarrows G\) of \(E\) are equal if and only if for all \(i \in I\), the deflections induced on the fibers \(u_{p_i}, v_{p_i} : F_{p_i} \rightrightarrows G_{p_i}\) are equal; that an arrow \(u : F \to G\) of \(E\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism) if and only if for all \(i \in I\), this is the case for the application \(u_{p_i} : F_{p_i} \to G_{p_i}\); that an object \(F\) of \(E\) is initial (resp. final) if and only if for all \(i \in I\), \(F_{p_i}\) is empty (resp. reduced to a point); that an object \(F\) is a subobject of the final object \(e_E\) if and only if for all \(i \in I\), \(F_{p_i}\) has at most one point.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5
  1. a)

    Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \((\varphi_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of fiber functors on \(C\) (6.3). For the family \((p_i)_{i \in I}\) of corresponding points of the topos \(E = \widetilde{C}\) to be conservative, it is necessary and it is sufficient that for every family \((X_j \to X)_{j \in J}\) in \(C\), such that for every \(i \in I\), the family corresponding \((\varphi_i(X_j) \to \varphi_i(X))_{j \in J}\) is surjective, the given family \((X_j \to X)_{j \in J}\) is covering.

  2. b)

    Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. If \(E\) admits enough points (6.4.1), then \(E\) admits a conservative family of points which is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.

Assertion b) is a special case of I 7.7. To prove a), let us apply I 7.7 to the family generator in \(E\) formed of \(\varepsilon(X)\) (\(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\)). Let us recall (II 4.4) that the family \(X_j \to X\) is covering if and only if the family \(\varepsilon(X_j) \to \varepsilon(X)\) is epimorphic. If the family of points \((p_i)_{i \in I}\) is conservative, it amounts to the same thing (6.4.3) to say that for all \(i \in I\), the family of \((X_j)_{p_i} \to (X)_{p_i}\) is surjective, i.e. the family \(\varphi_i(X_j) \to \varphi_i(X)\) is surjective. This establishes the “must” in a). For "it is enough", we apply criterion I 7.7, which brings us back to verifying that everything monomorphism \(F \to \varepsilon(X)\), such that \(F_{p_i} \to \varepsilon(X)_{p_i}\) is an isomorphism for all \(i \in I\), is a isomorphism, or what amounts to the same thing, an epimorphism. However, we can find a family covering morphisms \(\varepsilon(X_j) \to F\), and even if it means further refining, we can assume that the morphisms \(\varepsilon(X_j) \to \varepsilon(X)\) are induced by morphisms \(X_j \to X\). By hypothesis, for all \(i \in I\), the compound morphisms \((X_j)_{p_i} \to F_{p_i} \to (X)_{p_i}\) form a surjective family (as composed of two surjective families), i.e. the family of \(\varphi_i(X_j) \to \varphi_i(X)\) is surjective. This results in hypothesis that the family \(X_j \to X\) is covering, therefore that the family of \(\varepsilon(X_j) \to \varepsilon(X)\) is epimorphism, and a fortiori that \(F \to \varepsilon(X)\) is epimorphic.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.5.1

Let \(C\) be a category. A topology on \(C\) making \(C\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site admitting enough fiber functors (i.e. such that the associated topos admits enough points) is entirely known when we know the full subcategory \(\Phi\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,\mathrm{Ens})\) formed of fiber functors on \(C\).
Indeed, by virtue of 6.5 a) we then know how to describe the covering families in terms of the family fiber functors.

In fact, we will see below (6.8.3) that the subcategory \(\Phi\) is contained in the category of functors prorepresentable on \(C\), therefore \(\Phi^\circ \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widetilde{C})\) is identified up to equivalence with a full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}\text{-}C\).

We will compare 6.5.1 to the theorem of GIRAUD II 5.5, which establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the set of topologies on \(C\) and a certain set of subcategories full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,\mathrm{Ens})\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 6.5.2

Let \(C\) be a category equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\), \(P\) a subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\). For all \(p \in P\), we denote by \(X \mapsto X_p\) the functor prorepresented by \(p\). A family \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) in \(C\) is said to be \(P\)-covering if for all \(p \in P\), the family \((X_{i\,p} \to X_p)_{i \in I}\) is surjective. Show that there exists a topology \(T_P\) on \(C\) for which the covering families are exactly the \(P\)-spanning families, and that \(T_P\) is the finest topology on \(C\) for which the functors \(X \mapsto X_p\) (\(p \in P\)) are fiber functors. Show that the topology \(T_P\) makes \(C\) a site having sufficient fiber functors. Show that for any topology \(T\) on \(C\), among the topologies \(T'\) finer than \(T\) which have enough fiber functors, there is one less fine: it is the topology \(T_P\), where \(P\) is the strictly full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\), equivalent to \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(C)\), described in 6.8.3 below.

blocks.title: Issue
blocks.html:
Problem 6.5.3

Characterize subcategories (strictly full, stable by \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits filtering projectives ...) \(P\) of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) which can be deduced from a topology on \(C\) as image essential of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widetilde{C})\) (6.8.5).(∗)

Either \[ f : E \to E' \] a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, we deduce a canonical functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(f) = ( p \mapsto f \circ p) : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E') . \] When we have two composable topos morphisms \[ E \xrightarrow{\ f\ } E' \xrightarrow{\ g\ } E'' , \] we check trivially that we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(gf) = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(g) \circ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(f) : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E'') \] This therefore specifies the functorial dependence (without abuse of language for once) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) in relation to at the topos \(E\): if \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\), we find a (real!) functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}} : (\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-top}) \to (\mathcal{V}\text{-cat}) \] from the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos which are elements of \(\mathcal{V}\) in the category of categories elements of \(\mathcal{V}\).

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. Then any object \(F\) of \(E\) defines a counterfunctor \[ p \mapsto F_p = p^*(F) : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)^\circ \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) , \] hence for \(F\) variable a canonical functor \[ (6.7.1) \qquad E \to \widehat{\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)^\circ, (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})) , \] which by definition (6.5) is faithful if and only if \(E\) has enough points. (The functor (6.7.1) has a certain formal analogy with a Fourier transformation...)

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), which therefore defines a pre-beam \(\widehat{X}\) on \[ C = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) . \] We can therefore define the category \[ C_{/\widehat{X}} = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)_{/\widehat{X}} \] morphisms \(p \to \widehat{X}\) in the category \(\widehat{C}\) of presheaves on \(C\), i.e. the category of couples \((p,\xi)\), where \(p\) is a point of \(E\) and \(\xi\) an element of \(\widehat{X}(p) = X_p\), fiber of \(X\) in \(p\). Having said that, I say that we have an equivalence of canonical categories \[ (6.7.2) \qquad C' = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E_{/X}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } C_{/\widehat{X}} , \qquad \text{où } C = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) , \] whose composite with the inclusion functor \(C_{/\widehat{X}} \to C\) is none other than the functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(j_X) : C' = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E_{/X}) \to C = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \] deduced by functoriality (6.6) from the location morphism (5.2.1) \[ j_X : E_{/X} \to E . \] This is simply the special case of 5.12 obtained by doing \(E' = P\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.7.3

Let \(E\) be a topos. If \(E\) has enough points, the same applies to any induced topos \(E_{/X}\) (\(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\)). Conversely, if \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) is an object family of \(E\) that covers the final object, such that for all \(i \in I\), \(E_{/X_i}\) has enough points, then \(E\) has enough points.

For the first assertion, let \(f : X' \to X''\) be a morphism in \(E_{/X}\) which is not a isomorphism, we prove that there exists a point \(q\) of \(E_{/X}\) such that \(f_q\) is not an isomorphism. Like \(E\) has enough points, there exists a point \(p\) of \(E\) such that \(f_p : X'_p \to X''_p\) is not an isomorphism. This implies that there exists a \(q \in X_p\) such that the morphism induced by \(f_p\) for the fibers \(X'_{q,p}\), \(X''_{q,p}\) from \(X'_p\), \(X''_p\) to \(X_p\) to \(q\) is not an isomorphism. But under equivalence 6.7.2, \(q\) can identify with a point of \(E_{/X}\), and the application \(X'_q \to X''_q\) that we have just considered is none other than the application on the fibers in \(q\) induced by \(f : X' \to X''\), hence the conclusion.

Conversely, suppose that the \(X_i\) cover the final object of \(E\), and that the \(E_{/X_i}\) have enough of points, let us prove that the same is true of \(E\). Let \(f : X' \to X''\) be a morphism in \(E\) which is not an isomorphism. There therefore exists a \(i \in I\) such that \(f_i : X'_i \to X''_i\) is not an isomorphism, where the subscript \(i\) indicates the restriction to \(X_i\). There is therefore a point \(p_i\) of \(E_{/X_i}\) such that \(f_{i\,p_i} : X'_{i\,p_i} \to X''_{i\,p_i}\) is not an isomorphism. Designating as \(p\) the image of \(p_i\) in \(E\) by the location morphism \(E_{/X_i} \to E\), this means that \(X'_p \to X''_p\) is not not an isomorphism, which proves that \(E\) has enough points.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 6.7.4
