The preceding argument shows that if \((p_\alpha)\) is a conservative family of points of \(E\), then the family of points of \(E_{/X}\) which are above one of \(p_\alpha\) (family indexed by the sum set of \(E_{p_\alpha}\)) is conservative. Likewise, if the \(X_i\) cover the final object of \(E\), and if for all \(i \in I\), \(P_i\) is a conservative set of points of \(E_{/X_i}\), then the set of points of \(E\) images of \(p \in P_i\) for location morphisms \(E_{/X_i} \to E\) (\(i \in I\) and \(p\) variables) is conservative.

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(p\) be a point of \(E\). We call neighborhood of the point \(p\) of the topos \(E\) a couple \((X,u)\), where \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) and \(u \in X_p\). By virtue of (6.7.2), we can also interpret \(u\) as an increase in \(p\) at a point of the induced topos \(E_{/X}\). Introducing the fiber functor \(\varphi_p\) associated with the point \(p\), we can also interpret a neighborhood of the point \(p\) as an object of the category \(E_{/\varphi_p}\) (subcategory full of \((E^\circ)^{\wedge}_{/\varphi_p}\) formed from objects whose source is in \(E \subset \widehat{E}\)). This last category, i.e. the category of couples \((X,u)\) as above, will correctly be called the neighborhood category of the point \(p\) of topos \(E\); we also note it \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)\). Since \(\varphi_p\) is left exact and the limits finite projective limits are representable in \(E\), finite projective limits are representable in the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)\), and the canonical functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p) \to E\) commutes there (I ). A fortiori, the category opposite \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)^\circ\) is filtering. Moreover, it is clear (I 3.4) that we have a canonical isomorphism, functorial in \(F \in \operatorname{ob} E\) \[ (6.8.1) \qquad \varphi_p(F) = F_p \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)^\circ} F(X) . \] In other words the functor \(\varphi_p\) "fiber at the point \(p\)" is isomorphic to the filtering inductive limit of the functors represented in the topos \(E\) by the neighborhoods of the point \(p\) of \(E\). We also see that the fiber functor is ind-representable (I 8), which also means that it is representable by a pro-object \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\), where \(I\) is a filtering ordered set such that \(I \in \mathcal{U}\). Indeed, under loc. cit. , he is the same as saying that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)\) admits a small cofinal full subcategory. But be it \(C\) a small full subcategory generating \(E\), I say that the full subcategory \(C_{/\varphi_p}\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p) = E_{/\varphi_p}\), formed from the neighborhoods \((X,u)\) of \(p\) such that \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\) (category which is obviously small) is cofinal in \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}(p)\). Let \((X,u)\) be a neighborhood of \(p\), we must find a neighborhood \((X',u')\) of \(p\) above \((X,u)\), with \(X' \in \operatorname{ob} C\). But there exists a covering family \(X'_i \to X\), with the \(X'_i\) in \(C\), from which it follows that the \(X'_{i\,p}\) cover \(X_p\), so there exists a \(X' = X'_{i_o}\) and a \(u' \in X'_p\) such that \((X',u')\) is above \((X,u)\), which was stated.

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, and \(p\) be a point of \(C\), i.e. a point of the topos \(\widetilde{C}\). Let us designate again, by abuse of notations, by \(\varphi_p\) the "restriction" of the fiber functor \(\varphi_p\) to \(C\), i.e. the compound \(\varphi_p \circ \varepsilon\); we we will also often write, for an object \(X\) of \(C\) \[ X_p = \varphi_p(X) = \varepsilon(X)_p . \] We call neighborhood of the point \(p\) in the site \(C\) a pair \((X,u)\), where \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\) and \(u \in X_p = \varphi_p(X)\). These neighborhoods still form a category \(C_{/\varphi_p}\), which we can denote \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)\). Let us show that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)^\circ\) is still filtering, that it admits a cofinal full subcategory \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, and that we have a functorial isomorphism into the sheaf \(F\) on \(C\): \[ (6.8.3) \qquad F_p \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)^\circ} F(X) . \] We first note, by taking up the argument of 6.8.1, that if \(C'\) is a full subcategory of \(C\) which is topologically generating (II 3.0.1), then \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_{C'}(p)^\circ\) is cofinal in \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)^\circ\). Taking \(C'\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, we are therefore brought back, to establish the assertion, in case \(C \in \mathcal{U}\). In this case \(\widetilde{C}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and we have an associated beam functor \(a : \widehat{C} \to \widetilde{C}\), which allows us to construct the functor on \(\widehat{C}\) compound fiber \(\varphi_p a\). Applying 6.8.1 to the topos \(\widehat{C}\) and to the full generating subcategory \(C\) thereof, we find that the category \(C_{/\varphi_p a}\), which is clearly isomorphic to \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)^\circ\), is filtering, and that we have a functorial isomorphism in the presheaf \(P\) on \(C\): \[ \varphi_p a(P) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_X P(X) . \] If \(F\) is a sheaf on \(C\), we have \(F = aF\), and applying the previous isomorphism to \(F\) considered as presheaf, we obtain (6.8.3). At the same time, this argument establishes the more general formula \[ (6.8.4) \qquad (a P)_p \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p)^\circ} P(X) , \] functorial isomorphism into the arbitrary presheaf \(P\) on \(C\), at least when \(C\) is small. The general case is deduced from this, by introducing a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(C \in \mathcal{V}\), by considering \(\varphi_p\) on \(C\) as a functor fiber with values in \((\mathcal{V}\text{-Ens})\), and using the compatibility of the formation of the pre-bundle associated with the enlargement of the universes (II 3.6).

We have associated with any point \(p\) of the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) a pro-object of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\), defined by the functor canonical \(\underline{\mathrm{Vois}}_C(p) \to C\), taking into account the fact that the category of neighborhoods of \(p\) in \(C\) is cofilter and admits a small cofinal full subcategory. We immediately check that for \(p\) variable, we thus obtains a functor \[ (6.8.5.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widetilde{C}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(C) . \] This functor is fully faithful. Indeed, via the associated fiber functors, it is interpreted as a functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C) \to \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(\widetilde{C}) \approx \mathrm{Pro}(C)^\circ\). The first functor is an equivalence under 4.9.4 (recalled for fiber functors in 6.3), and the second is fully faithful according to the general sorite (I 8) pro-objects.

Let us take the special case where \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and has the chaotic topology, so that \(C = \widehat{C}\). I say that in this case the previous functor is even an equivalence of categories \[ (6.8.6.1) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Pro}(C) , \] Indeed, it remains to prove that this functor is essentially surjective, which results from the fact that the functors of the form \(P \mapsto P(X)\) on \(\widehat{C}\) are fiber functors, and from the obvious fact that any limit inductive filtering of fiber functors on a topos is still a fiber functor.

Identifier \(C\) to a full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) (I 8), the functor \[ C \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C}) \] induced by a quasi-inverse functor of (6.8.5.2) is clearly isomorphic to the canonical functor already envisaged in (4.6.2.2), of which the quasi-inverse envisaged can be considered as the extension canonical (I 8), taking into account the fact that \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C})\) is stable under small projective limits.

Let generally \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a pro-object of \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\), hence on \(\widetilde{C}\) a functor \[ (6.8.7.1) \qquad \varphi(F) = \varinjlim_I F(X_i) , \] and it is natural to ask, given (6.8.5), when this functor is a fiber functor. We find that the following conditions are equivalent:

1. (i)

    The previous \(\varphi\) functor is a fiber functor on \(\widetilde{C}\).

  2. (ii)

    The "restriction" from \(\varphi\) to \(C\) is a fiber functor on site \(C\) (6.3).

  3. (iii)

    For any covering family \(Y_\alpha \to Y\) of an object \(Y\) of \(C\), all \(i_o \in I\) and any morphism \(X_{i_o} \to Y\), there exists a \(i \ge i_o\), a \(\alpha\) and a morphism \(X_i \to Y_\alpha\) making commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X_i \ar[r] \ar[d] & Y_\alpha \ar[d] \\ X_{i_o} \ar[r] & Y } \]

In fact, condition (iii) simply expresses the fact that \(\varphi|C\) transforms covering families into covering families. The implications (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) are trivial, and it remains to be proven (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). As the functor \(\varphi\) is obviously left exact, it remains to be proven (4.9.4) that it transforms covering families \(F_\alpha \to F\) in \(\widetilde{C}\) into covering families, i.e. that for all \(i_o\) and all \(x_o \in F(X_{i_o})\), there exists a \(i \ge i_o\), a \(\alpha\) and a \(y \in F_\alpha(X_i)\), such that \(x_o\) and \(y\) even have image in \(F(X_i)\). Now as \(F_\alpha \to F\) is covering, there exists a covering family \(Z_\lambda \to X_{i_o}\) of \(X_{i_o}\), such that for all \(\lambda\) the inverse image of \(x_o\) in \(F(Z_\lambda)\) is reassembled into an element of a \(F_\alpha(Z_\lambda)\). By assumption (iii), there exists a \(i \ge i_o\) and a \(X_{i_o}\)-morphism \(X_i \to Z_\lambda\). So the picture of \(x_o\) in \(F(X_i)\) is therefore reassembled into an element \(y\) of a \(F_\alpha(X_i)\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 6.9

Let \(C\) be a site \(\in \mathcal{U}\), \(\varphi\) a fiber functor on \(C\), prorepresented by an object \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) from \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\). For any index \(i \in I\), any object \(Y\) of \(C\), any morphism \(u : X_i \to Y\) and any sieve covering \(R\) of \(Y\), let us choose an index \(j \ge i\) such that the compound \(X_j \to X_i \xrightarrow{\ u\ } Y\) is factorized by \(R\). For \(i\) fixed, let \(J(i)\) be the set made up of \(i\), and \(j\) obtained for \(Y,u,R\) variables; for one part \(I'\) of \(I\), or likewise \(J(I')\) the meeting of \(J(i)\) for \(i \in I'\). On the other hand, if \(I'\) is a finite game of \(I\), i.e. \(m(I') \in I\) an increase of \(I'\), and for any part \(I'\) of \(I\), i.e. \(M(I')\) the meeting of \(M(I'')\), where \(I''\) traverses the set of finite parts of \(I'\). We suppose that we choose the function \(I' \to m(I')\) so that for \(I'\) reduced to an element \(i\), we have \(m(I') = i\), which implies that for all part \(I'\) of \(I\), we have \(I' \subset M(I')\). Consider the iterates \((MJ)^n\) of the application \(MJ\) of the set of parts of \(I\) in itself, and be, for any part \(I'\) of \(I\), \(P(I')\) the union of \((MJ)^n(I')\).

  1. a)

    Show that for any part \(I'\) of \(I\), \(P(I')\) is a filtering part of \(I\) for the induced order, and that \(I\) is an increasing filtering union of \(P(I')\), when \(I'\) goes through all of the finite parts of \(I\).

  2. b)

    Show that there exists a cardinal \(c \in \mathcal{U}\), depending only on the cardinal \(\operatorname{card} \mathrm{fl}(C) = a\), such that for any finite part \(I'\) of \(I\), we have \(\operatorname{card}(P(I')) \le c\). (If \(a\) is infinite, we can take \(c = 2^a\)).

  3. c)

    Show, using 6.8.7, that for any part \(I'\) of \(I\) of functor \(\varphi_{I'}\), on \(C\) prorepresented by the pro-object \((X_i)_{i \in I'}\), is a fiber functor on \(C\), and that the fiber functor \(\psi\) is the filtering inductive limit of the functors \(\varphi_{I'}\), when \(I'\) traverses the set of finite parts of \(I\).

  4. d)

    Conclude that there is a small full subcategory \(\Phi\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C) \approx \underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(\widetilde{C})\), such that any object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C)\) is a filtering inductive limit of objects of \(\Phi\). (If \(a\) is infinite, we can take \(\Phi\) of cardinal \(\le 2^{2^a}\); if \(a\) is finished, we can take \(\Phi = C\) of course).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 6.10

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where the small filtering \(\varinjlim\) are representable, \(\underline{\mathrm{Fais}}(C,D)\) the category of sheaves on \(C\) with values in \(D\) (II 6.1). Define, in extending (6.8.3), a “fiber functor” \[ (6.10.1) \qquad F \mapsto F_p : \underline{\mathrm{Fais}}(C,D) \to D . \] If in \(D\) the finite \(\varinjlim\) are representable, and commute to the filtering \(\varinjlim\), then the functor previous is exact left.

footnotes.html: (∗) Result due to P. DELIGNE. The case where \(E\) is the punctual topos had been treated previously by Ms. HAKIM.
(∗) cf. 9.1.8 a) and e) for necessary conditions, and 9.1.12 b) for necessary and sufficient conditions in the case of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), \(X\) sober locally Noetherian space.

pages.title: 7. Examples of fiber functors and topos points

blocks.html: 7. Examples of fiber functors and topos points
7.1. Case of Top(X) for a topological space X

Let \(x\) be a point of \(X\). Let us look at the point set \(\{x\}\) as a topological space, and consider inclusion \[ i_x : \{x\} \to X . \] By virtue of 4.1.1 it defines a topos morphism (defined up to unique isomorphism) \[ (7.1.1) \qquad \mathrm{Top}(i_x) : \mathrm{Top}(\{x\}) \to \mathrm{Top}(X) . \] On the other hand, we can identify \(P\) with \(\mathrm{Top}(\{x\})\) (2.2), and we thus find a point \(p_x\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\): \[ (7.1.2) \qquad p_x : P \to \mathrm{Top}(X) , \] defined by \(x\) up to a single isomorphism. The associated fiber functor is given by the well-known formula [TF], resulting immediately from I 5.1: \[ (7.1.3) \qquad p_x^*(F) = F_x = F_{p_x} = \varinjlim_{U \ni x} F(U) , \] the limit being taken according to the open neighborhoods \(U\) of \(x\) in \(X\).

As we know that \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Top}(X_{\mathrm{sob}})\) (4.2.1), we see more generally that any point of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), i.e. any closed irreducible part \(Z\) of \(X\), defines a point of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), or even a fiber functor on \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), which we will also note \(F \mapsto F_Z\). Returning to its definition by the formula (7.1.3) on \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), we find \[ (7.1.4) \qquad F_Z = \varinjlim_{U \cap Z \neq \varnothing} F(U) , \] the inductive limit being taken according to the open \(U\) of \(X\) which meets \(Z\).

It is well known that the fiber functors \(F \mapsto F_x\) (\(x \in X\)) already form a family conservative. This is particularly evident on the interpretation of the beams on \(X\) in terms of spaces spread out, the fiber of \(F\) in \(x\) then being none other than its fiber in the sense of the fibered spaces on \(X\). It will be noted that the set of indices of the conservative family of fiber functors considered is \(X\), therefore \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.

Under 4.2.3, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathrm{Top}(X))\) is equivalent to the category associated with the set \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) ordered by the specialization relation. In other words: any fiber functor on \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is isomorphic to a fiber functor \(F \mapsto F_Z\), where \(Z\) is a uniquely determined element of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), i.e. a uniquely determined irreducible closed part of \(X\); on the other hand, if \(Z,Z' \in X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), then the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(F_Z,F_{Z'})\) is empty or reduced to a point, the latter case occurring if and only if \(Z\) is a specialization of \(Z'\) in \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\), i.e. if and only if \(Z \subset Z'\) as part of \(X\). When \(X\) itself is sober, we can replace \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) in these statements with \(X\) itself.

Note that the group of automorphisms of a fiber functor of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (opposed to the group of the automorphisms of the corresponding point of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\)) is always reduced to the unit group (more generally, 4.2.3 tells us the same thing for the group of automorphisms of any morphism of topos associated with topological spaces). This is a very special phenomenon for the particular case considered: see example 7.2, as well as VIII 7. In the case of topos associated with "module problems" (cf. by example [10]), the automorphism groups of the fiber functors also have an interpretation remarkable, like the groups of automorphisms of algebraic structures (on fields algebraically closed) which we propose to classify.

We have just seen how we can reconstruct a sober space \(X\) (or the sober space \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) associated with any topological space), at least as a set ordered by the relation of specialization, using the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (which it is even enough to know up to equivalence), like the set of "point" isomorphy classes of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\). According to what was said in 2.1, we also reconstructs the topology of \(X\), i.e. the family of its open objects, in the following way: for all sub-object \(U\) of the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\), i.e. \(\mathrm{Pt}(U)\) the set of \(x \in X\) such that \(U_x \neq \varnothing\) (which is also identified, by virtue of (6.7.2), with the set of isomorphy classes of points of the induced topos \(E_{/U}\)). Then \(U \mapsto \mathrm{Pt}(U)\) is an ordered set isomorphism of the set of sub-objects of \(e_E\) on all the openings of \(X\).

7.1.6 determination of the category of topos points \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) defined by a space topological \(X\) leads to adopting the following terminology for the points \(p,p'\) of any topos \(E\): we says that \(p\) is a specialization of \(p'\), or that \(p'\) is a generization of \(p\), when there is a morphism of \(p'\) in \(p\) (within the meaning of category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) of 6.1). These relations are still transitive, but we easily found thanks to 6.8.6 examples where \(p\) and \(p'\) are each specialization of the other, without \(p\) and \(p'\) are isomorphic (nor a fortiori equal). The category of generation of a point \(p\) of the topos \(E\), by which we mean the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)_{/p}\), plays in certain respects a role analogous to that of the transition to localized a diagram at a point. This role will be clarified in Chap. VI with the construction of the localized topos of \(E\) at the point \(p\), whose category of points is canonically equivalent to the category of generalizations by \(p\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.1.9

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. Prove that \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), where \(X\) is a topological space \(\in \mathcal{U}\), if and only if it satisfies the following two conditions:

  1. a)

    The family of sub-objects of the final object \(e_E\) is generator for the topos \(E\).

  2. b)

    \(E\) has enough points (6.5). (This condition is not superfluous: cf. 7.4.)

Compare with 7.8 c).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.1.10

Let \(X\) be a topological space with a group of operators \(G\) (\(X,G \in \mathcal{U}\)) and let \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) (2.3).

  1. a)

    Show that for any object \((X',G)\) of \(E\), the induced topos \(E_{/(X',G)}\) is canonically equivalent to the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X',G)\) (compare 5.7).

  2. b)

    When \(G\) operates cleanly and freely on \(X'\) (Bourbaki, Top. Gen. Chap. III § 4), show that the morphism of spaces with operators \((X',G) \to (X'/G,e)\) induces (4.1.2) a equivalence of topos \[ \mathrm{Top}(X',G) \to \mathrm{Top}(X'/G) . \]

  3. c)

    Conclude from b) that there exists an object \(Z\) of \(E\) such that the induced topos \(E_{/Z}\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), the location functor \(E \to E_{/Z}\) being isomorphic to the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)". (Copy the reasoning of 5.8.3.)

  4. d)

    Conclude from c) that any fiber functor on \(E\) is induced, via the "forget" functor operations of \(G\)", by a fiber functor on \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), therefore definable by a point of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\).

  5. e)

    Determine the structure of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) (up to equivalence) in terms operator space \((X_{\mathrm{sob}},G)\). Conclude in particular that two points of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) define fiber functors on \(E\) isomorphic if and only if they are conjugated under the action of \(G\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.1.11

Let \(X \in \mathcal{U}\) be a topological space. Prove that the \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) (2.5) has enough points. More precisely, for any object \(X'\) of \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\), and any \(x' \in X'\), define a fiber functor \(F \mapsto F_{X',x'}\) on \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\), depending only on the space "seed" \((X',x')\) above \(X\), and prove that this family of fiber functors is conservative (and indexed by a set of indices \(\mathcal{V}\)-small). Show, using a suitable filtering projective system \((X'_i,x'_i)_{i \in I}\), with \(I\) not \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, that we do not obtain in this way all the fiber functors of the \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\). Show that the set of isomorphy classes of such fiber functors is not of cardinal \(\in \mathcal{V}\).
7.2. Points of a classifying topos \(B_G\)

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(G\) a Group of \(E\), hence a classifying topos \(B_G\) (2.4). If \(e\) is the Point group, the morphisms \(e \to G \to e\) define (4.5) topos morphisms (taking into account that \(B_e \approx E\)) \[ (7.2.1) \qquad E \to B_G \to E , \] whose compound is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{id}_E\), hence by passing to the point categories of the functors \[ (7.2.2) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) , \] whose compound is isomorphic to the identity. Let's use the fact that the first morphism of topos (7.2.1) identifies with a location morphism relative to the object \(E_G = G\) of \(B_G\) (5.8), from which results in virtue of 6.7.2 that the first functor (7.2.2) identifies with the natural "inclusion" functor \[ (7.2.3) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G)_{/\widehat{E}_G} \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G) , \] where \(\widehat{E}_G\) denotes the pre-beam \(p' \mapsto (E_G)_{p'}\) on \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G)\). As \(E_G\) obviously covers the object final of \(B_G\), its fibers \((E_G)_p\) are non-empty, from which it follows that (7.2.3) is essentially surjective, this which means that any fiber functor on \(B_G\) is induced (up to isomorphism) by a fiber functor of \(E\), via the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)" \(B_G \to E\). We conclude in particular that if \(E\) has enough points (resp. a small conservative family of points) it is the same for \(B_G\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 7.2.4

We can go further and determine (up to equivalence) the structure of the category \(C' = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G)\) in terms of category \(C = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) and pre-bundle in groups \[ \widehat{G} : p \mapsto G_p : C^\circ \to (\mathrm{Groupes}) \] on this one. Let us indeed define a new category \(C_1\), having the same objects as \(C\), and such that for two objects \(p,q\) of \(C\), we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{C_1}(p,q) = \mathrm{Hom}_C(p,q) \times \widehat{G}(p) , \] the composition of the arrows in \(C_1\) being induced by that of \(C\), and by the group law of the pre-bundle \(\widehat{G}\). The data of a functor \(\varphi_1 : C_1 \to D\) in any category \(D\) is then equivalent to the data of a functor \(\varphi : C \to D\), provided with an operation of the pre-sheaf \(\widehat{G}\) on the latter (i.e. the data, for all \(p \in \operatorname{ob} C\), of an operation of \(\widehat{G}(p)\) on \(\varphi(p)\), satisfying a functionality condition obvious for \(p\) variable). Using this observation, we define a canonical functor \[ (7.2.4.1) \qquad \varphi_1 : C_1 \to C' , \] corresponding to the functor \(\varphi : C \to C'\) of (7.2.2), associating with any point \(p\) of \(E\) the point \(\varphi(p)\) induced on \(B_G\), with the natural operations of \(\widehat{G}(p) = G_p\) on the latter, coming from the operations of \(G_p\) on the \(X_p\) when \(X\) traverses \(B_G\). We leave it to the reader to prove that (7.2.4.1) is an equivalence of categories, using the structure (7.2.3) of the first functor of (7.2.2), and noting that the functor natural inclusion \(C \to C_1\) admits an analogous structure, relative to the inverse image \(P_1\) of pre-beam \(P' = \widehat{E}_G\) on \(C'\).

We conclude in particular that the point isomorphy classes of \(B_G\) correspond exactly to the isomorphy classes of points of \(E\): any point of the classifying topos \(B_G\) is induced, to non-unique isomorphism up to a point of \(E\). In particular, when \(E\) is the point topos \(P\), so that \(G\) is an ordinary group, then the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G)\) is a groupoid connected to group fundamental \(G\): any fiber functor on \(B_G\) is isomorphic (in a non-canonical way) to the functor \(\omega\) "forget of the operations of \(G\)", and the monoid of the endomorphisms of this last functor is the group \(G\) (so all endomorphism of \(\omega\) is an automorphism).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.2.6
  1. a)

    Let \((\mathrm{Tors})\) be the category of couples \((\Gamma,P) \in \mathcal{U}\), with \(\Gamma\) a group and \(P\) a torso on the right under \(\Gamma\). The functor \((\Gamma,P) \mapsto \Gamma\) \[ (7.2.6.1) \qquad (\mathrm{Tors}) \to (\mathrm{Groupes}) \] is a cofibrating functor. With the notations of 7.2.4, consider the functor \[ \widehat{G} : C^\circ \to (\mathrm{Groupes}) , \] and let \(D'\) be the cofibered category on \(C^\circ\) inverse image of the cofibered category (7.2.6.1), \(D\) the category corresponding fiber on \(C\), having the same fiber categories as \(D'\), so that for \(p \in \operatorname{ob} C\), we have \[ D_p = \text{catégorie des } G_p\text{-torseurs à droite} . \] Construct canonical functors \[ (7.2.6.2) \qquad D \to C' = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G) , \qquad C' \to D , \] almost opposites of each other. In particular, conclude that the category of points of \(B_G\) above a given point \(p\) of \(E\) is canonically equivalent to the category of right twisters under the group \(G_p\).

  2. b)

    Let \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\) be a strict projective system of Groups of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) (\(I \in \mathcal{U}\) a filtering preordered set), hence a classifying topos \(B_{\underline{G}}\), by copying the definition of 2.7.1. Determine the category of points of \(B_{\underline{G}}\), by tracing a). (NB. We will replace the categories \((\mathrm{Tors})\) and \((\mathrm{Groupes})\) by the categories of projective systems indexed by \(I\) of these categories.) In conclude that if \(I\) admits a countable cofinal set, then every fiber functor on \(B_{\underline{G}}\) is isomorphic to a functor induced by a fiber functor of \(E\) (via the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)"), the latter being determined up to non-unique isomorphism.

  3. c)

    Taking \(E\) to be the punctual topos, so that \(\underline{G}\) is an ordinary pro-group, show that the conclusion of b) is also valid if \(I\) is arbitrary, but on the other hand \(\underline{G}\) is profinite (i.e. the \(G_i\) are finite): any fiber functor is isomorphic to the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)".

  4. d)

    Always taking the case where \(E\) is the punctual topos, give an example of a fiber functor on \(B_{\underline{G}}\), for a suitable projective system \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\), which is not isomorphic to the functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)".

  5. e)

    Let us consider \(\underline{G} = (G_i)\) as a Group of the topos \(\widehat{I}\), and let \(T\) be a sheaf on \(I\) from link \(\underline{G}\) [3]. Show how we can “twist” the classifying topos \(B_{\underline{G}}\) using the sheaf \(T\), to obtain a topos \(B_{\underline{G}}^T\), inductive limit of subcategories \(B^T(i)\) equivalent (non-canonically) to the classifying topos \(B_{G_i}\). Show that the topos \(B_{\underline{G}}^T\) admits a fiber functor if and only if the sheaf \(T\) is "neutral", establishing an equivalence of categories between the category of fiber functors of \(B_{\underline{G}}^T\) and the category of sections of \(T\) on \(I\). To conclude, if the \(G_i\) are commutative, such that the classification of sheaves on \(I\) of link \(\underline{G}\) is done by \(\varprojlim_I^{(2)} G_i = H^2(I,\underline{G})\) (loc. cit.), an example of a topos (not "empty" (2.2)) of the form \(B_{\underline{G}}^T\) which has no points. (Take an example where \(\varprojlim_I^{(2)} G_i \neq 0\).)
7.3. Topos points \(\widehat{C}\); examples of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(\widehat{C}\) whose category of points is not equivalent to a small category

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \((\varphi_i)_{i \in I}\) be a filtering family of fiber functors on \(E\). It results of the correctness properties of the functors \(\varinjlim\) (I and ) that the functor \(\varphi = \varinjlim \varphi_i\) is also a fiber functor: any filtering inductive limit of fiber functors is a fiber functor. Subsequently, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(E)\) admits \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering inductive limits (and the inclusion functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}))\) switches there); in other words, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) admits \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering projective boundaries. This fact has already been used in exercise 7.1.10 to give an example of “big” categories of fiber functors.

We can construct much simpler examples, with topos of the form \(E = \widehat{C}\), \(C \in \mathcal{U}\), using (6.8.6.1). This immediately gives us examples where \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(C)\) is not equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\) i.e. where the cardinality of the set of isomorphy classes of objects of this \(\mathcal{U}\)-category is not \(\in \mathcal{U}\). This means that the category \(\mathrm{Pro}\text{-}(C)\) is not equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\). For example, it suffices to take for \(D = C^\circ\) the category of finite sets of the form \([0,n]\), where \(n \ge 0\) is an integer, in which case \(\mathrm{Ind}(D) \approx \mathrm{Pro}(C)^\circ\) is equivalent to the category non-empty sets. The topos \(E\) is in this case the well-known category of sets cosimplicials. We could also take for \(C\) the category of non-empty finite sets, we find that the category of points on the topos \(\widehat{C}\) of simplicial sets is equivalent to the category of profinite sets, or even to the category of totally discontinuous compact spaces.
7.4. Non-empty topos without dots

The following example is due to P. Deligne. (For another example, see 7.2.6 d).) We take a compact space \(K\) provided with a measure \(\mu\), and the ordered set \(U\) of the measurable parts of \(K\) to set near zero measurement. We make \(U\) a category \(\underline{U}\) such that \(\operatorname{ob} \underline{U} = U\), the morphisms of \(\underline{U}\) being the "inclusion morphisms" between elements of \(U\). We make \(\underline{U}\) a site by taking the pretopology for which \(\mathrm{Cov}(E)\) (for \(E \in U\)) is formed of the countable families of elements \(E_i\) of \(E\) increased by \(E\), such that \(E\) is the union of \(E_i\) up to zero measurement set. We deduce a Topos \(\mathrm{Top}(\mu) = \underline{U}^{\sim}\), admitting the set of sub-objects of the final object as generating family (which topos seems to have escaped the attention of probabilists). This topos is an "empty topos" (2.2) if and only if \(\mu = 0\). On the other hand, the category of points of this topos is equivalent to the discrete category defined by the set of points \(x \in K\) such as \(\mu(\{x\}) \neq 0\) (demonstration to the reader). She is therefore empty if \(K\) does not admit such points, for example if \(K\) is the unit segment of the line, with the measure induced by the Lebesgue measurement.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.5 (Karoubian categories and topos morphisms)(∗)
  1. a)

    Let \(C\) be a category, \(X\) an object of \(C\), \(p\) an endomorphism of \(X\). They say that \(p\) is a projector if \(p^2 = p\). Prove that if \(p\) is a projector, so that \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm{id}_X,p)\) is representable, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm{Coker}(\mathrm{id}_X,p)\) be, and that the two objects of \(C\) thus obtained are canonically isomorphic. We will then say that the projector \(p\) admits an image, and \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm{id}_X,p) \approx \mathrm{Coker}(\mathrm{id}_X,p)\) is called the projector image \(p\); we identify it according to the context with a sub-object or a quotient object of \(C\). An object isomorphic to a \(\mathrm{Im}\, p\), for a suitable projector \(p\) in \(X\), is called a direct factor of the object \(X\) of \(C\). We say that \(C\) is a category with factors direct, or a Karoubian category, if every projector in an object of \(C\) admits an image. If \(F : C \to C'\) is a functor that commutes to kernels or cokernels, so \(F\) transforms a projector admitting an image into a projector admitting an image.

  2. b)

    Show that for any category \(C\), we can find a functor \(\varphi : C \to \mathrm{kar}(C)\) of \(C\) in a Karoubian category (determined up to equivalence), such that for any karoubian category \(C'\), the functor \[ f \mapsto f \circ \varphi : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{kar}(C),C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \] either an equivalence of categories; we will call \(C'\) the Karoubi envelope of the category \(C\). (Hint: take for \(\operatorname{ob} \mathrm{kar}(C)\) the set of couples \((X,p)\), with \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\) and \(p\) a projector in \(X\), and for \(\mathrm{Hom}((X,p),(Y,q))\) the part of \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) formed by \(f : X \to Y\) such that \(f = qfp\).) Show that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful.

  3. c)

    Let \(F : E \to E'\) be a functor from one category to another. Show that if \(F\) commutes to a certain type of inductive or projective limits, it is the same for any direct factor of \(F\). In particular, if \(E\) and \(E'\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and if \(F\) is an inverse image functor for a morphism of topos \(E' \to E\), then it is the same for any direct factor of \(F\); consequently, the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E',E)\) is Karoubian, and in particular the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) is Karoubian.

  4. d)

    Show that for any \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\), the category \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is karubian (where \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is formed with the inductive systems of \(C\) indexed by a preordered set filter \(I \in \mathcal{U}\)). (If \(C \in \mathcal{U}\), use for example the fact (7.3) that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is equivalent to the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Fib}}(\widehat{C^\circ}) = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C^\circ})^\circ\).) Concluding another construction of \(\mathrm{kar}(C)\) as a subcategory full of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) formed from the images in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) of the object projectors \(X\) of \(C\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.6 (Essential morphisms of topos, essential points)
  1. a)

    Let \(f : E \to F\) be a topos morphism. Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

    1. i)

      \(f_!\) exists, i.e. \(f^*\) admits a deputy on the left.

    2. ii)

      \(f^*\) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective boundaries.

    3. ii')

      \(f^*\) switches to \(\mathcal{U}\)-products.

    We will then say that \(f\) is an essential morphism of the topos \(E\) in the topos \(F\). Show that if \(f\) satisfies these conditions, it is the same for any direct factor of \(f\). (Use 1.8 and 7.5 c).)

  2. b)

    Let \(E\) be a topos. We call essential point of the topos \(E\) any point \(p : P \to E\) of \(E\) such that \(p_!\) exists. Show that if \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\), \(X\) topological space, and if \(p\) is the point of \(E\) defined by a \(x \in X\), then \(p\) is essential if and only if \(x\) admits a smallest open neighborhood \(U\) (which means, if the points of \(X\) are closed, that \(x\) is an isolated point of \(X\)). So that a topos morphism \(f : E \to F\) is essential (a)), \(f\) must transform essential points into essential points, and this condition is also sufficient when \(E\) admits sufficient essential points (i.e. if the family of these points is conservative) (cf. d)).

  3. c)

    For a point \(p\) of the topos \(E\) to be essential, it is necessary and sufficient that the functor associated fiber is representable by an object \(X\) of \(E\). So that the covariant functor \(\varphi : E \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) represented by a given object \(X\) of \(E\) or a fiber functor (i.e. defines a point, necessarily essential, of \(E\)), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) be non-empty connected (4.3.5), and projective (i.e. that the functor \(\varphi\) transforms epimorphisms into epimorphisms). (Hint: first show that for \(\varphi\) to switch to are, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) be non-empty connected, then use criterion 4.9.4.) Conclude one equivalence between the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Pointess}}(E)\) and the full subcategory \(P\) of \(E\) formed from related objects non-projective voids.

  4. d)

    Show that the topology of \(P\) induced by that of \(E\) is the chaotic topology. Conclude the equivalence of the following conditions on \(E\) (due to J.E ROOS [12 c], prop. 1]): (i) The family essential points of \(E\) is conservative; (ii) The full subcategory \(P\) of \(E\) formed by objects related-non-vacuum projectives is generative; (iii) \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a category equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\) (or, if we prefer, \(C \in \mathcal{U}\)). (Use c) and the fact that if \(P\) is generating, the natural functor \(E \to P^{\sim}\) is an equivalence of categories.)

  5. e)

    The category of essential points of a topos \(E\) is Karoubian (cf. (7.5 c)). Let \(C\) be a category equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\). Prove that the fully faithful functor canonical (4.6.2) \[ C \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C}) \] is factorized by a functor \[ C \to \underline{\mathrm{Pointess}}(\widehat{C}) , \] which makes \(\underline{\mathrm{Pointess}}(\widehat{C})\) a Karoubi envelope (7.5 b)) of \(C\). In particular, this functor is a equivalence of categories if and only if the category \(C\) is Karoubian. (Hint: using c), prove that any isolated point of \(\widehat{C}\) is isomorphic to a direct factor of a \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\).)

  6. f)

    Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two categories equivalent to categories \(\in \mathcal{U}\). Prove that the canonical fully faithful functor (4.6.2) \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\widehat{C},\widehat{C}') \] takes its values in the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtopess}}(\widehat{C},\widehat{C}')\) of essential topos morphisms (a)), and that the functor induces \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtopess}}(\widehat{C},\widehat{C}') \] is an equivalence of categories if and only if \(C\) is empty or \(C'\) is Karoubian. (Use g) below.)

  7. g)

    Let \(C\) be a category equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\), and \(E\) be a topos. Define an equivalence between the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtopess}}(\widehat{C},E)\) of essential morphisms of topos \(\widehat{C} \to E\), and the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,\underline{\mathrm{Pointess}}(E))\). (Use b) and the fact that \(\widehat{C}\) has enough points essential).

  8. h)

    Let \(C\) be a finite category. Prove that every point of \(\widehat{C}\) is essential, and that \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\widehat{C})\) is equivalent to a finite category. (Note that the Karoubi envelope of \(C\) is finite, and using 6.8.6.1, reduce to proving that if \(C\) is a finite Karoubian category, then the functor canonical \(C \to \mathrm{Pro}(C)\) is an equivalence of categories). Using b), conclude that any morphism of topos \(\widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}\) is essential, and \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\widehat{C},\widehat{C}')\) is an equivalence if and only if \(C\) is empty or \(C'\) karubian.

  9. i)

    Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(f : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to P\) the topos morphism deduced from the continued application of \(X\) in point space. Show that \(f\) is essential if and only if the space \(X\) is locally connected, i.e. satisfies the following condition: for all \(x \in X\) and any open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\), the connected component of \(x\) in \(U\) is a neighborhood of \(x\), or again: for all open \(U\) of \(X\), the related components of \(U\) are open. (see 8.7 b) for generalization).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.7 (Unusual points of a classifying topos)(∗)
  1. a)

    Let \(G\) be a monoid. For \(G\) to contain an element \(g\) such that \(g \neq e\), \(g^2 = g\), it is necessary and sufficient that the classifying topos \(B_G\) admits an essential point which is not isomorphic at the trivial point (corresponding to the fiber functor "forgetting the operations of \(G\)"). (Use 7.6 e)).

  2. b)

    Let \(G\) be the additive monoid of the integers \(\ge 0\). Show that any essential point of topos classifier \(B_G\) is isomorphic to the banal point. Construct a point of \(B_G\) which is not isomorphic to the banal point. Show that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(B_G)\) is not equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.8 (Topology on Point(E), and topos associated with ordered sets)
  1. a)

    Let \(E\) be a topos. Let us designate by \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}'E\) the set of classes, at isomorphism close to, points of \(E\). For all open \(U\) of \(E\), i.e. \(U'\) the set of point classes \(p\) of \(E\) such as \(U_p \neq \varnothing\). Show that \(U \mapsto U'\) is an increasing map of the ordered set \(\mathrm{Ouv}(E)\) opens of \(E\) in all parts of \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), and that this application switches to finite Inf to any Sup. Conclude that the set of parts of \(X\) of the form \(U'\) defines on \(X\) a topology (called canonical topology on all classes of points of the topos \(E\)). Show that if \(E\) has enough points, the map \(U \mapsto U'\) is an isomorphism of the ordered set \(\mathrm{Ouv}(E)\) with the ordered set \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\).

  2. b)

    Let \(\mathcal{O} \in \mathcal{U}\) be an ordered set admitting finite Inf and Sup any, which therefore defines a category \(\mathrm{cat}(\mathcal{O})\) having finished products and any sums. We will say that a family of morphisms \(U_i \to U\) with the same goal \(U\) is covering if \(U\) is the Sup of \(U_i\). Assuming that in \(\mathrm{cat}(\mathcal{O})\) the sums are universal i.e. that in \(\mathcal{O}\) any Sup commutes in Inf, we thus define on \(\mathrm{cat}(\mathcal{O})\) a topology which makes it a site, hence a topos \(\mathrm{cat}(\mathcal{O})^{\sim}\), noted as simply \(\mathcal{O}^{\sim}\). Show that \(\mathcal{O}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Ouv}(\mathcal{O}^{\sim})\).

    Show that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,\mathcal{O}^{\sim})\) is equivalent to the category associated with the set ordered morphisms of ordered sets \(\mathcal{O} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(E)\) which commute to finite Inf and Sup any. (Use criterion 4.9.4.) If \(\mathcal{O}'\) is an ordered set satisfying the same conditions that \(\mathcal{O}\), conclude that \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathcal{O}^{\sim},\mathcal{O}'^{\sim})\) is equivalent to the category associated with the set ordered of all morphisms of ordered sets \(\mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}'\) which commute to finite Inf and Sup any.

  3. c)

    Show that for the topos \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathcal{O}^{\sim}\), with \(\mathcal{O}\) as in b), it is necessary and sufficient that the family of open sets of \(E\) be generating in \(E\). For it to be equivalent to a \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) for \(X \in \mathcal{U}\), it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfies the condition previous and that he has enough points. Assuming the first condition is fulfilled, express the second in terms of the structure of the ordered set \(\mathcal{O} = \mathrm{Ouv}(E)\), using the last assertion of b).

  4. d)

    Define a canonical topos morphism \[ E \to \mathrm{Ouv}(E)^{\sim} , \] which is universal (up to equivalence) for the morphisms of \(E\) in topos generated by their open (see c)).

  5. e)

    Let \(X'\) be a small subset of \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), which we equip with the topology induced by that of \(X\). Define a canonical morphism of topos \[ \mathrm{Ouv}(E)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Top}(X') , \] which is an equivalence when the family \(X'\) of points of \(E\) is conservative. Then conclude one canonical morphism of topos \[ E \to \mathrm{Top}(X') . \] Give a universal characterization (up to equivalence) of this topos morphism for morphisms of the topos \(E\) into topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\). (Note in this regard that up to equivalence, \(\mathrm{Top}(X')\) does not does not depend on the small conservative family of fiber functors chosen, or which amounts to the same thing (4.2), that \(X'_{\mathrm{sob}}\) does not depend up to homeomorphism on the choice of such a family).

  6. f)

    Suppose that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) is not equivalent to a small category (cf. 7.3), and that \(E\) admits a small conservative family of fiber functors. Show that if such a family is taken large enough, the part \(X'\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) that it defines is not a space sober topology for the induced topology.

  7. g)

    For relationships with exercise 7.6, cf. 8.

footnotes.html: (∗) Compare I 8.7.8.

blocks.html: 8. Location. Opened with a topos

Let \(C\) be a category, and \(T(X)\) be a relation involving an object \(X\) of \(C\). It is said that the relation \(T(X)\) is stable by change of base (in \(X\)), if for any morphism \(X' \to X\) of \(C\), the relationship \(T(X)\) implies \(T(X')\). It amounts to the same thing to say that the full subcategory \(R\) of \(C\), formed of objects \(X\) of \(C\) such that we have \(T(X)\), is a sieve (I 4.1).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.1.1

Suppose that \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (I 1.1), so that \(C\) can be identified to a full subcategory of \(\widehat{C}\) (I 1.4). It is sometimes convenient to extend the definition of relation \(T\) in \(\operatorname{ob} C\) into a relation \(\widehat{T}\) relating to an object \(F\) of \(\widehat{C}\), designating by \(\widehat{T}(F)\) the relation: for any arrow \(X \to F\) in \(\widehat{C}\), with \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\), we have \(T(X)\). The relationship \(\widehat{T}(F)\) is obviously still stable by change of base in \(F\). Again designating by \(R\) the sub-object of the object final \(e\) of \(\widehat{C}\) defined by the sieve \(R\) of \(C\) (I 4.2.1), the relation \(\widehat{T}(F)\) can also be expressed by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(F,R) \neq \varnothing\), i.e. it means that the unique morphism \(F \to e\) is factorized by the sub-object \(R\) by \(e\).

Now suppose that \(C\) is a site. A relation \(T(X)\) in an argument \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\) is said stable by descent if for any covering family \((X_i : X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) of an object \(X\) of \(C\), the relation "\(T(X_i)\) for all \(i \in I\)" implies the relation \(T(X)\). We say that \(T\) is a relation of a local nature if it is stable by change of base (8.1) and stable by descent. When \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and \(T\) is already assumed to be stable by change of base, i.e. that it is definable by a sieve \(R\) of \(C\), which we will identify with a sub-pre-beam of the final pre-beam on \(C\), we immediately see that \(T\) is of a local nature if and only if \(R\) is a bundle. Thus, relations of a local nature in an argument \(X \in \operatorname{ob} C\) correspond exactly to the sub-beams of the final beam of \(C\), i.e. to the sub-objects of the final object of topos \(\widetilde{C}\). They therefore essentially depend only on the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) defined by the site \(C\) (like all important concepts associated with a site!). In terms of the sub-bundle \(R\) of the final beam, the relation \(T(X)\) is expressed as \(\mathrm{Hom}(\varepsilon(X),R) \neq \varnothing\). It is canonically extended in the relation \(\widetilde{T}(F) : \mathrm{Hom}(F,R) \neq \varnothing\) on the topos \(\widetilde{C}\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.2.1

With the notation introduced in 8.1.1, for a pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\), as \(\mathrm{Hom}(F,R) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(a(F),R)\), the relation \(\widehat{T}(F)\) is equivalent to the relation \(\widetilde{T}(a(F))\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.3

We call open of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) any sub-object of the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\); if \(X\) is an object of \(E\), we sometimes call open of \(X\) any open of the induced topos \(E_{/X}\), i.e. all subobject of \(X\).

We call open of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\) an open of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos associated \(\widetilde{C}\).

Note that, the final objects of \(E\) being canonically isomorphic, the ambiguity introduced in 8.3 by the choice of \(e_E\) is harmless; we can also, in a more intrinsic but less manageable way from a practical point of view, define the open ones of \(E\) as being the full subcategories \(R\) of \(E\) which are such that the relation \(F \in \operatorname{ob} R\) for an object \(F\) of \(E\) is a relation of local nature. Likewise, the opened from a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\) correspond one-to-one to the full subcategories of \(C\) such as the relation \(F \in \operatorname{ob} R\) for an object of \(C\) is of local nature; this notion is therefore essentially independent of the chosen universe \(\mathcal{U}\). Finally, by virtue of what was said in 8.2, a relationship of nature local on a topos (or on a site) is essentially the same thing as opening said topos (or site).
8.4. Examples of open.

Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let us interpret \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (2.1) as the category of spaces spread over \(X\). As it is clear that the monomorphisms in \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) are the injective maps, it It follows that the open spaces of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) are identified with the open spaces of the space \(X\). More generally, the open of a topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) (2.4) are identified with the open of \(X\) invariants by the action of \(G\).

The open ones of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) form a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small set (I 7.4) ordered by the inclusion, admitting arbitrary sups and finite infs. It admits the initial object (or "empty object") \(\varnothing_E\) as more small element, and the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\) as the largest element. These two elements are only identical if \(E\) is an "empty topos" (2.2).

Let \(G\) be a Monoid in \(E\), hence a topos \(B_G\) (2.4), category of objects of \(E\) on which \(G\) operates on the left. The final object \(e_G\) of \(B_G\) is the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\) with trivial operation of \(G\). We see by following that the ordered set of open sets of \(B_G\) is canonically isomorphic to the category of open sets from \(E\). In particular, if \(E\) is the point topos therefore \(G\) an ordinary monoid, the set of open sets of \(B_G\) is exactly made up of two elements, namely \(\varnothing_{B_G}\) and \(e_G\).

If \(E\) is a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, then (8.1) the ordered set of the open ones of \(E\) is isomorphic to the ordered set of the sifters of \(C\).

Let \(X\) be a sober non-discrete topological space. Show that \(\mathrm{TOP}(X)\) (2.5) has open sets which do not come from openings of \(X\), i.e. openings of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), by inverse image using the morphism canonical (4.10.1) \(\mathrm{TOP}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\).
8.5. Examples of local relationships
For any object \(X\) of the topos \(E\), we denote by \(F \to F_X\) the location functor \(j_X^* : E \to E_{/X}\), \(Y \mapsto Y \times X\) (5.2).

Let \(u : F \to G\) be a morphism of \(E\). The relationship in the argument \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\), \[ u_X : F_X \to G_X \text{ est un monomorphisme (resp. un épimorphisme, resp. un isomorphisme)} \] is a local relationship. In particular, if \(G\) is a Group of \(E\), the relation "\(G_X\) is the group unit on \(X\)" is local in nature; the open of \(E\) which corresponds to it is called the cosupport of the Group \(G\).

Let \(u,v : F \rightrightarrows G\) be two morphisms of \(E\). The relationship in the argument \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) \[ u_X = v_X : F_X \to G_X \] is a local relationship. In particular, if \(G\) is a Group of \(E\), and \(u\) a section of \(G\) (4.3.6), the relationship in \(X\) \[ \text{la section } u_X \text{ du Groupe } G_X \text{ de } E_{/X} \text{ est nulle} \] is local in nature; the open of \(E\) which corresponds to it is called the cosupport of the \(u\) section of the Group \(G\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.5.3

When \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\), the co-support of a beam in groups \(G\), resp. of a section of such a beam \(G\), is none other than the open of \(X\) complementary to the support of \(G\) resp. of support for \(u\) in classical terminology [TF].

Let \(P(f)\) be a relation in the argument \(f \in \mathrm{fl}\, C\), \(C\) a site. When in \(C\) fiber products are representable, we say that the relation \(P(f)\) is stable by change of base (resp. stable by descent), resp. of a local nature) on the goal (or on the base, or "down"), if for any morphism \(f : X \to Y\) of \(C\), the relationship in argument \(Y' \in \operatorname{ob} C_{/Y}\): \[ \text{"le morphisme } f' : X' = X \times_Y Y' \to Y' \text{ déduit de } f \text{ par changement de base} \] by the structural morphism \(Y' \to Y\) satisfied \(P(f')\)" is stable by change of base, resp. stable by descent, resp. is local in nature. When the topology of \(E\) is defined using a pretopology, we says that the relation \(P(f)\) is local in nature on the source (or "up") if for any morphism \(f : X \to Y\) of \(C\) and any family \((g_i : X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\), the relation \(P(f)\) is equivalent to the relation "\(P(fg_i)\) for all \(i \in I\)". When \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) is formed of all the covering families of \(C\), this condition therefore also means that the relationship in the object \(X'\) of the site induces \(C_{/X}\) \[ P(fg), \text{ où } g : X' \to X \text{ est le morphisme structural} \] is local in nature. Note that if the relation \(P(f)\) is of a local nature at the top, it is a fortiori of a nature local at the bottom.

Take for example \(C = (\mathrm{Sch})\), schema category \(\in \mathcal{U}\), with the topology faithfully quasi-compact flat (SGA 3 IV 6.3) or a less fine topology. Then each of the following properties of a morphism is local in nature at the bottom:

surjective, radical, universally open, universally finite, proper, quasi-compact, quasi-compact and dominant, universal homeomorphism, separated, quasi-separated, locally of finite type, locally of finite presentation, of finite type, of finite presentation, an isomorphism, a monomorphism, an open immersion, a closed immersion, a quasi-compact immersion, affine, quasi-affine, finite, almost finished, whole, flat, faithfully flat, clean, smooth, spread

(EGA IV 2.6.4, 2.7.1 and EGA IV 17.7.1). On the other hand, let's equip \((\mathrm{Sch})\) with the pretopology to which, for any schema \(X \in \mathcal{U}\), \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) is formed of families of morphisms \((f_i : X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) which are surjective and such that the \(f_i\) are flat and locally of finite presentation. So each of following properties is local in nature at the top:
locally of finite type, locally of finite presentation, of finished type, flat, clean, smooth, spreading.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.6

Let \(f : E \to F\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. Consider the relationship in the argument \(X \in \operatorname{ob} F\): the induced morphism \(E_{/f^*(X)} \to F_{/X}\) is an equivalence of topos. Prove that this relationship is local in nature.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.7 (Partitions of a topos, sum of topos)

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. A family \((e_i)_{i \in I}\) of open \(E\), i.e. sub-objects of the object final \(e\) of \(E\), is called a partition of \(e\) (or again, of the topos \(E\)) if the canonical morphism \(\coprod_{i \in I} e_i \to e\) is an isomorphism, i.e. (II 4.6 2)) if \(e\) is the sup of \(e_i\) and if \(i \neq j\) implies \(e_i \cap e_j = \varnothing_E\).

  1. a)

    Show that for the family \((e_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(E\) to be a partition of \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \[ (8.7.1) \qquad E \to \prod_i E_{/e_i} \] defined by the location functors \(E \to E_{/e_i}\) i.e. an equivalence of categories.

  2. b)

    Conversely let \((E_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, with \(I \in \mathcal{U}\). Prove that \(E = \prod_{i \in I} E_i\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (which will be called the sum topos of the topos family \((E_i)_{i \in I}\)). Define a partition \((e_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\) and category equivalences \(E_{/e_i} \approx E_i\), such that way that projection functors \(E \to E_i\) identify with location functors \(E \to E_{/e_i}\).

  3. c)

    Let \(E\) be the sum topos of the family of topos \((E_i)_{i \in I}\). Show that for everything \(i \in I\) the projection functor \(E \to E_i\) is of the form \(u_i^*\), where \[ (8.7.2) \qquad u_i : E_i \to E \] is a topos morphism. Prove that for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F\), the functor \[ v \mapsto (v \circ u_i)_{i \in I} \] \[ (8.7.3) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,F) \to \prod_i \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E_i,F) \] is an equivalence of categories.

  4. d)

    Let \((e_i)_{i \in I}\) be a partition of the topos \(E\). For any topos morphism \(g : F \to E\), the family \((f_i)_{i \in I}\), with \(f_i = g^*(e_i)\), is a partition of \(F\), and the induced morphisms \(g_i : F_{/f_i} \to E_{/e_i}\) allow us to reconstitute \(g\) (up to a single isomorphism), the functor \(g^*\) identifying with the Cartesian product of the functors \(g_i^*\) (taking into account the equivalences of type (8.7.1)). In conclude a complete description of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E)\), in terms of categories of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F',E_i)\), where \(F'\) is a topos of the form \(F_{/f}\) (\(f\) direct summation of the final object of \(F\)) and \(E_i = E_{/e_i}\).

  5. e)

    In particular, if \(F\) is non-empty connected (cf. 4.3.5) i.e. if for any partition \((f_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(F\) there exists one \(i \in I\) and only one such that \(f_i \neq \varnothing_F\), prove that the canonical functor \[ (8.7.4) \qquad \coprod_{i \in I} \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E_i) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E) \] deduced from topos morphisms (8.7.2) is an equivalence of categories. More specifically, the family topos morphisms (8.7.2) induces an equivalence of categories \[ \coprod_{i \in I} \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) . \]

  6. f)

    A part \(I\) of the open set of \(E\) is called a reduced partition of \(E\) if the identical family \((e_i)_{i \in I}\) indexed by \(I\) is a partition, and if the \(e_i\) are \(\neq \varnothing_E\). Show that the refinement relation (I 4.3.2) between reduced partitions makes the set \(P\) reduced partitions a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small ordered set, such that the upper bound of two elements of \(P\) exists. We thus find a strict projective system \((I_p)_{p \in P}\), which will be considered as a pro-set and denoted \(\pi_o(E)\). We call it the pro-\(\pi_o\) of the topos \(E\).

  7. g)

    Prove that \(\pi_o(E)\) pro-represents the functor \[ T \mapsto f_* f^*(T) = \Gamma(E,T_E) : (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \] associated with the canonical morphism \(f : E \to P\) of \(E\) in the point topos \(P\) (4.3). In particular, so that this functor is representable, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\pi_o(E)\) be essentially constant, i.e. isomorphic (in as a pro-set) to an "ordinary" set, which will be noted again \(\pi_o(E)\). So that \(\pi_o(E)\) is reduced at one point, it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be "non-empty related". For \(\pi_o(E)\) to be empty, it is necessary and sufficient let \(E\) be the "empty topos" (2.2).

  8. h)

    Suppose \(E\) quasi-compact, i.e. that any overlap of its final object \(e\) admits a finite undercover. Show that then \(\pi_o(E)\) is a profinite set, and can be identified by consequence (by means of the well-known equivalence of categories between pro-objects of the category of sets finite, and totally discontinuous compact spaces) to a totally discontinuous compact space, which we will note also \(\pi_o(E)\).

  9. i)

    Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(\pi_o(X)\) the set of connected components of \(X\), considered a consistent pro-set. Define a canonical morphism of pro-sets \[ (8.7.5) \qquad \pi_o(X) \to \pi_o(\mathrm{Top}(X)) , \] or what amounts to the same thing, a canonical application \[ (8.7.6) \qquad \pi_o(X) \to \varprojlim \pi_o(\mathrm{Top}(X)) . \] Show that the first morphism is an isomorphism if and only if \(X\) is homeomorphic to a space sum of connected spaces (which is the case in particular if \(X\) is locally connected).

  10. j)

    Suppose \(X\) is a quasi-compact scheme. Prove that then (8.7.6) is bijective.

  11. k)

    Establish the "functional character" in \(X\) of the morphisms (8.7.5) and (8.7.6), in first specifying the meaning of this expression.

  12. l)

    (Compare 7.6 i)). Show that the following two conditions on the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) are equivalent: (i) The canonical morphism of \(E\) in the punctual topos is "essential" (exercise 7.6 a)) i.e. the functor \(I \mapsto I_E\) of \((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) in \(E\) commutes to products indexed by sets \(\in \mathcal{U}\). (ii) For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) has a \(\pi_o(E_{/X})\) which is an ordinary set, i.e. \(X\) is isomorphic to the sum of a family of related objects of \(E\). - We then say that \(E\) is a topos locally related (compare 2.7.5).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.8 (Dominant morphisms of topos)
  1. a)

    Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos morphism. Show equivalence of conditions following:

    1. (i)

      \(f_*(\varnothing_{E'}) = \varnothing_E\) (where \(\varnothing_E\), \(\varnothing_{E'}\) denote the initial objects of \(E,E'\)).

    2. (ii)

      For any object \(X\) of \(E\) which is "non-empty" i.e. not isomorphic to \(\varnothing_E\), \(f^*(X)\) is a non-empty object of \(E'\).

    3. (ii')

      Like (ii), with \(X\) a sub-object of the final object \(e_E\) of \(E\), i.e. \(X\) an open from the topos \(E\).

    We then say that the morphism \(f\) of topos is dominant.

  2. b)

    Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a continuous map of topological spaces, hence a topos morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(f) : \mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\) (4.1). Show that \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\) is dominant if and only if \(f\) is dominant, i.e. \(f(X')\) is dense in \(X\).

  3. c)

    Show that if \(f : E' \to E\) is a "conservative" morphism of topos (6.4.0), i.e. such that \(f^*\) is faithful, then \(f\) is dominant. Show that the converse is not necessarily true, even for a location morphism \(E_{/U} \to E\), where \(U\) is an open of the topos \(E\), (Show that in this case \(f\) is only conservative if \(U\) is the final object of \(E\) (so if \(f\) is an equivalence of topos), and use example b).)

  4. d)

    Let \(f : X' \to X\) be an arrow in a topos \(E\). We say that \(f\) is a morphism dominant in the topos \(E\) if the corresponding morphism of the induced topos \(E_{/X'} \to E_{/X}\) (5.5.2) is dominant. Show that this property only depends on the image \(f(X')\) of \(X'\) in \(X\), as an element of the ordered set \(\mathrm{ouv}(X)\) of sub-objects of \(X\). Show that the morphism \(E_{/X'} \to E_{/X}\) is conservative if and only if \(f\) is covering.
9. Sub-topos and topos joining
9.1. Subtopos and topos embedding

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.1.1
  1. a)

    A topos morphism \(f : F \to E\) is called an embedding if the functor \(f_* : F \to E\) is fully faithful (i.e. if the addition morphism \(f^* f_* \to \mathrm{id}_F\) is an isomorphism).

  2. b)

    We call sub-topos of a topos \(E\) a strictly full subcategory \(E'\) of \(E\) which is a topos and such that the inclusion functor \(\alpha : E' \to E\) is of the form \(i_*\), where \(i : E' \to E\) is a topos morphism (i.e. (3.1.1.) \(\alpha\) admits a left adjoint \(i^*\) which is exact on the left). The morphism \(i\) (which is determined up to a unique isomorphism) is then called the inclusion morphism of the subtopic \(E'\) in \(E\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.1.2
  1. a)

    It is clear that the inclusion morphism of a subtopos is an embedding, and that a subcategory \(E'\) of the topos \(E\) is a subtopos if and only if it is the essential image of a direct image functor \(f_*\) associated with a topos morphism \(g : F \to E\) which is an embedding.

  2. b)

    It immediately follows from the definitions that a topos morphism \(f : F \to E\) is a embedding if and only if it is isomorphic to a compound morphism \[ F \xrightarrow{\ g\ } E' \xrightarrow{\ i\ } E , \] where \(g\) is a topos equivalence and \(i\) the inclusion morphism of a sub-topos \(E'\) of \(E\). The latter is uniquely determined by previous conditions, such as the essential image of \(f_*\), and the previous factorization is also unique up to unique isomorphism. Of course, there is reason to practice of identifying, by means of \(g\), \(F\) in the subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\) (compare IV 3.4.1).

  3. c)

    An equivalence of topos (3.4.) \(u : E \approx E_1\) obviously defines a bijection between the set of subtopos of \(E\) and the set of subtopos of \(E_1\). Indeed, \(u_*\) being a equivalence of categories, establishes a bijection between the set of subcategories strictly full of \(E\) and the set of strictly full subcategories of \(E_1\) (to \(E' \subset E\) corresponding to the image essential \(E'_1\) of \(u_*|E'\)), and we immediately see that \(E'\) is a subtopos of \(E\) if and only if \(E'_1\) is a subtopic of \(E_1\). We can therefore, for the study of subtopos, return to the case where \(E\) is of the form \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a small site. In this case, it follows from II 5.5 that a strictly full subcategory \(E'\) of \(E\) is a subtopos if and only if the inclusion functor \(\alpha : E' \to E\) admits a left adjoint which is exact on the left (this therefore already implies that \(E'\) is a topos), and that all of these sub-topos are in one-to-one correspondence with the set of topologies \(T'\) on \(C\) which are finer than the topology given \(T\) of \(C\), by associating with any such \(T'\) the strictly full subcategory \((C, T')^{\sim}\) of \(\widetilde{C}\), formed beams for topology \(T'\). This shows at the same time, for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\): a subcategory strictly full \(E'\) of \(E\) is a subtopos if and only if the inclusion functor \(\alpha : E' \to E\) admits an assistant on the left who is exact on the left; the ordered set \(\mathscr{C}(E)\) of subtopics of \(E\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, and any part of \(\mathscr{C}(E)\) has an upper bound and a lower bound.

  4. d)

    If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopos of the topos \(E\), their intersection is a subtopos by \(E\). Indeed, as this intersection is strictly full, we are brought back to the case where \(E\) is of the form \(\widetilde{C}\). With the notations of b), \(E'\) and \(E''\) then correspond to two topologies \(T'\), \(T''\) on \(C\) finer than \(T\), and it suffices to see that then \(E' \cap E''\) is the category of bundles for the topology \(T'''\) terminal higher than \(T'\) and \(T''\). Let us indicate the demonstration of this fact (which should have appeared as a corollary after II 4.4 or II 5.5!). Let \(E'''\) be the category of sheaves for \(T'''\), obviously contained in \(E'\) and \(E''\) therefore in \(E' \cap E''\). Let, for any full subcategory \(D\) of \(E\), \(t(D)\) be the finest topology among those for which the elements of \(D\) are sheaves (II 2.2). The inclusions \(E''' \subset E' \cap E'' \subset E'\), \(E''\) obviously imply \(t(E'), t(E'') \le t(E' \cap E'') \le t(E''')\), on the other hand we have (II 4.4.1) \(t(E') = T'\), \(t(E'') = T''\), \(t(E''') = T'''\), so that the previous inequalities and the definition of \(T'''\) as bound higher imply that \(T''' = t(E' \cap E'')\), therefore \(E' \cap E'' \subset E'''\), cqfd. More generally, this argument shows that if \(T'\) is an upper bound topology of any family of topologies \((T_i)\), the category \(E'\) of beams for \(T'\) is the intersection of the categories \(E_i\) of beams for \(T_i\). We concludes in particular:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.1.3

Let \(E\) be a topos. Then for any family of subtopics \((E_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\), the intersection \(\bigcap_{i \in I} E_i\) is a subtopic of \(E\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.1.4

Let \(i : E' \to E\) be a topos embedding, and \(F\) be a topos. The functor \[ (9.1.4.1) \qquad f \mapsto i \circ f : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E) \] is fully faithful, and its essential image is formed from topos morphisms \(g : F \to E\) such that the essential image of \(g_*\) is contained in that of \(i_*\).

Let us indeed consider the obvious commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E') \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(F,E) \\ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,E') \ar[u] \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,E) \ar[u] } \] where the vertical arrows are the functors \(h \mapsto h_*\), which are fully faithful by definition of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}\) (IV 3.2.). On the other hand the second horizontal arrow \(G \mapsto i_* G\) is fully faithful, since \(i_*\) is, so the same goes for the first horizontal arrow. It remains to prove the characterization of the essential image of (9.1.4.1.). The necessity of the condition being evident by the formula \((if)_* = i_* f_*\), it remains to be proven that if the essential image of \(g_*\) is contained in that of \(i_*\), i.e. if we can write \(g_* = f_* i_*\), with \(f_* : F \to E'\) a functor, then \(g\) is in the essential image of (9.1.4.1.). He returns to even to say that \(f_*\) admits an adjoint on the left which is exact on the right. Now it is clear that \(f_*\) admits \(g_* i_*\) as left adjoint, and this functor is left exact, as composed of left exact functors \(g_*\) and \(i_*\), cqfd.
For a converse of 9.1.4, cf. exercise 9.1.6.
Taking for \(F\) the punctual topos (IV 2.2), we deduce in particular from 9.1.5. :

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.1.5

If \(f : E' \to E\) is a topos embedding morphism, the functor corresponding \[ (9.1.5.1.) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(f) : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \] is fully faithful.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.6 (Topos embeddings and 2-monomorphisms)

Let \(i : E' \to E\) be a topos morphism.

  1. a)

    If \(F\) and \(G\) are two topos above \(E'\), define (with the notations of 5.14 a)) a canonical functor \[ (9.1.6.1.) \qquad f \mapsto f \circ i : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{E'}(F, G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_E(F, G) . \]

  2. b)

    Suppose that \(i\), as 1-arrow of the 2-category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-\(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (3.3.2.), either a 2-monomorphism, i.e. such that for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F\) which is an element of \(\mathcal{V}\), the functor (9.1.4.1.) be fully faithful. Show that for any pair of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F\), \(G\) (not necessarily \(\in \mathcal{V}\)) the functor (9.1.6.1.) is fully faithful.

  3. c)

    Show that \(i\) is an embedding if and only if \(i\) is a 2-monomorphism. (For sufficiency, apply b) to induced topos \(E'_{/X'}\) and \(E'_{/Y'}\), and use 5.14. c)).

  4. d)

    Let \(g : F \to E\) be a topos morphism, and suppose that the fibered 2-product (5.11) \(F' = F \times^2_E E'\) exists (condition always verified, as shown by P. DELIGNE). Let \(j : F' \to F\) be projection morphism. Proving that \(j\) is an embedding, the same is true of \(j\). (Use c), and note that the stability of the notion of 2-monomorphism by 2-change of base is formal). In case \(E'\) is a subtopos of \(E\) and \(i : E' \to E\) is the canonical inclusion morphism, the essential image of \(F'\) by \(j_*\) (which is a subtopic of \(F\) which does not does not depend on indeterminacy in the construction of a 2-fibred product) is called the subtopos of \(F\) inverse image by \(g : F \to E\) from the subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\).

  5. e)

    With the notations at the end of d), let's choose the subtopos as the 2-fibred product of \(F\) inverse image of the subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\). Let \(X\) be an object of \(F\). Show that for \(X\) to belong to \(F'\), it must satisfy the following condition: for any object \(U\) of \(F\), designating by \(g_U : F_{/U} \to E\) the topos morphism induced by \(f\), we have \(g_{U*}(X|U) \in \operatorname{Ob} E'\), where \(X|U = (X \times U \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_2} U)\) is the "restriction from \(X\) to \(U\)". (NB: We can note that if \(i_U : F_{/U} \to F\) is the canonical inclusion, we have \(g_U = g \circ i_U\), hence \(g_{U*}(X|U) = g_*(i_{U*}(X|U)) = g_*(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(U,X))\) where \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(U,X)\) is the object defined in 10.1 below).

    Problem Conversely, is the previous condition on \(X\) sufficient for \(X\) belongs to \(F'\)? It amounts to the same thing to ask whether the strictly full subcategory of \(F\) formed by these objects \(X\) is a subtopic of \(F\)

  6. f)

    Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \(X' = i^*(X)\), and \(i_{/X} : E'_{/X'} \to E_{/X}\) the topos morphism induced by \(i\) (5.10.1). Show that if \(i\) is an embedding, it is the same for \(i_{/X}\). (Use d) and 5.11). Conversely, if \((X_\alpha)_\alpha\) is a family of objects covering the final object, and if for every \(\alpha\), \(i_{/X_\alpha}\) is an embedding, it is the same for \(i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.7 (Subtopos and multiplicative sets of arrows)
  1. a)

    Let \(i : E' \to E\) be a topos morphism, \(S\) the set of arrows \(u\) of \(E\) such that \(i^*(u)\) is an isomorphism, and consider the canonical functor induced by \(i^*\) (cf. [2, Chap I] or VI): \[ (9.1.7.1.) \qquad \rho : E[S^{-1}] \to E' . \] Show that \(i\) is an embedding if and only if the previous functor \(\rho\) is an equivalence of categories, and in this case \(S\) admits a calculation of fractions on the left and a calculation of fractions on the right. (Use [2, Chap I 1.3.]). In this case, the essential image of \(i_*\) is found from \(S\) as formed \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} E\) such that for all \(u : Y \to Y'\) in \(S\) \(\mathrm{Hom}(u,X) : \mathrm{Hom}(Y',X) \to \mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\) either bijective.

  2. b)

    We now assume that \(i\) is an embedding. Show that for all topos \(F\), the functor \[ f^* \mapsto f^* i^* : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E',F) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F) \] induces an equivalence between the category of \(f^*\) coming from topos morphisms \(f : F \to E'\) (i.e. exact on the left and admitting an adjoint on the right) and the category of functors \(g^* : E \to F\) coming from morphisms of topos \(g : F \to E\), and such that \(g^*\) transforms objects of \(S\) into isomorphisms.

  3. c)

    Let \(S\) be a part of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\), where \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. Show that \(S\) is associated with a subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\) by the method of a) if and only if \(S\) satisfies the following conditions:

    ST 1) \(S\) contains invertible arrows, is stable by composition and by change of base.

    ST 2) If a compound \(vu\) is in \(S\), then \(u \in S\) if and only if \(v \in S\).

    ST 3) If \(u : X \to Y\) is such that there exists a covering family \(Y_i \to Y\), \(i \in I\), such that \(u_i : X_i = X \times_Y Y_i \to Y_i\) is in \(S\) for all \(i \in I\), so \(u \in S\). Show that we thus obtain a one-to-one correspondence between the set of subtopics \(E'\) of \(E\), and the set of parts \(S\) of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) satisfying conditions ST 1), ST 2), ST 3). (\(S\) given, associate a finer \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology \(T'\) that the canonical topology \(T\) of \(E\), whose covering families \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) are those for which the inclusion \(X'_i \hookrightarrow X\) of the image of \(X_i\) is \(\in S\). Show that the category of sheaves for \(T'\) is equivalent to a subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\), and use II 4.4. to prove that it gives \(S\).

  4. d)

    Show that \(S\) is known when we know the part \(S_o\) of \(S\) formed by monomorphisms. Show that the application \(S \mapsto S_o\) establishes a bijection between the set of parts \(S\) of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) satisfying the conditions ST 1) to ST 3) of c), and all the parts of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) formed of monomorphisms and satisfying the same conditions ST 1) to ST 3).

  5. e)

    Let \((E'_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of subtopos of \(E\), and \((S_i)_{i \in I}\) be the family of corresponding parts of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\). Show that \(\bigcap_{i \in I} S_i\) satisfies conditions ST 1) to ST 3) of c), therefore corresponds to a subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\), and that the latter is the subtopos generated by the \(E_i\), i.e. the upper bound (see 9.1.2. c)) of the sub-topos \(E'_i\).

  6. f)

    Let \(A\) be a part of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\). Show that there exists a smaller part \(S\) of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) among those containing \(A\) and satisfying conditions ST 1) to ST 3), and that \(S\) corresponds to the largest subtopis \(E'\) of \(E\) such that the corresponding multiplicative part of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) contains \(S\). Show that \(S\) is equal to the union of the parts \(A_n\) (\(n \ge 0\)) of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\), constructed by induction in the following way: \(A_o = A\), \(A_{n+1}\) is formed by arrows \(u : X \to Y\) which are of one of the following three types (i) to (iv):

    (i) \(u\) is invertible, or the compound of two arrows of \(A_n\), or deduced by change of base with an arrow of \(A_n\).

    (ii) \(u\) is one of the factors of a compound arrow whose other factor as well as the compound are in \(A_n\).

    (iii) \(u\) is a sum of a small family of arrows \(\in A_n\).
