    (iv) There exists an epimorphism \(Y' \to Y\), such that \(u' : X' = X \times_Y Y' \to Y'\) deduced from \(u\) by base change either in \(A_n\).

  7. g)

    With the notations of e), let \(A\) be the union of \(S_i\), \(S\) the part of \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\) deduced from \(A\) by the method of f), and \(E'\) the corresponding subtopos of \(E\). Show that \(E'\) is the intersection (= lower bound) of the subtopics \(E'_i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.7.2 (Canonical factorization of a topos morphism)

Either \[ f : F \to E \] a topos morphism. Show that we can find a factorization of \(f\), up to isomorphism, \[ (9.1.7.3.) \qquad F \xrightarrow{\ f'\ } E' \xrightarrow{\ i\ } E , \] where \(f'\) is conservative (i.e. (6.4.0.) \(f'^*\) is conservative, or even faithful) and where \(i\) is an embedding, and that this factorization is unique up to equivalence (see 9.1.4.). To do this, introduce the whole \(S_f \subset \mathrm{Fl}\, E\) of the arrows \(u\) of \(E\) such that \(f^*(u)\) is an isomorphism, prove that \(S_f\) satisfies the conditions of 9.1.7. c), take for \(E'\) the corresponding subtopos of \(E\), and define \(f'^*\) by (9.1.7.1.), which corresponds to a topos morphism \(f'\) thanks to 9.1.7 b).

  1. b)

    The subtopos of \(E\) defined by the embedding \(i\) is called the subtopos of \(E\) image of toposmorphism \(f\). Show that it is the smallest of the subtopics \(E_1\) of \(E\) such that \(f\) is factors, up to isomorphism, through \(E_1\), i.e. (9.1.4.) the smallest of the subtopos of \(E\) containing the image of \(f_*\) (or again, the essential image of \(f_*\)). For \(f\) to be an embedding, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) induces an equivalence \(f'\) of \(E\) with its image; for \(f\) to be conservative, it is necessary and sufficient that its image is equal to \(E\) integer.

  2. c)

    Suppose that \(f = \mathrm{Top}(f_o)\) is associated with a continuous application of spaces sober topological \(f_o : Y \to X\) (4.1.) Consider the canonical factorization of \(f_o\) in \[ Y \xrightarrow{\ f'_o\ } X' = f_o(Y) \xrightarrow{\ i_o\ } X , \] where \(i_o\) is the inclusion. Show that the canonical factorization of \(f\) is then identified with the factorization corresponding \[ \mathrm{Top}(Y) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Top}(f'_o)} \mathrm{Top}(X') \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Top}(i_o)} \mathrm{Top}(X) . \] Let \(Z\) be the smallest sober subspace of \(X\) containing \(X' = f(Y)\), i.e. the set of points \(x\) of \(X\) such that \(\overline{\{x\}} \cap X'\) is dense in \(\overline{\{x\}}\). (Note that \(Z = X'\) if the points of \(X\) are closed, or if \(X'\) is a constructible part of \(X\) assumed to be locally Noetherian). Then \(f\) is conservative, i.e. the subtopos of \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\) image of \(F = \mathrm{Top}(Y)\) by \(f = \mathrm{Top}(f_o)\) is equal to \(E\) itself, if and only if \(Z = X\), i.e. if and only if \(f(Y)\) is very dense (EGA IV 10.13) in \(X\). This specifies to what extent it is lawful to look at the notion of conservative morphism of topos as the natural generalization of the notion continuous surjective application of topological spaces.

  3. d)

    Either \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[d] & Y' \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ X & X' \ar[l] } \] a Cartesian diagram of topological spaces. If \(Y\) and \(X'\) are discrete spaces, then \(Y'\) discrete, then the corresponding topos diagram is 2-Cartesian (5.11). (NB. For a counter-example when we do not assumes more discrete \(Y\), \(X'\), \(Y\), \(X'\) being subspaces of the separate space \(X\), cf. 9.1.10. c)). Deduce an example of a 2-Cartesian topos diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[d]_-{f} & F' \ar[l] \ar[d]^-{f'} \\ E & E' \ar[l] } \] such that \(f\) is conservative, but \(f'\) non-conservative, more precisely \(E'\) = punctual topos, \(F'\) = empty topos (2.2). (Take \(Y\) such that the image of \(Y\) in \(X\) is very dense and \(\neq X\), and take \(X'\) reduced to a point which is not in the picture).

  4. e)

    Let \((E_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of subtopos of a topos \(E\). Prove that \(E\) is the sup of \(E_i\) if and only if the family of embedding morphisms \(E_i \to E\) is conservative (6.4.0).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.8 (Subtopos and points of E. Case of topological spaces)

Let \(E\) be a topos.

  1. a)

    Let \(Z\) be a subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) (or what amounts to the same thing: a part of \(\operatorname{Ob} \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\)). Show that the set \(S(Z)\) of arrows of \(E\) which are transformed into bijections by the fiber functors corresponding to \(z \in \operatorname{Ob} Z\) satisfies the conditions of 9.1.7. c), and therefore defines a subtopic \(E_Z\) of \(E\). Show that this admits enough points, and that \(Z\) is equivalent to a full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E_Z)\). Show that we can obtain by the preceding process any subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) which admits enough points. (Take for \(Z\) the essential image of the functor (9.1.5.1.) ). Show that we obtain a bijection between the set of subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) admitting sufficient points, and a subset of the set of strictly full subcategories \(Z\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) which are stable by direct factors (I 10.) and by small filtering projective limits (cf. 7.3.). (Problem: What are the subcategories of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) that we find like this? It is essentially, in another form, the problem already raised in 6.5.3., taking into account dictionary 9.1.2.c). Note that in addition to the conditions necessary indicated just now, there are also more subtle conditions on the topological nature of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), gender, sobriety, cf. e).

  2. b)

    Let \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of subcategories full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), and let \(Z\) be the subcategory full meeting of \(Z_i\). Show that the corresponding subtopos \(E_Z\) defined in a) is the subtopic of \(E\) generated by \(E_{Z_i}\). (Use 9.1.7.e) ). Conclude that the subtopos of \(E\) generated by a family of subtopics having sufficient points, also has sufficient points.

  3. c)

    Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos embedding. Show that the application \(U \mapsto f^*(U)\) \[ (9.1.8.1.) \qquad \mathrm{Ouv}(E) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(E') \] is surjective, and conclude that the application \[ (9.1.8.2.) \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \] induced by \(f\) on the isomorphy classes of points induces a homeomorphism of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E')\) on a subspace of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), for the topologies defined in 7.8. has). (For injectivity, use 9.1.5.).

  4. d)

    Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a continuous map, hence a topos morphism (4.1.) \[ \mathrm{Top}(f) : \mathrm{Top}(X') \to \mathrm{Top}(X) . \] Prove that the latter is an embedding if and only if the topology of \(X'\) is inverse image by \(f\) of that of \(X\) (i.e., when \(X'\) is a Kolmogoroff space, for example a sober space, if and only if \(f\) induces a homeomorphism of \(X'\) on a subspace of \(X\)). (For necessity, use c), for sufficiency, 9.1.7.2. c) ).

  5. e)

    Let \(X\) be a sober topological space, and \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\). Using a) and d), establish a isomorphism of ordered sets \(X' \mapsto E_{X'}\), between the set of sober subspaces \(X'\) of \(X\) (i.e., when \(X\) is separated, between the set of all parts of \(X\) and the set of subtopos of \(E\) having enough points. Show that \(E_{X'}\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X')\). If \((X'_i)_{i \in I}\) is a family of sober subspaces of \(X\), show that there exists a smaller sober subspace \(X'\) of \(X\) containing the \(X'_i\), equal to the union of \(X'_i\) if \(I\) is finite or \(X\) is separated, and \(E_{X'}\) is the subtopos of \(E\) generated by the \(E_{X'_i}\). (Use b)).

  6. f)

    Give an example of a topos \(E\) of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), and of a sub-topos \(E'\) which does not have not enough points, and even which is non-empty (2.2.) without points. (Take \(E\) of the form \(\widehat{\underline{U}}\), \(E' = \underline{U}^{\sim}\), where \(U\) is the ordered set defined in 7.4. Or better, take the example of 9.1.10. b), which shows that we can take for \(X\) any non-empty compact space without isolated point.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.9 (Case of topos defined by an ordered set)
  1. a)

    We use the yoga of 7.8. b) and c), giving a dictionary between, on the one hand, the topos generated by their open and the morphisms of such topos, on the other hand the ordered sets having arbitrary Sup, finite Inf and where the Inf of two elements is distributive with respect to the Sup arbitrary, the morphisms between such ordered sets being the increasing applications switching to finite Inf and arbitrary Sup.

  2. b)

    Let \(E\) be a topos, \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos embedding. Show that for all generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) in \(E\), the family \((f^*(X_i))_{i \in I}\) is generating. Deduce that if \(E\) is generated by its open, it is the same for \(E'\) (Use 9.1.8. c) ).

  3. c)

    Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a morphism of topos generated by their open ones, associated with a morphism \(g : \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}'\) on the corresponding ordered sets. Show that \(f\) is an embedding if and only if \(g\) is surjective, and \(g\) is surjective on the graphs of order relations, i.e. the order of \(\mathcal{O}'\) is quotient of that of \(\mathcal{O}\).

  4. d)

    Let \(E\) be a topos of the form \(\mathcal{O}^{\sim}\) as in a). Deduce from b) and c) a one-to-one correspondence between the set of subtopics of \(E\), and the set of equivalence relations \(R\) in \(\mathcal{O}\) having the following properties, analogous to conditions ST 1) to ST 3) of 9.1.7 c):

    QO 1) The relation \(R\) is compatible with finite Inf, or again: if \(U, U'\) are equivalent by \(R\), the same is true of \(U \cap V\) and \(U' \cap V\) for all \(V \in \mathcal{O}\).

    QO 2) The relation \(R\) is compatible with any Sup, or again: if \(U_i\) is equivalent to \(U'_i\) for all \(i \in I\), then \(\mathrm{Sup}_i U_i\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Sup}_i U'_i\).

    Show that if subtopics \(E'_i\) of \(E\) correspond to the equivalence relations \(R_i\), then the subtopos generated by \(E'_i\) corresponds to the intersection relation of \(R_i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.10 (Intersection of subspaces; new topos without points; non-existence of the complement of a subtopos)

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X) = \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\) (2.1).

  1. a)

    Let \(R\) be the following relation on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\): \((U, U') \in R \iff U \cap U'\) is dense in \(U\) and in \(U'\). Show that \(R\) is an equivalence relation satisfying the conditions QO 1) and QO 2) of 9.1.9. d), and therefore defines a subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\). Show that for a point \(x \in X\), the corresponding point is in the essential image of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E')\) if and only if \(x\) is thick i.e. belongs to the adhesion of inside \(\overline{\{x\}}\). Show that \(E'\) is the empty topos (2.2.) if and only if \(X\) is empty.

  2. b)

    If \(X\) is sober, show that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E')\) is equivalent to the category defined by the ordered subset of \(X\) (for the specialization relation) defined by the thick points of \(X\). Show that if the points of \(X\) are closed (resp. if \(X\) is Noetherian) then the thick points of \(X\) are the isolated points of \(X\) i.e. such that \(\{x\}\) is open (resp. are the maximum points of \(X\)). Conclude that if \(X\) is a separate non-empty topological space without isolated points, then the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\) is a non-"empty" topos which has no points.

  3. c)

    Let \(X'\) be a part of \(X\), and \(E_{X'}\) be the subtopos of \(E\) associated with \(X'\) by the process 9.1.8. has). Show that if \(X'\) is dense, then \(E_{X'}\) contains \(E'\). Show that if \(X\) is sober and if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are two sober parts of \(X\), their intersection \(X' \cap X''\) is sober and \(E_{X' \cap X''} \subset E_{X'} \cap E_{X''}\), and that the inclusion can be strict(∗). (Take a separate non-empty space \(X\) admitting two parts dense disjoint \(X'\), \(X''\)). Show that the previous inclusion is an equality if and only if the subtopos \(E_{X'} \cap E_{X''}\) has enough points. Show that this is so if \(X'\) or \(X''\) is a locally closed part. by \(X\).

  4. d)

    Let \(X'\) be a part of \(X\), and \(X''\) be the complement of \(X'\). A subtopic \(F\) of \(E\) contains the \(E_x\) for \(x \in X''\) if and only if it contains \(E_{X''}\) (cf. 9.1.8. b) ). Conclude that if \(X''\) is union of locally closed parts of \(X\) (for example if the points of \(X''\) are closed), then the whole subtopos \(F\) of \(E\) whose intersection with \(E_{X'}\) is the empty subtopos \(\mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) does not contain a plus large element (which then deserves the sub-topos name of \(E\) weak complement of \(E_{X'}\), cf. 9.1.13.), only if \(E_{X'} \cap E_{X''}\) is the empty subtopos (condition which is not satisfied if \(X \neq \varnothing\) and if \(X'\) and \(X''\) are both dense in \(X\), \(X, X', X''\) being sober, cf. c) ). Show that when this condition is in default, then in the ordered set of subtopos of \(E\), the Inf of two elements is not distributive by compared to any Sup. (The editor does not know whether it is distributive with respect to finite Sups; cf. however 9.1.11. e) and 9.1.12. d) ).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.11 (Subtopos of locally Noetherian topos. Case of Noetherian spaces)
  1. a)

    Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos embedding. Show that if \(X\) is an object pre-noetherian of \(E\) (i.e. (VI 1.) any increasing sequence of sub-objects of \(X\) is stationary) then \(X' = f^*(X)\) is a pre-Noetherian object of \(E'\). (Introducing the induced topos \(E'_{/X}\), and \(E_{/X}\), and using 9.1.6. e), first reduce to the case where \(X\) is the final object \(e\) of \(E\), therefore \(X'\) is the final object \(e'\) of \(E'\). Note then that any increasing sequence of sub-objects of \(e'\) defines, by application of \(f_*\), an increasing sequence of subobjects of \(f_*(e') = e\) ). Conclude that if \(E\) admits a generating family made up of objects pre-Noetherian (resp. if \(E\) is a Noetherian topos, resp. locally Noetherian (VI 2.)) then \(E'\) satisfies the same condition. (Use 9.1.9. b) ).

  2. b)

    Let \(E\) be a locally Noetherian topos (VI 2.). Show that any subtopic of \(E\) is of the form \(E_Z\) (9.1.8. a)), for a suitable full subcategory \(Z\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\). (Use a) and the DELIGNE's theorem that any locally Noetherian topos admits sufficient points). Conclude that if \(E\) is of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), where \(X\) is a locally Noetherian sober topological space, then \(Z \mapsto E_Z\) is an isomorphism of the ordered set of sober subspaces of \(X\), with the ordered set of subtopic of \(X\). (Use 9.1.8.e) ). The intersection of sober subspaces \(Z_i\) (is necessarily sober and) defines the intersection of subtopics \(E_{Z_i}\) (unlike what can happen in the general case (9.1.10. c)).

  3. c)

    Let \(X\) be a sober locally Noetherian space, \(X'\) a part of \(X\), \(X''\) its complementary. Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

    (i) \(X'\) is constructible.

    (ii) \(X'\) and \(X''\) are sober.

    (iii) \(X'\) is sober, and the subtopos \(E_{X'} \approx \mathrm{Top}(X')\) of \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\) admits a subtopos "complementary" (see 9.1.13. e)).

    (iv) The intersection of the subtopics \(E_{X'} \approx \mathrm{Top}(X')\) and \(E_{X''} \approx \mathrm{Top}(X'')\) of \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\) is the "empty" subtopic of \(E\).

    Show that if \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopics of \(E\), they are weak complements if and only if they are complementary (9.1.13. c) ).

  4. d)

    Let \(X\) be a sober Noetherian space. Show equivalence of conditions following:

    (i) Any sober subspace \(X'\) of \(X\) is constructible, i.e. (c) ) its complement is sober.

    (i bis) For any maximal point \(x\) of \(X\), \(X - \{x\}\) is sober, i.e. \(\{x\}\) is constructible.

    (ii) \(X\) is finished.

    (iii) Any subtopos of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) admits a complementary subtopos (see 9.1.13.).

    (iv) In the ordered set of subtopos of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), the Inf of two elements is distributive by compared to any Sup.

    (Use the argument of 9.1.10. d), thanks to the intersection formula proven in b) ).

  5. e)

    Let \(X\) be a locally Noetherian space. Show that in the ordered set of subtopos of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), the Inf of two elements is distributive with respect to the finite Sups. (Return to case \(X\) sober, and use b) ).

  6. f)

    For any sober subspace \(Z\) of the locally Noetherian sober space \(X\), let \(T_Z\) the topology on the category \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) of the open ones of \(X\) for which a family of inclusions \(U_i \to U\) is covering if and only if we have \(U \cap Z = \bigcup_i U_i \cap Z\). Show that \(Z \mapsto T_Z\) establishes a bijection (reversing the inclusion relations) between the set of sober subspaces \(Z\) of \(X\), and the set of topologies on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) finer than the canonical topology \(T_X\). (Use b) and 9.1.2. c) ).

  7. g)

    Let \(X\) be a sober topological space. A part \(X'\) of \(X\) is sober if and only if its complement is a meeting of locally closed parts; if \(X\) is locally Noetherian, this also means that \(X'\) is "probuildable" (EGA IV 1.9.4). Show that there exists on \(X\) a topology \(T_{\mathrm{cons}'}\), such that the closed parts of \(X_{\mathrm{cons}'} = (X, T_{\mathrm{cons}'})\) are the sober parts of \(X\). Let us now suppose \(X\) locally Noetherian, \(X_{\mathrm{cons}'}\) is therefore none other than the space \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\) of EGA IV 9.1.13. Then deduce from b) and c) that the ordered set \(\Sigma\) of the subtopos of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is isomorphic to the ordered set of closed parts of \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\), and in this correspondence, the subtopos admitting a complement corresponds to both open and closed parts of \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\). Show that \(X\) is locally compact, and that it is compact if and only if \(X\) is Noetherian i.e. the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(E)\) is Noetherian.

  8. h)

    Let \(E\) be a locally Noetherian topos. Suppose he has the intended property in 9.1.14. b) (condition may always be met...). Show that the essential images of the functors canonical \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E') \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\), where \(E'\) traverses the set \(\Sigma\) of subtopics of \(E\), defined in the set \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) of point isomorphy classes of \(E\) a set of parts, which is the set closed parts for a topology \(T_{\mathrm{cons}}\) on \(X\), and that in this way we obtain an isomorphism of ordered sets between the set \(\Sigma\) of the subtopos of \(E\) and the set of closed parts of \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\). Conclude that in \(\Sigma\), the Sup of two elements is distributive with respect to the any Inf. Show that \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\), which is not necessarily \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, has an associated sober space (4.2.1.) which is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.12 (Finished Topos)

A topos \(E\) is said to be finite if it is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), with \(C\) a category finished.

  1. a)

    (Dictionary). Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\). Let \((\mathrm{Karfiness})\) be the 2-category defined as 2-full subcategory of the category \((\mathrm{Cat})_{\mathcal{V}}\), formed from the element categories of \(\mathcal{V}\), which are Karobian and equivalent to a finite category and \((\mathrm{Topfin})\) the 2-defined category like the 2-full subcategory of \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Top})\) (3.3.1.) formed from the finite \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos which are \(\in \mathcal{V}\). Show that we have 2-equivalences that are quasi-inverse of each other: \[ (9.1.12.1.) \qquad C \mapsto \widehat{C} : (\mathrm{Karfiness}) \to (\mathrm{Topfin}) \] \[ (9.1.12.2.) \qquad E \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) : (\mathrm{Topfin}) \to (\mathrm{Karfiness}) . \] (Use 7.6. h), e) ).

  2. b)

    Show that a finite topos is Noetherian (VI 2).

  3. c)

    Show that every subtopos of a finite topos \(E\) is a finite topos. More precisely, if \(E\) is equivalent to \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a finite Karoubian category (or, more generally, equivalent to a finite category), show that we obtain an ordered isomorphism between the set of subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) and the set of strictly full subcategories \(C'\) of \(C\) which are Karoubian (or, what amounts to even, stable in \(C\) by direct factors), by associating with all \(C'\) the essential image of \(f : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C}\), where \(f : C' \to C\) is the inclusion functor. (Use 5.6. to ensure that \(f_*\) is fully faithful, and b) and 9.1.11. b) for the fact that any subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\) is obtained as indicated).

  4. d)

    Let \(E\) be a finite topos. Build on the finite set \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) of classes of isomorphy of points of \(E\) a topology which makes it a sober topological space, and such that the set ordered subtopics of \(E\) is canonically isomorphic to the set of closed parts of \(X\). (Take the "constructible topology" \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathrm{cons}}\) of \(X\), defined via the order relation \((x \le y) \iff (x \text{ est un facteur direct de } y)\), for which the adhesion of a point \(x\) is formed of \(y\) such that \(y \le x\) ). In particular the set \(\Sigma\) of the subtopos of \(E\) is finite, and the Inf there is distributive with respect to to any Sup.

  5. e)

    Let \(C\) be a category equivalent to a finite category. Show that for all full subcategory \(C'\) of \(C\), there exists on \(C\) a topology \(T_{C'}\) for which a family \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) is covering if and only if for all \(Y \in \operatorname{Ob} C'\), the family of applications \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\) (\(i \in I\)) is surjective. Show that the category of sheaves on \((C,T_{C'})\) is equivalent to \(\widehat{C}'\), and that \(C' \mapsto T_{C'}\) is a bijection (reversing the order relations) between the set strictly full subcategories \(C'\) of \(C\) which are Karoubian (or, which amounts to the same thing, stable in \(C\) by direct factors), and the set of topologies (II 1.1) on the category \(C\). (Use c) and 9.1.2. c) ). In particular, if \(C\) is a site whose underlying category is equivalent to a category finite, then the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) is a finite topos.

  6. f)

    Let \(f : C' \to C\) be a functor of \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, with \(C\) equivalent to a category finished. Show that the topos morphism \(\widehat{f} : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C}\) (4.6.) is conservative if and only if every object of \(C\) is isomorphic to a direct factor of an object in the image of \(f\). (Reduce to the case where \(f\) is a inclusion \(f : C' \to C\), with \(C'\) as in c) ).

  7. g)

    Let \(C\) be the category having two objects \(e\) (the final object) and \(a\), and, in addition to the arrows identical, three arrows \(f : a \to e\), \(g : e \to a\), \(p = gf\) (so \(p^2 = p\) ). Show that \(E = \widehat{C}\) has, in addition to subtopos \(E\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\), exactly one subtopos \(E'\), namely \(E' = \{e\}^{\wedge}\); consequently, for everything subtopic \(E''\) of \(E\), \(E'' \neq \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\), we have \(E' \cap E'' \neq \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.1.13 (Sub-topos complementary to a topos)

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(E'\) and \(E''\) two sub-topos. We say that \(E'\) and \(E''\) are complementary from each other if \(E' \cap E'' = \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\), \(E' \vee E'' = E\). (Where the sign \(\vee\) designates the Sup in the whole ordered subtopics of \(E\) ).

We will assume that in the set \(\Sigma\) of the subtopos of \(E\), the Inf of two elements is distributive compared to the finished Sup.

  1. a)

    If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are complementary, \(E''\) is the largest among the subtopics \(E''_1\) such that \(E' \cap E''_1 = \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) (i.e. \(E''\) is a weak complement of \(E'\), in the terminology of 9.1.10. d) ).

  2. b)

    Show that the following conditions on \(E\) are equivalent:

    (i) If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopics of \(E\) such that \(E''\) is a weak complement of \(E'\), then \(E''\) is a complement to \(E'\).

    (ii) For any subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) such that \(E' \neq E\), there exists a subtopos \(E''\) of \(E\) such that \(E'' \neq \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) and \(E' \cap E'' = \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\).

    (i bis) If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopics of \(E\) such that \(E''\) is maximal in the set of subtopics \(E''_1\) such as \(E' \cap E''_1 = \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\), then \(E''\) is a complement of \(E\).

    Show that even if \(E\) is a finite topos (9.1.12.), these conditions are not necessarily verified (9.1.12. g) ). They are, however, if \(E\) is of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), \(X\) a space locally noetherian. (Use 9.1.11. b) ).

  3. c)

    A subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) is called complemented if it admits a complement (compare 9.1.12. c) ). Prove that the set of complemented subtopics is stable by finite Inf and Sup, and by complementarity, and that the latter transforms Inf and Sup, Sup into Inf.

  4. d)

    State the duals of observations a), b), c) (which were in fact statements trivial generals on an abstract ordered set \(\Sigma\); note that the hypothesis made on \(\Sigma\) is in fact autodual).
9.1.14. Open questions.
Let \(E\) be a topos.
a) In all the subtopos of \(E\), is the Inf of two elements distributive with respect to the sup finished?
b) Let \(E'\), \(E''\) be two subtopics of \(E\) such that \(E = E' \vee E''\). So is it true that every point of \(E\) is isomorphic to the image of a point of \(E'\) or a point of \(E''\)?

Note that if the answer to b) is affirmative for \(E\) and all its subtopics, then the answer to a) is affirmative at least for the case of sub-topos having sufficient points (therefore for all the subtopos, if \(E\) is locally Noetherian (9.1.11. b) ). The answer to a) (and, a fortiori to b)), is not known however for all Noetherian topos. However the answer to a) and b) is affirmative if \(E\) is form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), \(X\) is locally Noetherian space (9.1.11.), or if \(E\) is a finite topos (9.1.12.).
Note that a) can be reformulated in the following form (applied to all the subtopics \(F\) of \(E\) ):

a') If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopos of a topos \(F\), such as the canonical morphism (cf. 8.7. b)) \[ (9.1.14.1.) \qquad E' \sqcup E'' \to F \] is conservative (which also means \(E = E' \vee E''\) (9.1.7.2. e) ), then it is the same for the morphism inferred by 2-base change \(F_1 \to F\), where \(F_1\) is a subtopos of \(F\).

We have an analogous reformulation b') of b), taking a change of base \(F_1 \to F\) by a topos punctual \(F_1\) (2.2.). We therefore see that an affirmative answer to a), b) would result from an answer affirmative to the following question (where we take for \(F\) any subtopos of \(E\)):

c) If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are two subtopos of the topos \(F\), the sup of which is \(F\) i.e. such that the morphism of topos (9.1.14.1.) is conservative, then is this morphism even universally conservative, i.e. does it remain conservative after any 2-base change \(F_1 \to F\)?
This statement makes sense, thanks to the result of DELIGNE ( ) affirming the existence of 2-fibrated-products of topos. However, see example 9.1.7.2. d).
On the other hand, we see by 9.1.11. h) that an affirmative answer to b) would provide in the case locally Noetherian an affirmative answer to the following question:
d) In the ordered set \(\Sigma\) of the subtopos of \(E\), is the Sup of two elements distributive by in relation to any Inf (not necessarily finished)?

This also means that \(\Sigma\) is interpreted as the ordered set of "closed subtopics" of a suitable topos \(E_{\mathrm{cons}'}\) (namely \(\Sigma^{\circ\sim}\), where the opposite ordered set \(\Sigma^\circ\) of \(\Sigma\), considered as a category, is provided with its canonical topology, cf. 7.8.b) ), which is generated by the sub-objects of the final object, therefore, is very close to an ordinary topological space. It remains to study this topos \(E_{\mathrm{cons}'}\), drawing inspiration from examples 9.1.11. g) and 9.1.12. d), and in particular to determine whether it is Noetherian if \(E\) is, which amounts to the following question for the subtopics \(F\) of \(E\), which has meaning independently of d):

e) Let \(F\) be a Noetherian topos, \((E_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of sub-topos of \(E\) whose intersection is \(\mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\). So does there exist a finite subfamily with the same property?
On a rather different note, let us also recall the question raised in (9.1.6 e)), which deserves to be clarified.
9.2. Case of open subtopics.

Let \(U\) be an open of a topos \(E\), i.e. a sub-object of the final object \(e\) of \(E\) (8.3). Let's consider the location morphism (5.2) \[ (9.2.2) \qquad j : E_{/U} \to E . \] As \(U \to e\) is here a monomorphism, the functor \(j_!\) (which is interpreted as the forget functor) is fully faithful, so its biadjoint \(j_*\) is also faithful (1.5.7 a)). In other words, the morphism of location \(j\) (9.2.2) associated with an open \(U\) of the topos \(E\) is an embedding of topos (9.1.1 a)).

A topos embedding \(F \to E\) is called an open embedding if it is isomorphic to the embedding of topos defined by an open \(U\) of \(E\). A subtopos \(E'\) (9.1.1. b)) of \(E\) is called an open subtopos if it is defined using an open of \(E\), i.e. if the canonical inclusion morphism \(E' \to E\) is an embedding open. Note that the open \(U\) of \(E\) associated with an open embedding of topos \(j : E' \to E\) is determined from uniquely as equal to the subobject \(j_!(e')\) of \(e\), where \(e'\) denotes the final object of \(E'\). Subsequently, we find a one-to-one correspondence between open \(U\) of \(E\) and open subtopics of \(E\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.2.3

Consistent with the previous definitions, the open subtopic of \(E\) associated in the open \(U\) of \(E\) is the strictly full subcategory of \(E\) essential image of the direct image functor \[ j_* : E_{/U} \to E \] We will be careful not to confuse this subcategory of \(E\) with the strictly full subcategory, image essential of \(j_!\), formed of the objects \(X\) of \(E\) such that the structural morphism \(X \to e\) is factorized by \(U\). He is clear, however, that these two subcategories mutually determine each other, and that they are canonically equivalent. This is why certain authors may have been tempted to call (or even call) "sub-topos open defined by \(U\)" the strictly full essential image subcategory of \(j_!\), whose description in terms of \(U\) is simpler than that of the essential image of \(j_*\). This terminology does not present inconvenient as long as we do not propose to study other kinds of subtopos than subtopos open. As this is not our case, we will not follow the above-mentioned authors here.
For the convenience of the reader, we will summarize the most important special properties of open embeddings:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.2.4

Either \[ j : \mathscr{U} \to E \] an open embedding of topos (9.2.1). Then the functor \(j_!\) adjoint to the left of \(j^*\) exists, so that we have a sequence of three adjoint functors \[ j_! , \quad j^* , \quad j_* : \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathscr{U} \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{j_!} \ar@<-1ex>[r]_-{j_*} & E \ar[l]_-{j^*} } \] Additionally:

  1. a)

    The functor \(j_!\) and the functor \(j_*\) are fully faithful.

  2. b)

    The functor \(j_!\) commutes to fibered products, to products indexed by small sets \(I \neq \varnothing\), and to projective limits relating to small categories of co-filtering indices (1.2.7).

  3. c)

    For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the addition morphism \[ j_! j^*(X) \to X \] is a monomorphism.

Proof. We can assume that \(j\) is the location morphism defined by an open \(U\) from \(E\). Then a) is put for memory, b) comes from the interpretation of \(j_!\) as a forgetting functor and the makes \(U \to e\) a monomorphism. Finally c) is immediate, noting that the morphism envisaged is not other than the morphism deduced from the inclusion \(U \to e\) by the change of base \(X \to e\), taking into account that by change of base a monomorphism is transformed into a monomorphism.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 9.2.4.1

We can find topos embeddings \(j : E' \to E\) such that \(j_!\) exists, but that \(j_!\) does not commute to the products of two objects, nor to co-filtering projective limits (exercise 9.5.9 c)).

Let it be now \[ g : E' \to E \] any topos morphism, and let \(U' = g^*(U)\) be the inverse image of the open \(U\) of \(E\). We saw in 5.11 that the topos diagram \[ \xymatrix{ E'_{/U'} \ar[r]^-{j'} \ar[d]_-{g_U} & E' \ar[d]^-{g} \\ E_{/U} \ar[r]_-{j} & E } \] is 2-Cartesian. We conclude in particular that the inverse image by \(g\) of an open subtopos of \(E\) (cf. 9.1.6 d)) is an open subtopos of \(E'\), or what amounts to the same thing, as the notion of open embedding of topos (9.2.1) is stable by 2-basis changes in the 2-category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (elements of a universe given \(\mathcal{V}\)).

Let us also note that for the morphism of topos \(g : E' \to E\) to be factorized, up to isomorphism, by \(j : E_{/U} \to E\), it is obviously necessary and sufficient that \(j' : E'_{/U'} \to E'\) is an equivalence of categories, which still means that the inclusion \(U' \to e'\) (=final object of \(E'\)) is an isomorphism. This therefore specifies 9.1.4, in characterizing in a simple way the essential image of the functor envisaged in loc. cit.

Let's apply 9.2.5 to the case where \(E'\) is of the form \(E_{/V}\), where \(V\) is an open second of \(E\), \(g\) being the location morphism. Then \(U' = U \cap V\), and we find that the 2-Cartesian product of \(E_{/U}\) and \(E_{/V}\) on \(E\) identifies as \(E_{/U \cap V}\). We conclude that a finite intersection of open subtopos of \(E\) is a subtopos open from \(E\), more precisely the application (9.2.6.1) \[ U \mapsto \text{sous-topos de } E \text{ défini par } U \] commutes at finite intersections.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.2.7

Prove that the application (9.2.6.1) also switches to any Sups. (Use 9.1.7.2 e)).
9.3. Construction of the closed subtopos complementary to an open subtopos.

Let \(T\) be a topological space, and \(U\) be an open of \(T\), such that \(\mathrm{Top}(U)\) is identified with a open subtopic of \(\mathrm{Top}(T)\) (notations of 2.1). Let \(Y\) be the closed topological subspace of \(T\) complementary to \(U\). We can then (up to equivalence) consider \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) as a sub-topic of \(\mathrm{Top}(T)\), namely the sub-topos formed of the objects \(F\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(T)\) whose restriction to \(U\) is the final topos of \(U\). This description of a subcategory of \(E = \mathrm{Top}(T)\) keeps a sense each time we have a topos and an open \(U\) of this one, and we will see that it always provides a subtopic of \(E\). Moreover, it is immediate in the case particular considered first (and with the terminology introduced in exercise 9.1.13) that \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(U)\) are complementary subtopics of each other (use 9.2.5 and 9.1.7.2 e)). It will still be the same in the general case, and we will see that this property uniquely characterizes the subtopos considered. It therefore deserves from all points of view, the name of complementary closed subtopos of the envisaged open \(U\) or of open subtopic defined by \(U\). The details of the construction of this topos \(\mathscr{F}\), and the inverse image functor \(i^* : E \to \mathscr{F}\), will be given in this section, while section 9.4 will develop the first properties.
Let us therefore, like 9.2.1, be \(E\) a topos, \(U\) an open of \(E\), and consider the morphism of location \[ (9.3.2.1) \qquad j : \mathscr{U} = E_{/U} \to E , \] which is an open embedding.

For any object \(X\) of \(E\), let's set \[ (9.3.2.2) \qquad X_{\mathbf{C}U} = U \sqcup_{U \times X} X , \] where the amalgamation is done for the canonical projections \[ \mathrm{pr}_1 : U \times X \to U , \qquad \mathrm{pr}_2 : U \times X \to X \] We therefore have a CoCartesian diagram (I 10) in \(E\), depending functionally on \(X\): \[ (9.3.2.3) \qquad \xymatrix{ U \times X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & X \ar[d] \\ U \ar[r] & X_{\mathbf{C}U} } \] Note that in the example considered in 9.3.1, \(X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(i_* i^*(X)\) (where \(i : Y \to T\) is inclusion), and \(X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) identifies with the addition morphism.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.3.3

With the previous notations, we have the following:

  1. a)

    For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the following conditions are equivalent:

    (i) There exists an object \(Y\) of \(E\) and an isomorphism \(X \approx Y_{\mathbf{C}U}\).

    (ii) The canonical morphism \(X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) is an isomorphism.

    (iii) The canonical morphism \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : X \times U \to U\) is an isomorphism (i.e. \(j^*(X)\) is an object final of \(E_{/U}\)).

  2. b)

    For any morphism \[ m : X \to X' \] in \(E\), the following conditions are equivalent:

    (i') The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{m} \ar[d] & X' \ar[d] \\ X_{\mathbf{C}U} \ar[r] & X'_{\mathbf{C}U} } \] is Cartesian.

    (ii') The morphism \(m \times \mathrm{id}_U : X \times U \to X' \times U\) is an isomorphism, i.e. \(j^*(m)\) is an isomorphism.

  3. c)

    the functor \[ X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U} \] of \(E\) in \(E\) is left exact, transforms epimorphic families into epimorphic families, commutes to filtering inductive limits and amalgamated sums.

Proof. We first prove the proposition in the case where \(E\) is of the form \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a small category, and for this we are brought back by I.3.1 to the case where \(E\) is the "punctual" topos (Ens), where the proposition is obvious. We move from there to the general case by the standard processes of II 4, by use of the "associated bundle" functor.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.3.4

With the notations of 9.3.2, the strictly full subcategory \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\) defined by the sub-objects \(X\) of \(E\) such that \(j^*(X)\) is a final object of \(\mathscr{U}\) (cf. 9.3.3 a)) is a sub-topos of \(E\), i.e. (9.1.1. ) it is a topos, and the inclusion functor \(i_* : \mathscr{F} \to E\) is the direct image functor by a morphism of topos \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\), whose reciprocal image functor is the functor \(X \mapsto i^* X = X_{\mathbf{C}U}\). The adjunction morphism \(X \to i^* i_* X\) is the canonical morphism \(X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) of (9.3.2.3)

Proof: For any object \(X\) of \(E\), let us denote by \(u(X)\) the canonical morphism \(X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U}\). The morphism \(u(X)\) is functorial in \(X\) and for any object \(X\), \(u(X_{\mathbf{C}U})\) is an isomorphism. It then follows formally and trivially from these two properties that the functor \(i^*\) is added on the left to the functor \(i_*\) and the addition morphism is \(u(X)\). As the functor \(X \to X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) is left exact, the functor \(i^*\) is left exact. It then follows from II 5.5 that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a topos, and from definition 3.1 that the couple \((i^*, i_*)\) is a topos morphism.

The subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\) described in 9.3.4 is called the closed subtopos of \(E\) complementary to the open \(U\) of \(E\), or (optionally) the open subtopic of \(E\) corresponding to \(U\). In accordance with 9.1.1 b), the morphism \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\) built in 9.3.4. is called the inclusion morphism. A full subcategory \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\) is called a closed subtopos of \(E\) if there exists an open \(U\) of \(E\) such that \(\mathscr{F}\) is the subtopos closed complementary to \(U\). Note that this \(U\) is uniquely determined as \(i_*(\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}})\), where \(\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}\) is the initial object of \(\mathscr{F}\), equal to \(i^*(\varnothing_E)\); it is also called theopen of \(E\) complementary to closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) and the subtopos of \(E\) defined by \(U\) is also called the open subtopos of \(E\) complementary to\(\mathscr{F}\). Finally, a topos embedding (9.1.1 a)) \(i : F \to E\) is said to be a closed embedding if the corresponding subtopic of \(E\) is closed.

If \(G\) is a Group on \(E\), \(s \in \mathrm{Hom}(e_E,G)\), we call support of \(G\) (resp. of \(S\)) the subtopos closed of \(E\) complementary to the open cosupport of \(G\) (resp. \(s\)) (8.5.1, 8.5.2).

With the notations of 9.3.2. and 9.3.4. we therefore find two topos morphisms \[ (9.3.6.1) \qquad \mathscr{U} \xrightarrow{\ j\ } E \xleftarrow{\ i\ } \mathscr{F} , \] defining five functors forming two sequences of adjoint functors \((j_! , j^* , j_*)\) and \((i^* , i_*)\): \[ (9.3.6.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathscr{U} \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{j_!} \ar@<-1ex>[r]_-{j_*} & E \ar[l]_-{j^*} \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{i^*} & \mathscr{F} \ar@<1ex>[l]^-{i_*} } \] The functor \[ (9.3.6.3) \qquad \rho = i^* j_* : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F} \] is called the gluing functor relative to the open \(U\) of \(E\); more generally if \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are two any topos, a functor \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) is called a gluing functor if it is left exact and accessible (I.9.2), or what amounts to the same thing (I.8), if it admits a pro-deputy. The reasons for this Terminology will appear in 9.4.1 d) and 9.5.4. below.
9.4. First properties of the closed subtopic \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\)

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.4.1

The notations are those of 9.3.2 and 9.3.4.

  1. a)

    The functor \(i_* : \mathscr{F} \to E\) transforms epimorphic families into families epimorphic. It commutes to the formation of amalgamated sums and filtering inductive limits.

  2. b)

    For any object \(X\) of \(E\) such that the projection morphism \(U \times X \to U\) is a epimorphism, the addition morphism \(X \to i_* i^* X\) is an epimorphism.

  3. c)

    The pair of functors \((i^*, j^*)\) of \(E\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{U}\) respectively is conservative (I 6.1) i.e. a morphism \(m\) of \(E\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(i^*(m)\) and \(j^*(m)\) are isomorphisms (this is equivalent to saying that the pair of functors \((i^*, j^*)\) is faithful (6.4.0)).

  4. d)

    The joining functor \(\rho = i^* j_* : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) is a left-exact functor and accessible (I 9.2), or what amounts to the same (I 8. ) it admits a pro-adjoint \(\sigma : \mathrm{Pro}\, \mathscr{F} \to \mathrm{Pro}\, \mathscr{U}\).

Demonstration: a) results in 9.3.3 c). To demonstrate b) it is sufficient to refer to the definition (9.3.2.2) of \(X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) taking into account that \(i_* i^* X = X_{\mathbf{C}U}\) (9.3.4). Assertion c) results from 9.3.3 b). The functors \(i_*\) and \(j_*\) are left-exact and therefore \(\rho\) is left-exact. The functor \(j_*\) is a direct image functor for a topos morphism and consequently it is accessible (I 9.5; we note that a topos is an accessible category (I 9.11.3)). As the functor \(i^*\) commutes at inductive limits (it is a reciprocal image functor), the functor \(\rho\) composed of two accessible functors is accessible.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.4.2

Let \(E'\) be a topos. Then the functor \(h \mapsto i \circ h\): \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E' ,\mathscr{F}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E', E) \] is fully faithful, and its essential image is formed from topos morphisms \(g : E' \to E\) such that \(g^*(U)\) is an initial object \(\varnothing_{E'}\) of \(E'\).

By 9.1.4, we know that the considered functor is fully faithful and that \(g\) is in its image essential if and only if for any object \(X'\) of \(E'\), the object \(g_*(X')\) of \(E\) belongs to \(\mathscr{F}\), i.e. that we have \[ (*) \qquad j^*(g_*(X')) \approx e_{\mathscr{U}} \quad \text{pour tout } X' \in \operatorname{Ob} E' . \] Now, let us set \(U' = g^*(U)\), and consider the topos diagram \[ \xymatrix{ E'_{/U'} \ar[r]^-{j'} \ar[d]_-{g_U} & E' \ar[d]^-{g} \\ E_{/U} \ar[r]_-{j} & E } \] it is clear that we have a canonical isomorphism \[ j^*(g_*(X')) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } g_{U*}(j'^*(X')) , \] on the other hand it is also immediate that the functor \(j'^*\) is essentially surjective, therefore the condition (∗) is equivalent to the condition \[ (**) \qquad g_{U*}(Y') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } e_{\mathscr{U}} \quad \text{pour tout } Y' \in \operatorname{Ob} E'_{/U'} . \] Using the addition property of \(g_{U*}\) and \(g_U^*\), we immediately see that this means that \(g_U^*(Y)\) is a initial object of \(E'_{/U'}\), for any object \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{U}\), or again (using that the initial object of \(E'_{/U'}\) is strict (II 4.5)) that it is so for \(Y = e_{\mathscr{U}}\), final object of \(\mathscr{U}\). But this also means that the final object \(U'\) of \(E'_{/U'}\) is initial, or, which obviously amounts to the same thing, if and only if \(U'\) is an initial object \(\varnothing_{E'}\) by \(E'\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.4.3

Let \(g : E' \to E\) be a morphism of topos \(U' = g^*(U)\), \(\mathscr{U}' = E'_{/U'}\), and \(\mathscr{F}'\) the closed subtopos of \(E'\) complementary to the open \(U'\), finally \(i' : \mathscr{F}' \to E'\) the inclusion morphism. So the topos morphism \(g \circ i' : \mathscr{F}' \to E\) factorizes up to isomorphism into a topos morphism \(g_{\mathscr{F}} : \mathscr{F}' \to \mathscr{F}\), and the corresponding topos diagram \[ (9.4.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F}' \ar[r]^-{i'} \ar[d]_-{g_{\mathscr{F}}} & E' \ar[d]^-{g} \\ \mathscr{F} \ar[r]_-{i} & E } \] is 2-Cartesian (cf. 5.11).
Formally results from 9.4.2. and definitions.

With the terminology introduced in 9.1.6 d), we can therefore say, in particular, that the image inverse by a topos morphism \(g : E' \to E\) of a closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\) is a closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}'\) of \(E'\) (namely the closed subtopic complementary to the open \(U' = g^*(U)\), where \(U\) is the open of \(E\) complementary to \(\mathscr{F}\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.4.4

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(\mathscr{U}\) be an open sub-topos of \(E\), \(\mathscr{F}\) be the closed sub-topos complementary.

  1. a)

    We have \[ \mathscr{U} \cap \mathscr{F} \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing) , \qquad \mathscr{U} \vee \mathscr{F} = E , \] where the sign \(\vee\) designates the Sup in the set of all subtopos of \(E\) (9.1.2 c)). (With the terminology introduced in 9.1.13, \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are subtopics of \(E\) complementary to each other).

  2. b)

    \(\mathscr{U}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{F}\)) is the largest among the subtopics \(E'\) of \(E\) such that we have \(E' \cap \mathscr{F} \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) (resp. \(\mathscr{U} \cap E' \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\)).

Demonstration. a) the first relation amounts to saying that if \(E'\) is a topos and \(g : E' \to E\) is a morphism of topos which is factorized up to isomorphism by \(\mathscr{U}\) and by \(\mathscr{F}\), then \(E' \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\); gold in under 9.2.5 and 9.4.2, the assumption means that \(g^*(U)\) is both an initial object and a final object of \(E'\), hence the conclusion. For the second relation, we note that by 9.1.7.2 e) it is equivalent to 9.4.1 c).

b) Suppose \(E' \cap \mathscr{F} \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\) ; under 9.4.3 \(E' \cap \mathscr{F}\) is equivalent to subtopos of \(E'\) complementary to \(g^*(U)\), where \(g : E' \to E\) is the inclusion morphism, therefore the condition considered means that \(U' = g^*(U)\) is a final object of \(E'\), i.e. (9.2.5) that \(E' \subset \mathscr{U}\). Suppose that \(\mathscr{U} \cap E' \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\); under 5.11 \(U \cap E'\) is equivalent to \(E'_{/U'}\), so the condition considered means that \(U'\) is initial object of \(E'\), i.e. (9.4.2) that \(E' \subset \mathscr{F}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.4.5

Let \((\mathscr{F}_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of closed subtopos of the topos \(E\), and for all \(i \in I\), i.e. \(U_i\) the open of \(E\) complementary to \(\mathscr{F}_i\). Then the subtopos of \(E\) intersection of \(\mathscr{F}_i\) (9.1.3) is a closed subtopic, whose complementary open is \(U = \mathrm{Sup}\, U_i\).

This follows formally from the universal property of intersection as a 2-product and from 9.4.2, given that for a topos morphism \(g : E' \to E\), \(g^*(U) = \mathrm{Sup}\, g^*(U_i)\) is an initial object if and only if the \(g^*(U_i)\) are.
We prove in an analogous way:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.4.6

With notations of 9.4.5 let's assume \(I\) finished. Then the subtopos of \(E\) bounds superior of (9.1.2 c)) is a closed subtopos of \(E\), whose complementary open is equal to \(\bigcap U_i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.4.7 (Collection of subtopos)

Let \(E\) be a topos.

  1. a)

    Let \(E'\) be a subtopos of \(E\), \((S_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of objects of \(E\) covering the final object, and for all \(i \in I\), let \(S'_i\) be the inverse image of \(S_i\) in \(E'\), and \(E'_i \approx E'_{/S'_i}\) the subtopos of \(E_i = E_{/S_i}\) inverse image by the location morphism \(E_i \to E\) of the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) (9.1.6 d) and 5.11). Show that for an object \(X\) of \(E\), \(X\) belongs to \(E'\) if and only if for all \(i \in I\), the inverse image \(X|S_i\) of \(X\) in \(E_i\) belongs to \(E'_i\).

  2. b)

    Suppose \(E\) is of the form \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. Show that the pre-beam on \(C\) \[ S \mapsto \text{ensemble des sous-topos du topos } (C_{/S})^{\sim} \approx E_{/\varepsilon(S)} \] is a beam. \(E\) being arbitrary again, conclude that there exists an element of \(E\) which represents the functor \[ S \mapsto \text{ensemble des sous-topos du topos } E_{/S} ; \]

  3. c)

    With the notations of a), show that for the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\) to be a open subtopos (resp. a closed subtopos), it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), it is the same for the induced subtopos \(E'_i\) of \(E_i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.4.8 (Interior, exterior, grip and border of a subtopos)

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(E'\) be a sub-topos, \(i : E' \to E\) be the inclusion morphism.

  1. a)

    Show that \(i_*(\varnothing_{E'})\) is the largest open \(U\) of \(E\) such that we have \(E' | \mathscr{U} \approx \mathrm{Top}(\varnothing)\), where \(\mathscr{U}\) is the open subtopic of \(E\) defined by \(U\). We will call this \(U\), or \(\mathscr{U}\), the exterior of the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\), and the closed subtopos of \(E\) complementary to \(U\) the adhesion of \(E'\).

  2. b)

    Show that there exists an open greatest \(V\) of \(E\) such that the open subtopos corresponding \(\mathscr{V}\) of \(E\) is contained in \(E'\). (Use 9.2.7.) We will call this \(V\), or \(\mathscr{V}\), the interior of the subtopic \(E'\) of \(E\). The open \(U\) and \(V\) of \(E\) are disjoint (\(U \cap V = \varnothing_E\)); the closed subtopic of \(E\) complement of \(\mathrm{Sup}(U,V) = \mathscr{U} \cup \mathscr{V}\) is called the border of the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\).

  3. c)

    For \(E'\) to be an open (resp. closed) subtopic of \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that it is equal to its interior (resp. it is equal to its adhesion). For the boundary of \(E'\) to be empty, it is necessary and it suffices that \(E'\) is an open and closed subtopic of \(E\), or that it is the open subtopic of \(E\) defined by an open \(V\) of \(E\) which is direct command of the final object \(e\), i.e. such that there exists a sub-object \(V\) of \(e\) with \(e \approx U \cup V\). For the exterior of \(E'\) to be "empty" i.e. for the adhesion to be equal to \(E\), it It is necessary and sufficient that \(i : E' \to E\) be dominant (8.8).

  4. d)

    Let \(S\) be an object of \(E\), \(F = E_{/S}\) the induced topos, \(F' \approx F_{/S'}\) the sub-topos of \(F\) induced by the subtopos \(E'\) of \(E\). Show that the exterior (resp. adhesion, resp. interior, resp. boundary) of \(F'\) is induced from the outside (resp. ...) of \(E'\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.4.9 (Locally closed sub-topos of a topos)

A sub-topos \(F\) of a topos \(E\) is said to be a locally closed sub-topos of \(E\) if it is a intersection of an open subtopos and a closed subtopos.

  1. a)

    Prove that the intersection of a finite family of locally closed subtopos of \(E\) is locally closed. If \(g : E' \to E\) is a topos morphism, prove that the inverse image (9.1.6 d)) of a locally closed subtopos of \(E\) is a locally closed subtopos of \(E'\).

  2. b)

    Let \(X\) be a topological space, and \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\). For any part \(X'\) of \(X\), consider the subtopos \(T(X')\) of \(E\) essential image of \(\mathrm{Top}(X')\) in \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\). Show that if \(X'\) is a locally closed part, \(T(X')\) is a locally closed subtopos of \(E\), the converse being true if we further assumes \(X\) and \(X'\) sober. Prove that \(X' \mapsto T(X')\) establishes an isomorphism of ordered sets between the set of locally closed parts \(X'\) of \(X\) and the set of locally closed subtopos of \(E\).

  3. c)

    Let \(F\) be a subtopos of \(E\). Show that any open subtopic of \(F\) is induced by an open subtopic of \(E\). (Use 9.1.8 c)). Show that \(F\) is a locally closed subtopic of \(E\) if and only if it is an open subtopos of its adherence (9.4.8 a)). Conclude, using 9.4.8 d), that the property for \(F\) to be a locally closed subtopic of \(E\) is a local property on \(E\).

  4. d)

    Let \(F\) be a locally closed subtopos of \(E\). Show that among all the ways to write \(F\) as an intersection of an open subtopos \(\mathscr{U}\) and a closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\), it is one with \(\mathscr{U}\) as large as possible, and \(\mathscr{F}\) as small as possible. (Take \(\mathscr{F}\) = grip of \(F\), and \(\mathscr{U}\) = more large open subtopos inducing on \(\mathscr{F}\) the subtopos \(F\).) Compatibility of the formation of \(\mathscr{U}, \mathscr{F}\) with the location on \(E\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.4.10

Develop the notion of constructible sub-topos, locally constructible, quasi-constructible, locally quasi-constructible, on the model of familiar notions in the case of topological spaces (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 9.1, EGA IV 10.1). In the case of a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), we will find a bijection between the set of constructible parts (resp. ...) of \(X\), and the set of constructible subtopics (resp. ...) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\).
9.5. The regluing theorem

Let \(f : A \to B\) be a functor between two categories. Let us designate by \((B,A,f)\) the following category: the objects of \((B,A,f)\) are the triples \[ (X,Y,u) \] where \(X\) is an object of \(B\), \(Y\) an object of \(A\), and \(u\) a morphism of \(X\) into \(f(Y)\); morphisms between two objects \((X,Y,u)\) and \((X',Y',u')\) are the pairs \((m,m')\), where \(m\) is a morphism of \(X\) into \(X'\) and \(m'\) a morphism of \(Y\) into \(Y'\), such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{m} & f(Y) \ar[d]^-{f(m')} \\ X' \ar[r]_-{u'} & f(Y') } \] the composition of morphisms is defined in the obvious way.

Note that the category \((B,A,f)\) depends functorially on the data system \(A, B, f\), in a sense that we leave to the reader to specify. In particular, if \(f'\) is a second functor of \(A\) in \(B\), then any morphism of functors \[ f \xrightarrow{\ u\ } f' \] defines a functor \((A,B,f) \to (A,B,f')\), which is an isomorphism if \(u : f \to f'\) is.

Let's return to the situation in 9.3., and consider the category \((\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{U}, \rho)\) defined in 9.5.1. A each object \(X\) of \(E\) corresponds to an object \((i^*X, j^*X, h(X) : i^*X \to \rho j^*X)\) of \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) where \(h(X)\) is obtained by transforming the addition morphism \(X \to j_* j^*(X)\) with the functor \(i^*\) (we have \(\rho = i^* j_*\) (9.10)). We therefore defined a functor \[ (9.5.3.1) \qquad \Phi : E \to (\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{U}, \rho) . \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 9.5.4
  1. a)

    Let \(E\) be a topos, \(\mathscr{U}\) be an open sub-topos of \(E\), \(\mathscr{F}\) be the closed sub-topos complementary. The functor \[ \rho = i^* j_* : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F} \] is left-exact and accessible and the functor \(\Phi\) (9.5.3.1) is an equivalence of categories.

  2. b)

    Conversely, let \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) be two topos and \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) be a functor of joining (9.3.6) (i.e. accessible (I 9.2) and exact on the left). The category \(E = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U}, \rho)\) is a topos. Let \(\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}\) an initial object of \(\mathscr{F}\), \(e_{\mathscr{U}}\) a final object of \(\mathscr{U}\), and either \(U = (\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}, e_{\mathscr{U}}, \varnothing_{\mathscr{F}} \to \rho(e_{\mathscr{U}})) \in \operatorname{Ob} E\). The functor \(X \mapsto (\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}, X, \rho(X))\) induces a equivalence of \(\mathscr{U}\) to \(E_{/U}\), therefore with the open subtopic \(\mathscr{U}\) of \(E\) defined by \(U\). The functor \(Y \mapsto (Y,e,Y \to \rho(e))\) is a closed embedding (9.3.5), i.e. induces an equivalence of \(\mathscr{F}\) with a subtopos closed \(\mathscr{F}'\) from \(E\). The subtopics \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}'\) are complementary. Let \(\rho' : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}'\) be the functor of regluing. The functors \(\rho\) and \(\rho'\) are compatible with the equivalences explained above.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.5.5

Let \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) be two topos and \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) be a joining functor (9.3.6). The topos \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) is called the topos constructed from \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) by joining using the functor \(\rho\).
9.5.6. Demonstration of 9.5.4. a)(∗)

The first assertion is only a reminder of 9.4.1 d). The pair of functors \((i^*, j^*)\) is conservative, or again, faithful (9.4.1 c)). A fortiori the functor \(\Phi\) is faithful. Let us show that he is fully faithful. It results of 9.3.3 b) that, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), the following diagram is Cartesian: \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ X \ar[r] \ar[d] & j_* j^* X \ar[d] \\ i_* i^* X \ar[r] & i_* i^* j_* j^* X } \] Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two objects of \(E\), and \((m,m')\): \[ \xymatrix{ i^* X \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{m} & i^* j_* j^* X \ar[d]^-{i^* j_* m'} \\ i^* Y \ar[r] & i^* j_* j^* Y } \] a morphism between \(\Phi(X)\) and \(\Phi(Y)\). Using the Cartesian product functoriality and the diagrams (∗), we obtain a morphism \(w : X \to Y\) which makes the following diagrams commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{w} \ar[d] & Y \ar[d] \\ j_* j^* X \ar[r] & j_* j^* Y } \qquad \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{w} \ar[d] & Y \ar[d] \\ i_* i^* X \ar[r] & i_* i^* Y } \] By respectively applying the functors \(j^*\) and \(i^*\) we obtain: \(j^* w = m'\), \(i^* w = m\). Let's show now that the functor \(\Phi\) is essentially surjective. Let \((Y,X,u : Y \to i^* j_*(X))\) be an object of \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},i^* j_*)\). We deduces a diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & j_* X \ar[d] \\ i_* Y \ar[r]_-{i_* u} & i_* i^* j_* X } \] Hence, taking the fiber product, a Cartesian diagram: \[ (**) \qquad \xymatrix{ W \ar[r] \ar[d] & j_* X \ar[d] \\ i_* Y \ar[r]_-{i_* u} & i_* i^* j_* X } \] We leave it to the reader to show that \(\Phi(W)\) is isomorphic to \((Y,X,u : Y \to i^* j_*(X))\) and that the diagram (∗∗) is isomorphic to the diagram (∗) relative to \(W\).
9.5.7. Demonstration of 9.5.4.1).
We will limit ourselves to showing that \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) is a topos. The demonstration of others assertions is left to the reader. Let's check properties a), b), c), d) of 1.1.2.
a) Finite projective limits are representable: Clear because the categories \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are topos and \(\rho\) commute to finite projective limits.

b) Direct sums are representable, disjoint and universal Let \((Y_i \xrightarrow{u_i} \rho(X_i))\), \(i \in I\), a family of objects of \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) By definition of inductive limits, we have a canonical morphism \[ \coprod_{i \in I} \rho(X_i) \to \rho(\coprod_{i \in I} X_i) \] hence, by composing with the morphism \(\coprod u_i\), a morphism \[ n : \coprod_{i \in I} Y_i \to \rho(\coprod_{i \in I} X_i) . \] We immediately verify that this last object is the direct sum of the family considered, and that this direct sum is disjoint and universal.

c) The equivalence relations are effective and universal: Let \[ \xymatrix{ R_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar[d] & Y \ar[d]^-{w} \\ \rho(R_2) \ar@<0.5ex>[r]^-{\rho(u)} \ar@<-0.5ex>[r]_-{\rho(v)} & \rho(X) } \] an equivalence relation on the object \(Y \xrightarrow{\ w\ } \rho(X)\). The relation \(R_1 \rightrightarrows Y\) is then a relation of equivalence on \(Y\) in \(\mathscr{F}\), and the relation \(R_2 \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} X\) is an equivalence relation on \(X\) in \(\mathscr{U}\). The quotients \(Y/R_1\), \(\rho(X)/\rho(R_2)\), \(X/R_2\) are representable in \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{U}\) because these categories are topos. Moreover, by definition of inductive limits, the canonical morphism \(\rho(X) \to \rho(X/R_2)\) is factor by \(\rho(X)/\rho(R_2)\). We deduce, by the functoriality of the inductive limits, a canonical morphism \(Y/R_1 \xrightarrow{\ t\ } \rho(X/R_2)\). We verify that this last object is the quotient of \(Y \xrightarrow{\ w\ } \rho(X)\) by the equivalence relation considered, that this quotient is effective (I 10) and that all these properties are preserved by change of base.

d) \((\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) admits a small generating family. This property results from (1.9.2 5) (account given that \(\mathscr{F}\) is accessible), taking \(B = \Delta_1 = (0 \to 1)\), \(E_o = \mathscr{F}\), \(E_1 = \mathscr{U}\), \(f^* = \rho : E_1 \to E_o\), hence \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E) = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\).

We can also avoid using 1.9.25 (the demonstration given was quite painful!) and use the hypothesis on \(\rho\) in the form that \(\rho\) admits a pro-adjoint \[ \sigma : \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U}) , \] (this interpretation (I 8) based on (I 9.), the demonstration of which is much more understandable). He then suffice to note that if \(C'\) (resp. \(C''\)) is a generating subcategory of \(\mathscr{U}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{F}\)), and if for all \(X'' \in \operatorname{Ob} C''\), we represent \(\sigma(X'')\) in the form of a projective system \((\sigma(X'')_i)_{i \in I(X')}\), where \(I(X')\) is a small filtering ordered set, then the family of objects of \(E\) which are, either of the form \((\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}, X', \varnothing_{\mathscr{F}} \to \rho(X'))\) with \(X' \in \operatorname{Ob} C'\), or of the form \((X'', \sigma(X'')_i , u_{X''} : X'' \to \rho(\sigma(X'')_i))\), where \(X'' \in \operatorname{Ob} C''\), \(i \in I(X'')\), and \(u_{X''}\) corresponds to addition to the canonical morphism \(\sigma(X'') \to \sigma(X'')_i\), - is a generating family (obviously small) if \(C'\), \(C''\) are chosen small. Verification of this fact is indeed immediate, and left to the reader.

Let's use the notations of 9.5.7 b), and show how we can explain, with isomorphisms canonicals near functors, the system of functors (9.3.6.2), where we do \(E = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\). The details of Verification of the assertions below (mainly mechanical using 9.5.4) is left to the reader.
The functor \[ i^* : (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho) \to \mathscr{F} \] is given by \[ i^*(X,Y,u : X \to \rho(Y)) = X . \]
The functor \[ i_* : \mathscr{F} \to (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho) \] is given by \(i_*(X) = (X,e,X \to \rho(e))\) (\(e\) = final object of \(\mathscr{U}\)).
The addition morphism \[ \mathrm{id} \to i_* i^* \] is isomorphic to the functorial morphism \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{id}} & \rho(Y) \ar[d] \\ X \ar[r] & \rho(e) } \]
The functor \[ j^* : (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho) \to \mathscr{U} \] is given by \(j^*(X,Y,u : X \to \rho(Y)) = Y\).

The functor \[ j_! : \mathscr{U} \to (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho) \] is given by \(j_!(Y) = (\varnothing_{\mathscr{F}}, Y, \varnothing_{\mathscr{F}} \to \rho(Y))\) (\(\varnothing\) initial object of \(\mathscr{F}\)).
The functor \[ j_* : \mathscr{U} \to (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho) \] is given by \(j_*(Y) = (\rho(Y), Y, \mathrm{id} : \rho(Y) \to \rho(Y))\).

The addition morphism \[ j_! j^* \to \mathrm{id} \] is isomorphic to the functorial morphism \[ \xymatrix{ \varnothing_{\mathscr{F}} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \rho(Y) \ar[d]^-{\rho(\mathrm{id})} \\ X \ar[r]_-{u} & \rho(Y) } \]

The addition morphism \[ \mathrm{id} \to j_* j^* \] is isomorphic to the functorial morphism \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{u} & \rho(Y) \ar[d]^-{\rho(\mathrm{id})} \\ \rho(Y) \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{id}} & \rho(Y) } \]

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.5.9 (Accuracy properties of a pasted topos)

Let \(\mathscr{U}\), \(\mathscr{F}\) two topos, \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) a joining functor, \(E = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) the pasted topos, \(j : \mathscr{U} \to E\) and \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\) the morphisms of canonical topos, so that \(\rho = i^* j_*\).

  1. a)

    Prove that \(i^*\) commutes to (small) products (i.e. (1.8 or 1.8) the functor \(i^*\) admits an adjoint on the left \(i_!\)) if and only if it is the same for \(\rho\), i.e. (loc. cit.) if and only if \(\rho\) admits a deputy on the left \(\sigma : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{U}\) (or even if and only if the pro-adjoint of \(\rho\) restricted to \(\mathscr{F}\) (9.6.3) is factorized up to isomorphism by \(\mathscr{U}\)): we then have \(\sigma = j^* i_!\). So the joining functors \(\rho\) having this property correspond up to isomorphism to the functors \(\sigma : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{U}\) which have an adjoint to right, i.e. (1.6) which commute at \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits, or which are continuous. When \(\mathscr{F}\) is equivalent to a topos \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, these functors therefore correspond up to isomorphism to the continuous functors \(C \to \mathscr{U}\) (III 1.2 and 1.7).

  2. b)

    Suppose the functor \(i_!\) is defined. Show that for \(i_!\) to commute to a type determined small projective limits, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\sigma : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{U}\) commute there. (For necessity, use that \(j^*\) commutes to small projective limits; for sufficiency, use the equivalent description of \(E\) in terms of \(\sigma\) as formed from the triples \((Y,X,u)\) with \(Y \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{F}\), \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathscr{U}\), \(u : \sigma(Y) \to X\).)

  3. c)

    Deduce from a) and b) an example of closed embedding of topos \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\) such that \(i_!\) exists, but neither commutes to fiber products nor to filtering projective limits. (Just find a continuous functor between two topos which has neither of these two accuracy properties.)

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.5.10 (Collection of topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\))

Let \(C\) be a small category, and \(E = \widehat{C}\), which is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos.

  1. a)

    Remember that the open \(U\) of \(E\) correspond to the screens \(C'\) of \(C\) (1.4.1, 4.2), \(E_{/U}\) then being equivalent to \(\widehat{C}'\), the functor \(j^* : E \to E_{/U}\) being identified with the restriction functor (1.5.11), the inclusion morphism \(j : E_{/U} \to E\) being \(\widehat{f} : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C}\) (4.6.1), where \(f : C' \to C\) is the inclusion. Let \(C''\) be the full subcategory of \(C\) complement of \(C'\) (i.e. \(\operatorname{Ob} C''\) is the complement of \(\operatorname{Ob} C'\) in \(\operatorname{Ob} C\)), \(g : C'' \to C\) inclusion. Then the closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E = \widehat{C}\), complementary to the defined open subtopos by \(C'\), is canonically equivalent to \(\widehat{C}''\), the inclusion morphism \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\) identifying with \(\widehat{g} : \widehat{C}'' \to \widehat{C}\).

  2. b)

    Consider the functor \[ (9.5.10.1) \qquad h : C'^\circ \times C'' \to (\mathrm{Ens}) , \qquad h(X',X'') = \mathrm{Hom}_C(X',X'') , \] and note that \(C\) is reconstituted up to isomorphism, with its full subcategory \(C'\), by the knowledge of categories \(C',C''\) and the bifunctor \(h\) (the latter can be chosen arbitrarily). (Compare 9.7.1 more low.) The data of \(h\) is equivalent to that of a functor \(C'' \to \widehat{C}' = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C'^\circ,(\mathrm{Ens}))\), or even (III 1.2 and 1.7) to that of a functor \[ (9.5.10.2) \qquad \sigma : \widehat{C}'' \to \widehat{C}' \] continuous, i.e. (1.6) commuting to small inductive limits, or again, admitting an adjoint on the right. \[ (9.5.10.3) \qquad \rho : \widehat{C}' \to \widehat{C}'' . \] Show that \(\rho\) is none other than the joining functor associated with the open subtopic \(\widehat{C}'\) of \(E = \widehat{C}\).

  3. c)

    Deduce from a) and b) that if we give ourselves a joining functor (9.5.10.3) between two categories of prebundles, hence a glued topos \(E\), the following conditions are equivalent:

    (i) \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\).

    (ii) The functor \(\rho\) commutes to small products (or again, it admits a left adjoint (9.5.10.2)).

    (iii) The inclusion morphism \(i : \widehat{C}'' \to E\) is "essential" i.e. \(i^*\) commutes to products, or again \(i^*\) admits an assistant on the left \(i_!\).

    When this is so, explain a category \(C\) such as \(E \approx \widehat{C}\) using the bifunctor (9.5.10.1) defined by the restriction of (9.5.10.2) to \(C''\).

  4. d)

    Show that a topos obtained by joining two punctual topos is not necessarily equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.5.11 (Morphism of a topos pasted into a topos)
  1. a)

    Let \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) be a functor between two categories, \(E = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) the category "glued" from 9.5.1, and \(F\) any category. Consider the functor \(f \mapsto \rho \circ f\): \[ \widetilde{\rho} : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,\mathscr{U}) \to \widetilde{\mathscr{F}} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,\mathscr{F}) , \] and define an equivalence of categories \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,E) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\widetilde{\mathscr{F}},\widetilde{\mathscr{U}},\widetilde{\rho}) . \]

  2. b)

    Let \(f : F \to E\) be a functor, and consider its compounds with canonical functors \(j^* : E \to \mathscr{U}\) and \(i^* : E \to \mathscr{F}\). Show that \(f\) commutes at a determined type of inductive limits (assumed representable in \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\), therefore in \(E\)) if and only if \(j^* f\) and \(i^* f\) commute there. Show that if \(\rho\) commutes to a determined type of projective limits (which is therefore representable in \(E\)), then \(f\) commutes there if and only if it is the same for \(j^* f\) and \(i^* f\).

  3. c)

    Now suppose that \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are topos, and \(\rho\) a functor of regluing. Let \(F\) be a topos, \(f : F \to E\) be a functor. Conclude from b) that \(f\) is an inverse image functor associated with a topos morphism \(E \to F\) if and only if the same is true of the functors \(j^* f : F \to \mathscr{U}\) and \(i^* f : F \to \mathscr{F}\). Conclude from this and a) that we have an equivalence between the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,F)\), and the category of triples \((f',f'',u)\), where \(f'\) (resp. \(f''\)) is an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathscr{U},F)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathscr{F},F)\)), and where \(u : f''^* \to \rho \circ f'^*\) is a homomorphism of functors \(F \to \mathscr{F}\): if \(f\) is a object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,F)\), \((f',f'',u)\) the corresponding triple, then \(f' = f \circ j\), \(f'' = f \circ i\).

  4. d)

    Specify in what sense it can be said that c) provides a characterization up to equivalence (in the 2-category of topos) of the topos \(E\) in terms of the triple \((\mathscr{U},\mathscr{F},\rho)\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.5.12 (Joining two topological spaces)
  1. a)

    Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(\rho : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to (\mathrm{Ens}) = \mathrm{Top}(\mathrm{pt})\) be a functor of regluing, i.e. (I 8. ) a prorepresentable functor, \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Top}(X))\) the object which prorepresents it, \(E\) the topos deduced from \(\rho\) by joining. Show that \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) if and only if \(A\) is isomorphic to an object of \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Ouv}(X))\). Conclude that if \(X\) is not empty, we can find \(\rho\) such that \(E\) is not equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\).

  2. b)

    More generally, consider a glue functor \(\rho : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(Y)\), with \(X,Y\) two topological spaces. Deduce an application \[ \varphi : X \to \operatorname{Ob}\mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Top}(Y)) \] (composed of \(X \to \operatorname{Ob}\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathrm{Top}(X)) \to \operatorname{Ob}\mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Top}(X))\) (6.8.5) and the pro-deputy \(\sigma\) of \(\rho\). (N. B. for \(x \in X\), \(\varphi(x)\) is identified with the fiber in \(x\) of the splicing beam (9.6.4) defined by \(\rho\).) Show that if \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(Z)\), then \(\varphi\) takes its values in the essential image of \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Ouv}(Y))\).

Problem: Do we have a reciprocal?
9.6. Joining beam

We saw in 9.5.4 that the data of a topos \(E\) provided with an open \(U\) essentially amounts to that of two topos \(\mathscr{U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) and a joining functor \[ (9.6.1) \qquad \rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F} \] i.e. a functor admitting a pro-adjoint \[ (9.6.2) \qquad \sigma : \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U}) . \] According to the sorite on proadjoint functors (I 8), the functor \(\rho\) is known up to unique isomorphism (and consequently the glued topos \(E = (\mathscr{F},\mathscr{U},\rho)\) is known up to equivalence of topos) when we know the functor \(\sigma\), or again, the functor induced by \(\sigma\) \[ (9.6.3) \qquad \sigma_o : \mathscr{F} \to \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U}) . \] The functor \(\sigma\) is determined, up to a unique isomorphism, by the property of commuting at small limits filtering projectives and to extend the functor \(\sigma_o\). On the other hand, for a given functor (9.6.3) to be isomorphic to a pro-adjoint, it is obviously necessary and sufficient that for any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{U}\), the functor contravariant \[ Y \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})}(\sigma_o(Y),X) \] on \(\mathscr{F}\) is representable, or again, that it is a sheaf on \(\mathscr{F}\) for the canonical topology (1.1.2 (iii)). We immediately see that this also means that the opposite functor \[ (9.6.4) \qquad \sigma_o^\circ : \mathscr{F}^\circ \to \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})^\circ \subset \widetilde{\mathscr{U}} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathscr{U},(\mathrm{Ens})) \] is a sheaf on \(\mathscr{F}\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})^\circ\) (II 6.1). Thus, the data of a joining functor (9.6.1) is also equivalent (up to isomorphism) to that of a sheaf on \(\mathscr{F}\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})^\circ\). The beam thus associated with a joining functor (or with a situation \((E,U,\mathscr{F})\) as in (9.3)) is called the reglue beam associated with the considered reglue functor \(\rho\) (resp. to the open \(U\) of the topos \(E\)).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.6.5

Specify 9.5.4 into a statement of 2-equivalence of 2-categories, between the 2-category formed of the pairs \((E,U)\) of a topos \(E\) and an open \(U\) of the said, and the 2-category formed of the triples \((\mathscr{U},\mathscr{F},\rho)\) topos \(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{F}\) and a joining functor \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\) (the explicit description of the 2-categories in question being left to the reader). Give a variation of this statement involving the 2-category triples \((\mathscr{U},\mathscr{F},\varphi)\), where \(\varphi\) is now a bundle on \(\mathscr{F}\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})^\circ\).
9.7. Points from a pasted topos

Let \(C\) be a category, and \[ u : D' \to C , \qquad v : D'' \to C \] two fully faithful functors, such that the essential images \(C'\), \(C''\) of \(u\) and \(v\) are such that \[ \operatorname{Ob} C' \cap \operatorname{Ob} C'' = \varnothing , \qquad \operatorname{Ob} C' \cup \operatorname{Ob} C'' = \operatorname{Ob} C . \] Let us further assume that \[ X' \in \operatorname{Ob} C' , X'' \in \operatorname{Ob} C'' \implies \mathrm{Hom}(X'',X') = \varnothing , \] or what amounts to the same thing, whether we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(v(A''),u(A')) = \varnothing \quad \text{pour } A' \in \operatorname{Ob} D' , A'' \in \operatorname{Ob} D'' . \] It is then immediate that the category \(C\) is reconstituted, with isomorphism canonical, by knowing the categories \(C'\) and \(C''\) and the functor \[ (X',X'') \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X',X'') : C'^\circ \times C'' \to (\mathrm{Ens}) ; \] similarly, \(C\) is reconstituted up to equivalence (this defined up to unique isomorphism) by the knowledge of \(D',D''\) and the functor \[ (A',A'') \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(u(A'),v(A'')) : D'^\circ \times D'' \to (\mathrm{Ens}) . \] This remark applies in particular to the situation described in the following proposition, and shows us that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) is determined, up to equivalence, by the knowledge of the categories \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{U})\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F})\) and a certain functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{U})^\circ \times \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F}) \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] deduced from the joining functor \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.7.2

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(U\) an open one of \(E\), \(\mathscr{U} = E_{/U}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) the closed subtopos of \(E\) complementary to \(U\). Consider the functors \(p \mapsto p \circ j\) and \(q \mapsto q \circ i\) induced by the morphisms include \(j : \mathscr{U} \to E\) and \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\): \[ (9.7.2.1) \qquad u : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{U}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E), \quad v : \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) . \] So we have the following:

  1. a)
