    The previous functors \(u\) and \(v\) are fully faithful, and any point of \(E\) belongs to the essential image of one or other of the functors \(u\) and \(v\) exclusively.

  2. b)

    Let \(p\) and \(q\) be two points of \(E\), such that \(p\) (resp. \(q\)) belongs to the image essential of \(u\) (resp. of \(v\)). So we have \[ (9.7.2.2) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(q,p) = \varnothing . \]

  3. c)

    Let \(p\) (resp. \(q\)) be a point of \(\mathscr{U}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{F}\)). We then have an isomorphism, functorial in \((q,p)\): \[ (9.7.2.3) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(u(p),v(q)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Pro}(\rho)(p),q) \approx \mathrm{Hom}(p,\sigma(q)) , \] where \[ \sigma : \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U}) \] is the functor (9.6.2) pro-adjoint of the joining functor \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\), and where \(\rho : \mathscr{U} \to \mathscr{F}\), we have identified (up to equivalence) the categories \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{U})\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F})\) to subcategories full of \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U})\), \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F})\) respectively (6.8.5).

Demonstration. a) The full fidelity of \(u\) and \(v\) is known (9.1.4). The assertion about images essential of \(u,v\) results from criteria 9.2.5 and 9.2.4 so that a point \(P \to E\) is factorized with isomorphism close by \(\mathscr{U}\) resp. \(\mathscr{F}\), taking into account the fact that the point topos \(P\) has only two open ones, namely the initial object and the final object of \(P\).

b) Taking into account a), the assertion means that if the point \(p : P \to E\) is factorized by \(\mathscr{U}\), it is even from any point \(p' : P \to E\) such as \(\mathrm{Hom}(p',p) \neq \varnothing\). But if \(\alpha : p' \to p\) it induces \(p^*(U) \to p'^*(U)\), and then \(p^*(U) = e_p\) implies \(p'^*(U) = e_p\), c.q.f.d.

c) Thanks to Lemma 9.7.2.4 below, applied to the inclusions \(j : \mathscr{U} \to E\) and \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\), we have a commutative functor diagram up to canonical isomorphisms \[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{U}) \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F}) \ar[l]_-{v} \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{U}) \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{Pro}(j_*)} & \mathrm{Pro}(E) & \mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F}) \ar[l]^-{\mathrm{Pro}(i_*)} } \] where the vertical arrows denote the canonical fully faithful functors (6.8.5). On the other hand, as \(i_*\) admits an adjoint on the left \(i^*\) \(\mathrm{Pro}(i_*)\) admits an adjoint on the left \(\mathrm{Pro}(i^*)\) (I 8 ), and we finally obtain functorial isomorphisms \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(u(p),v(q)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(E)}(\mathrm{Pro}(j_*)(p),\mathrm{Pro}(i_*)(q)) \\ &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F})}(\mathrm{Pro}(i^*)(\mathrm{Pro}(j_*)(p)),q) \\ &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(\mathscr{F})}(\mathrm{Pro}(i^* j_*)(p),q), \end{aligned} \] which is none other than the first formula (9.7.2.3), the second then being trivial by definition of \(\sigma\) as pro-deputy of \(\rho\). This therefore proves 9.7.2, modulo le

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.7.2.4

Let \(f : F \to E\) be a topos morphism. Then the functor diagram \[ (9.7.2.4.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(F) \ar[r]^-{p \mapsto f \circ p} \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E) \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Pro}(F) \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{Pro}(f_*)} & \mathrm{Pro}(E) } \] is commutative up to canonical isomorphism (the vertical arrows designating the functors fully faithful 6.8.5).
Verification is immediate from the definitions, and left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.7.3

Let \(E\) be a topos admitting enough points, then it is the same for all closed subtopic \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\). More precisely, if \((p_i)_{i \in I}\) is a conservative family of points of \(E\), then the family of points of \(\mathscr{F}\), defined by the subfamily \((p_i)_{i \in J}\) formed of \(p_i\) which come from points of \(\mathscr{F}\), is conservative. (Compare 6.7.3, 6.7.4.)

Indeed, let \(f : X \to Y\) be an arrow of \(\mathscr{F}\) transformed into an isomorphism by the fiber functors associated with the considered points of \(\mathscr{F}\). Like \(i^* i_* \approx \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}\), we see that \(i_*(f) : i_*(X) \to i_*(Y)\) is transformed into isomorphism by the fiber functors associated with \(p_i\) with \(i \in J\); it is the same for the \(p_i\) with \(i \in I - J\), i.e., coming from points of \(U\), since \(j^* i_*\) is the constant functor of value of the final object. So \(i_*(f)\) is an isomorphism, therefore also \(f \approx i^* i_*(f)\), c.q.f.d.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.7.4

If \(E\) is a topos having enough points, it is clear that an open \(U\) of \(E\) is determined when we know the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) essential image of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E_{/U})\); in fact, if \(C\) is a full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) defining a conservative family of points of \(E\), it suffices even to know the subcategory \(C_{/U}\) of the elements of \(C\) belonging to the essential image of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(U)\). From this and from 9.7.2 a) it follows that a closed subtopos \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(E\) is determined when we know the essential image of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(\mathscr{F})\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) or even just the insertion of it with \(C\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.7.5

Let \(E\) be a topos. For any subtopos \(F\) of \(E\), let \(P(F)\) be the subset of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) formed from isomorphy classes of points of \(E\) which are factorized by \(E\). Thus, \(P(F)\) is homeomorphic to \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(F)\) (9.1.8 c)). Show that if \(F\) is an open subtopic (resp. closed, resp. locally closed 9.4.9)) of \(E\), then \(P(F)\) is an open (resp. closed, resp. locally closed) part of \(E\). Show that when \(E\) has enough points, it is the same for all locally closed subtopics of \(E\), and the application \(F \mapsto P(F)\) induces a bijection between the set of open (resp. closed) subtopos, resp. locally closed) of \(E\), and all the open parts (resp. closed, resp. locally closed) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\). Generalize the previous considerations in the case where we replace \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) by any subspace \(X\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) corresponding to a conservative family of points of \(E\).
9.8. Additions to certain topos linked to topological spaces.

We indicate in this issue some additions, which will be given in the form of exercises. He The reader is advised to go through them, to get used to the point of view of the topos in various situations of classic type.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.8.1 (Descent of beams on a topological space)

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces \(\in \mathcal{U}\), hence a functor \[ f^* : \mathrm{Top}(Y) \to \mathrm{Top}(X). \]

  1. a)

    The following conditions are equivalent:

    (i) \(f^*\) is faithful.

    (ii) \(f^*\) is conservative.

    (iii) \(f(X)\) is a very dense part (EGA IV 10.1.3) of \(Y\).

    For this it is sufficient that \(f\) or \(f_{\mathrm{sob}}\) be surjective. Give an example where \(f^*\) is faithful and where \(f = f_{\mathrm{sob}}\) is not surjective (take \(X\) discrete, \(f\) injective, \(f(X)\) = set of closed points of \(Y\), \(Y\) a non-discrete Noetherian topological space). Give an example where \(f_{\mathrm{sob}}\) is surjective but not \(f\), another where \(f\) is surjective but not \(f_{\mathrm{sob}}\).

  2. b)

    Suppose \(f(X)\) is very dense in \(Y\), and its topology quotient of that of \(X\). Prove that the arrow \(f^*\) of \((\mathrm{Esp})\) is a descent morphism for the fibered category of sheaves of sets on variable spaces, i.e. that the functor \(f^*\) induces a fully faithful functor of the category \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\) in the category of objects from \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) provided with descent data relative to \(f : X \to Y\). Bringing back a case \(f\) surjective, and copy the reasoning of VIII 9.1 given in the context of flat topologies of diagrams.) Give a reciprocal.

  3. c)

    Suppose that \(f(X)\) is very dense in \(Y\), that its topology is a quotient of that of \(X\), and that the fibers of \(f\) are connected. Prove that the functor \(f^*\) is fully faithful. (Trace the reasoning of SGA 1 IX 3.4.) Give a converse, at least if the points of \(Y\) are closed.

  4. d)

    Suppose that \(Y\) is a union of openings \(Y_i\) above which \(X\) admits sections. Prove that then \(f\) is an effective descent morphism for the fibered category of set sheaves on variable topological spaces, i.e. that the functor considered in c) is even an equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.8.2 (Topos quotients of topological spaces. Topological extents)
  1. a)

    Either \[ (9.8.5.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & X_1 \ar@<0.5ex>[r]^-{p_1} \ar@<-0.5ex>[r]_-{p_2} & X_o } \] a semi-simplicial object truncated to order 2 of the category \((\mathrm{Esp})\) of topological spaces \(\in \mathcal{U}\), hence by inverse image functors a diagram of categories of sheaves \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Top}(X_o) \ar@<0.5ex>[r]^-{p_1^*} \ar@<-0.5ex>[r]_-{p_2^*} & \mathrm{Top}(X_1) \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & \mathrm{Top}(X_2) , } \] (more precisely, we have a category co-fibered on the category of simplex-types \(\Delta_n\), \(0 \le n \le 2\)). Show that the category \(\varprojlim\) of this diagram (i.e. the category of sheaves of sets on \(X_o\), munis a descent data relative to the diagram (9.8.6.1), i.e. an isomorphism \(p_1^*(F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } p_2^*(F)\) such as ...), is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(T\). (For a more general statement of stability of topos by operations \(\varprojlim\), cf VI) Define a topos morphism \(q : \mathrm{Top}(X_o) \to T\), and prove that for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(T,E)\) is equivalent via \(q^*\) to the category \(\varprojlim\) of the following category diagram, deduced from (9.8.2.1): \[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathrm{Top}(X_o),E) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathrm{Top}(X_1),E) \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathrm{Top}(X_2),E) . } \]

  2. b)

    In particular, let \(R\) be an equivalence relation in an object \(X\) of \((\mathrm{Esp})\), i.e. (I 10) a subobject \(R\) of \(X \times X\) (N.B. the inclusion \(R \hookrightarrow X \times X\) is continuous, but the topology of \(R\) is not necessarily induced by that of \(X \times X\)), such that for any object \(Y\) of \((\mathrm{Esp})\), \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,R)\) is the graph of a equivalence relation in \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\). We deduce a diagram of the form (9.8.2.1), with \(X_o = X, X_1 = R, X_2 = (R,p_2) \times_X (R,p_1)\), where \(p_1, p_2\) are the two projections of \(R\) into \(X\), hence a topos, which we will note \(\mathrm{Top}(X)/R\), or even \(\mathrm{Top}(X/R)\), or even \(X/R\), by abuse of notations, and which plays the role of a quotient of \(X\) by \(R\), in a sense specified by a). Suppose that the topology of \(R\) is induced by that of \(X \times X\), and that, denoting by \(X/R\) the ordinary quotient topological space, \(X\) locally admits sections on \(X/R\), prove that then \(T\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X/R)\). (Use 9.8.1 d)).

  3. c)

    Let \(H \to X\) be an injective morphism of topological groups, i.e. a monomorphism of objects groups in \((\mathrm{Esp})\), and let \(R\) be the equivalence relation that it defines in \(X\), i.e. \(R = H \times H\), with \(p_1 = \mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(p_2\) defined by the action of \(H\) on \(X\) via left translations. Show that so that the topology of \(R\) is induced by that of \(X \times X\), it is necessary and sufficient that the topology of \(H\) is induced by that of \(X\). Give examples where this condition is not met, and where the ordinary quotient topology of \(X/H\) is the topology coarse with a) \(H = \mathbf{Z} \times \mathbf{Z}\), \(X = \mathbf{R}\), and b) \(H = \mathbf{R}\), \(X = \mathbf{T} \times \mathbf{T}\), with \(\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{R}/\mathbf{Z}\), (“geodesics of the torus”). Prove that the two topos obtained are equivalent and are not equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X/H)\) (which is a final topos (2.2), nor to any topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(Y)\).

  4. d)

    Let \(G\) be a topological group operating on a topological space \(X\), hence in a good way known a semi-simple object \[ \xymatrix{ \ldots\ G \times G \times X \ar@<1ex>[r] \ar[r] \ar@<-1ex>[r] & G \times X \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & X . } \] Show that the topos \(T\) which is deduced by virtue of a) is identified with the category of spaces \(X'\) with group of \(G\) operators above \(X\), such that \(X' \to X\) is a spreading (compatible with the action of \(G\)). Especially, when \(G\) is discrete, we find the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) of 2.3.

    The above considerations justify the notation \(\mathrm{Top}(X)/G\), or even \(\mathrm{Top}(X/G)\) or even simply \(X/G\), for the previous topos \(T\). We will pay attention, however, that when \(G\) is a discrete group (to fix the ideas) operating properly on \(X\), so that the ordinary quotient space \(X/G\) has properties quite reasonable, the natural topos morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Top}(X/G)\) deduced from the universal characterization a) of \(T\) is not an equivalence of topos only if \(G\) operates freely i.e. without fixed points, i.e. when \(G \times X \to X \times X\) is a monomorphism; therefore in the case of a "pre-equivalence relation (or "groupoid" in the sense of (SGA 3 V 1) which is not an equivalence relation, the notion of passage to the quotient in the sense of the topos (or "passage to the fine quotient") does not generally correspond (via the correspondence \(X \to \mathrm{Top}(Y)\)) to the transition to the quotient usual topology.

  5. e)

    Let \(T\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

    i) There exists a family \((S_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(T\) covering the final object of \(T\), such that for all \(i \in I\), the induced topos \(T_{/S_i}\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), with \(X \in (\mathrm{Esp})\).

    i') There exists a \(S \in \operatorname{Ob} T\) covering the final object such that the induced topos \(T_{/S}\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\).

    (ii) There exists a topological space \(X\), and an equivalence pre-relation \(R\) in \(X\) (in the sense of the category \((\mathrm{Esp})\)) which is spread (i.e. such that \(p_1 : R \to X\) is a spreading), such that \(T\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X/R)\), where the notations are those of b).

    We will then say that the topos \(T\) is locally a topological space, or that \(T\) is an extent topological (or simply an extent, if no confusion is to be feared).

  6. f)

    Show that the topos \(T\) constructed in a) has enough points and more precisely, that it admits a conservative family of points parameterized by the (small) set \(X_o\). In particular, any extent has a small conservative family of points, therefore has enough points. When a scope \(T\) is made in the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X/R)\) as in e) ii), prove that every point of \(T\) is isomorphic to the image of a point of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), therefore is defined by an ordinary point of \(X_{\mathrm{sob}}\) (or even of \(X\), when \(X\) is sober).

  7. g)

    Show that the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X/G)\) of 2.3 is an extent. (Use its description d) or 7.1.10 c)) Show that for all \(x \in X\), the monoid of endomorphisms of the point of \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G) = \mathrm{Top}(X)/G\) defined by \(x\) is canonically isomorphic to the stability group \(G_x\) of \(x\). In particular, the points of a extended can have non-trivial automorphism groups. Determine \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(T)\) for extent \(T\) defined by an equal equivalence prerelation in a space \(X\), and show that any endomorphism of a point of \(T\) is an automorphism.

  8. h)

    Let \(T\) be a topos. Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

    i) For any point \(p\) of \(T\), any endomorphism of \(p\) (resp. any automorphism of \(p\)) is the identity.

    ii) For any topos \(S\) having sufficient points, and any topos morphism \(f : S \to T\), all endomorphism of \(f\) (resp. any automorphism of \(f\)) is the identity.

    Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

    i') For two points \(p,q\) of \(T\), there exists at most one morphism from \(p\) into \(q\).

    ii') For two morphisms \(f,g : S \rightrightarrows T\) of a topos \(S\) having sufficient of points in \(T\), there exists at most one morphism of \(f\) in \(g\).

    When these last conditions are verified, we say that the topos \(T\) is punctually rigid, or simply rigid. Show that if \(X\) is a topological space provided with an equal equivalence pre-relation \(R\), the quotient range \(T = \mathrm{Top}(X)/R\) is rigid if and only if \(R\) is an equivalence relation.

  9. i)

    Let \((X,G)\) be a topological space with a discrete group of operators, \(H\) be a distinguished subgroup of \(G\) such that the induced operation of \(H\) on \(X\) is clean and free, and are \(X' = X/H\), \(G' = G/H\), so that \(G'\) operates on \(X'\) in an obvious way. Prove that the morphism of topos \[ \mathrm{Top}(X,G) \to \mathrm{Top}(X',G') \] induced by the canonical morphism \((X,G) \to (X',G')\) of spaces with operators (4.12) is an equivalence of topos.

    Show that the set of openings of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X/G)\) is identified with the set of openings of \(X\) stable by the action of \(G\), or even to the set of open spaces of the topological quotient space \(X/G\). In conclude that \(\mathrm{Top}(X/G)\) is connected if and only if any part of \(X\) that is both open and closed and stable under the action of \(G\) is empty or equal to \(X\). When the related components of \(X\) are opened, \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) is connected if and only if \(G\) operates transitively on the set \(\pi_o(X)\) of the connected components of \(X\); more generally \(\pi_o(\mathrm{Top}(X))\) (8.7 g) is an essentially constant proset isomorphic to the set quotient \(\pi_o(X)/G\).

    Show that when \(X\) is locally connected and locally simply connected, and \(T = \mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) related i.e. \(G\) transitive on \(\pi_o(X)\), then among all the ways to realize \(T\) using a space operators \((X',G')\) (see above for some such ways), there is one, unique to non-unique isomorphism close, for which \(X'\) is connected and simply connected. Show that the choice of such a \((X',G')\) amounts to the choice of a "universal covering" of the final object of \(T\), and that \(G'\) is isomorphic to the group \(\pi_1(T)\) relating to this universal coating (see 2.7.5).

    (Hint: show that the data of an equivalence of \(T\) with a \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\) amounts to the data of a \(G_T\)-torsor \(P\) in \(T\), and if \((X,G)\) and \(P\) match, we have a canonical equivalence \(\mathrm{Top}(X) = T_{/P}\).)

    Conclude from this a trivial proof of the last assertion of c). Characterize the topos \(T\) equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X,G)\), where \(G\) is a discrete group operating on a locally connected topological space and locally simply connected by transitively permuting the connected components, as being the connected, locally connected and locally simply connected extents including the universal covering \(P\) of the final object \(e_T\) of \(T\) is such that the induced topos \(T_{/P}\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (or, as we will simply say by abuse of language \(T_{/P}\) "is" a topological space).

    Give an example of an extent, locally isomorphic to \(\mathbf{R}\), which is connected and simply connected but which is not a topological space. (Take the universal covering from the right \(\mathbf{R}\) with origin split.)

  10. j)

    A ringed topos \((T,\mathcal{O}_T)\) (cf. §11) is called a differentiable extent (resp. a extent real analytics. resp. a complex analytical scope) if there are objects \(S_i\) of \(T\) covering the final object, such that the induced ringed topos \((T_{/S_i}, \mathcal{O}_T/S_i)\) is equivalent to the ringed topos defined by a variety differentiable with its bundle of real functions \(C^\infty\) (resp. to the ringed topos defined by an analytical space real, resp. to the ringed topos defined by a complex analytical space). Give a constructive description of these ringed topos, of the type of e) ii) (where we will take for \(X\) respectively a differentiable variety, a space complex analytic or a real analytic space). Give examples of such ringed topos which are not equivalent to ringed topos associated with topological spaces, using the examples considered in c).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.8.3 (Topos associated with local equivalence relations and foliations)(∗)

Let \(X\) be a topological space.

  1. a)

    For all open \(U\) of \(X\), let \(\mathrm{Quot}(U)\) be the set of equivalence relations in \(U\), and for an open \(V \subset U\), consider the natural map \(\mathrm{Quot}(U) \to \mathrm{Quot}(V)\), hence a prebundle \(\mathrm{Quot}_X\) on \(X\). Let \(Q_X\) or simply \(Q\) be the associated sheaf, and let \(r\) be a section of \(Q\) ("local equivalence relation on \(X\)"). From the order relation on the set \(\mathrm{Quot}(U)\) of equivalence relations on an open \(U\) of \(X\), deduce on the pre-sheaf \(\mathrm{Quot}\), and on the associated beam \(Q\), an order structure i.e. a structure of pre-beam resp. of value sheaves in the category of ordered sets.

  2. b)

    Consider, for any equivalence relation \(R\) on \(X\), the section \(\mathrm{loc}(R)\) of \(Q\) that it defines. Let \(E(r)\) be the set of equivalence relations on \(X\) such that \(\mathrm{loc}(R)\) is less fine than \(r\), and let \(\mathrm{glob}(r)\) the upper bound equivalence relation of \(E(r)\), (whose graph is the intersection of the graphs of \(R \in E(r)\)). Let \((U_x)_{x \in X}\) be a family of open neighborhoods of \(x \in X\), and for all \(x \in X\) be \(R_x\) a equivalence relation in \(U_x\) whose germ in \(x\) is \(r_x\). For any family \(\underline{V}\) of open \(V_x\) (\(x \in X, x \in V_x \subset U_x\)), i.e. \(R_{\underline{V}}\) the equivalence relation in \(X\) generated by the family of relations \(R_x|V_x\). Show that if \(\underline{V}' \le \underline{V}\) (in an obvious sense) we have \(R_{\underline{V}'} \ge R_{\underline{V}}\), and that \(\mathrm{glob}(r)\) is the upper bound of the growing filtering family of equivalence relations \(R_{\underline{V}}\). Give an example where \(\mathrm{glob}(r)\) is not in \(E(r)\), i.e. where there exists a \(a \in X\) such that, for any open neighborhood \(W \subset U_a\) of \(a\), it exists one \(\underline{V} = (V_x)\) and two points \(y,z\) of \(W\) which are equivalent for \(R_a\), but which are not equivalent for \(R_{\underline{V}}\). Show that for all \(R \in \mathrm{Quot}(X)\) we have \(\mathrm{glob}\,\mathrm{loc}(R) \ge R\).

  3. c)

    We say that \(r\) is a preconsistent local equivalence relation (resp. consistent) if \(\mathrm{glob}(r) \in E(r)\) i.e. \(\mathrm{loc}(\mathrm{glob}(r)) \le r\) (resp. if for all open \(U\) of \(X\), the restriction of \(r\) to \(U\) is pre-consistent). We say that \(r\) is globally coherent if it is coherent, and if, in addition, \(r = \mathrm{loc}\,\mathrm{glob}(r)\). We say that an equivalence relation \(R\) on \(X\) is locally coherent if \(r = \mathrm{loc}(R)\) is coherent, consistent if moreover \(R = \mathrm{glob}(r)\) i.e. \(R = \mathrm{glob}(\mathrm{loc}(R))\). Show that the equivalence relations globally coherent \(R\) on \(X\) correspond one-to-one to the coherent equivalence relations on \(X\), by \(r \mapsto \mathrm{glob}(r)\) and \(R \mapsto \mathrm{loc}(R)\) ;

  4. d)

    Show that for \(r\) to be consistent it is sufficient that for all \(a \in X\) and all open neighborhoods \(W' \subset U_a\) of \(a\), there exists an open neighborhood \(W \subset W'\) of \(a\), such that any equivalence class of \(R_a|W\) is contained in a connected component of an equivalence class of \(R_a|W'\) (a fortiori, it is sufficient that there exists a fundamental family of open neighborhoods \(W_a \subset U_a\) of \(a\) such as the fibers of the equivalence relations induced \(R_a|W_a\) are related). (Hint: boils down to establishing pre-consistency in the case where \(r\) is defined by a \(R \in \mathrm{Quot}(X)\), and note in this case that the fibers of the equivalence relations \(R_{\underline{V}}\) are parts relatively open - therefore also relatively closed - fibers of \(R\)). Show that for a relationship global equivalence \(R\) is coherent, it suffices that it is locally coherent and that its fibers are related; prove that this condition is necessary if the fibers are closed. Give an example of a equivalence relation with connected and locally connected fibers, and which is not locally coherent (?).

  5. e)

    Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a continuous application. Define a natural prebundle homomorphism \(f_*(\mathrm{Quot}_{X'}) \to \mathrm{Quot}_X\), inducing a homomorphism of sheaves \(f_*(Q_{X'}) \to Q_X\) hence \(f^*(Q_X) \to Q_{X'}\). If \(r\) is a local equivalence relation on \(X\), we say that \(f\) is a "fiber application" for the relation of local equivalence \(r\) on \(X\), if the inverse image of \(r\) is the coarse local equivalence relation on \(X'\). Either, for any topological space \(X'\), \(\mathrm{Homfib}_r(X',X)\) the set of continuous applications of \(X'\) in \(X\) which are fiber applications in relation to \(r\). Show that for \(X'\) variable, we obtain a counterfunctor in \(X'\).

  6. f)

    Assume \(r\) consistent. Show that the previous functor is representable by a space topological \(X^r\) above \(X\). Show that the universal fiber application \(\varphi : X^r \to X\) is bijective, and that all \(x \in X^r\) admits an open neighborhood \(U\) such that the map \(U \to X\) induced by \(\varphi\) is a homeomorphism of \(U\) on his image. Show that the connected components \(X^r\) are open and correspond by the bijection \(\varphi\) to the fibers of the equivalence relation \(R = \mathrm{glob}(r)\). Give an example where the restriction of \(\varphi\) to components related of \(X^r\) does not induce homeomorphisms of these spaces with their images in \(X\).

  7. g)

    The local equivalence relation \(r\) is said to be open if it is a section of the sub-sheaf \(Q^{\mathrm{ouv}}_X\) of \(Q\) coming from the sub-prebundle \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\mathrm{ouv}}_X\) of \(\mathrm{Quot}_X\) whose open-all value \(U\) of \(X\) is the set of open equivalence relations of \(U\). We will say that the local equivalence relation \(r\) is strictly open if it is coherent and open or what amounts to the same thing, if it is coherent and if for all open \(U\) of \(X\), \(\mathrm{glob}(r|U)\) is an open equivalence relation in \(U\). It is said that the relationship equivalence \(R\) in \(X\) is strictly open if it is consistent and if \(\mathrm{loc}(R)\) is a relation of strictly open local equivalence. Prove that for \(r\) supposed open to be strictly open, it it is enough that it satisfies the condition envisaged in d); if \(r\) is locally defined by a \(R\) with closed fibers, then this condition is also necessary.

  8. h)

    Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\), For all open \(U\) of \(X\), let \(\mathrm{Quot}(U,F)\) be the set pairs formed by an equivalence relation \(R_U\) in \(U\) and an equivalence relation \(R_{F|U}\) in \(F|U\) (\(F\) being interpreted as space spread over \(X\)) such that the structural morphism \(p : F|U \to U\) is compatible with the equivalence relations \(R_U\), \(R_{F|U}\), and that the corresponding diagram of topological spaces \[ \xymatrix{ F|U \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{p} & (F|U)/R_{F|U} \ar[d]^-{q} \\ U \ar[r] & U/R_U } \] is Cartesian with \(q\) a spreading (so that \(F|U\) is identified with the inverse image of the beam \((F|U)/R_{F|U}\) on \(U/R_U\)). Show that the \(\mathrm{Quot}(U,F)\) for \(U\) variable define a pre-beam on \(X\), of which the associated beam will be noted \(Q(F/X)\). For a given section \(r\) of \(Q_X\), we call \(r\)-structure on the beam \(F\) any section of \(Q(F/X)\) above the given section \(r\) of \(Q_X\). Set category \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) of \(r\)-sheaves on \(X\), and define a conservative and faithful functor: "forgetting the \(r\)-structure" \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\). Prove that in \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) finite projective limits and finite inductive limits are representable, and that the previous functor commutes at said limits. Conclude that in \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) the finite sums are disjoint and universal and the equivalence relations are effectively universal. Prove that \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) admits a small generating family. Give an example with consistent \(r\), where \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) does not admit not infinite direct sums (?), therefore is not a topos.

  9. i)

    Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous application compatible with \(R = \mathrm{glob}(r)\). Define a functor \[ f_r^* : \mathrm{Top}(Y) \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r) \] whose compound with the inclusion functor \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\) is \(f^*\). Show that if \(r\) is globally consistent and strictly open (i.e. if \(R\) is strictly open and \(r = \mathrm{loc}(R)\)), and if \(f\) is the application canonical \(X \to Y = X/R\), then \(f_r^*\) is an equivalence of categories, therefore \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\) is the inverse image functor of a topos morphism.

  10. j)

    Conclude from i) that if \(r\) is strictly open, then \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) is a topos, and the functor inclusion \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r) \to \mathrm{Top}(X)\) is the inverse image functor associated with a topos morphism \[ p : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r) . \]

  11. k)

    Define a functorial law of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) and the previous topos morphism \(p\), by relation to the couple \((X,r)\) of a topological space \(X\) provided with a strictly open local equivalence relation. Show that if \(X' \to X\) is a spread and if \(r'\) is the inverse image of \(r\) in \(X'\), then the morphism induces \(\mathrm{Top}(X'/r') \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) is equivalent to a location morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)_{/E} \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r)\), where \(E\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) (determined up to unique isomorphism). For \(E\) to cover the final object of \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\), it It is necessary and sufficient that the saturated under \(R = \mathrm{glob}(r)\) of the image of \(X'\) in \(X\) is equal to \(X\).

  12. l)

    Deduce from k) that \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) is a rigid extension (9.8.2 b)). Show that if \(X\) is sober the set of point isomorphy classes of \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) is homeomorphic, for its canonical topology ( ) to the quotient topological space \(X/\mathrm{glob}(r)\), and that for two points of \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\), coming from points \(x\) and \(x'\) of \(X\), there is at most one morphism from one to the other; there is indeed one if and only if \(x'\) and \(x\) are mod equivalents \(\mathrm{glob}(r)\) at points \(x'_1\) and \(x_1\) such that \(x_1\) generates \(x'_1\).

  13. m)

    Study the canonical topos morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r)\), noting that for any object \(U\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) such that the topos induced on \(U\) "be" an ordinary topological space, the induced morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X)_{/p^*(U)} \to \mathrm{Top}(X/r)_{/U}\) "is" a continuous map of ordinary topological spaces \(X_U \to U\). Show that the fibers of the map \(X_U \to U\) are homeomorphic to connected components of the space \(X^r\) by \(f\).

  14. n)

    Let \(X\) be a differentiable manifold provided with a foliation, i.e. a sub-sheaf \(F\) locally direct factor of the tangent beam of \(X\), stable by brackets. Set to \(X\) a local equivalence relation strictly open associated with foliation, and define on the quotient topos \(T = \mathrm{Top}(X/r)\) a Ring which makes it a differentiable extent (9.8.2. j)). Define an equivalence of the category of locally free Modules on the differentiable extent \(T\) (also called differentiable vector bundles on \(T\)), and the category of Modules locally free on \(X\) provided with a connection relative to the given sub-fiber \(F\) of the tangent bundle. Give variants in the real analytical and complex analytical case.

footnotes.html: (∗) See however 9.1.11. b) below for the locally Noetherian case \(X\).
(∗) For a more general joining statement than 9.5.5, cf. exercise.
(∗) This exercise is given without reservation, having been hastily written and insufficiently verified. Mr. N. SAAVEDRA verified parts a) to e). Please send us any observations you may have.

blocks.html: 10. Morphism bundles

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 10.1

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(X\) and \(Y\) two objects of \(E\); the functor \(Z \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z \times X,Y) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/Z}}(X_Z,Y_Z)\) is representable.

Indeed, inductive limits are universal in \(E\) (II 4.3). The functor \(Z \mapsto Z \times X\) commutes therefore at the inductive limits. Consequently, the functor \(Z \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z \times X,Y)\) transforms the inductive limits of the argument \(Z\) in projective limits. It is therefore representable (IV 1.4 and 1.2).

The object representing the functor \(Z \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z \times X,Y)\) is denoted \(\mathscr{H}om_E(X,Y)\) (or more simply \(\mathscr{H}om(X,Y)\)) and is called the bundle of morphisms from \(X\) into \(Y\). It is a bifunctor in \(X\) and \(Y\). We therefore have a trifunctional isomorphism \[ (10.2.1.) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z,\mathscr{H}om_E(X,Y)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z \times X,Y) . \] It then follows from formula (10.2.1) that the bifunctor \((X,Y) \mapsto \mathscr{H}om(X,Y)\) transforms the inductive limits from the argument \(X\) (resp. the projective limits of the argument \(Y\)) to projective limits in \(E\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 10.3

Let \(v : E \to E'\) be a topos morphism. For a variable object \(X\) of \(E\) and a variable object \(Y\) of \(E'\), we have a bifunctional isomorphism. \[ (10.3.1) \qquad v_* \mathscr{H}om_E(v^*Y,X) \approx \mathscr{H}om_{E'}(Y,v_*X) . \]

Demonstration. For any object \(Z\) of \(E'\), we have a sequence of isomorphisms, functorial in all arguments: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,v_*\mathscr{H}om_E(v^*Y,X)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*Z,\mathscr{H}om_E(v^*Y,X)) & \text{(adjonction)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*Z,\mathscr{H}om_E(v^*Y,X)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*Z \times v^*Y,X) & (10.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*Z \times v^*Y,X) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*(Z \times Y),X) & (v^*\text{ exact à gauche})\\ \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*(Z \times Y),X) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z \times Y,v_*X) & \text{(adjonction)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z \times Y,v_*X) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,\mathscr{H}om_{E'}(Y,v_*X)) & (10.2.1) \end{aligned} \] The two members of (10.3.1) therefore represent isomorphic functors, c.q.f.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(X\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundle on \(C\), \(Y\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle on \(C\), \(\mathcal{V}\) a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(C\) is \(\mathcal{V}\)-small, \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the topos of the \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves on \(C\), \(\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the topos of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(C\). The pre-bundle \(\mathscr{H}om_{\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(X,Y)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle. Either \(X \to aX\) the canonical morphism of \(X\) in its associated bundle. The corresponding morphism \(\mathscr{H}om_{\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(aX,Y) \to \mathscr{H}om_{\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(X,Y)\) is an isomorphism. We have a canonical isomorphism \(\mathscr{H}om_{\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(aX,Y) \approx \mathscr{H}om_{\widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}}}(aX,Y)\).

Let's first assume that \(C\) is a small site and that \(\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{U}\). We then have a topos morphism: \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) (IV 4.8), hence (10.3.1) the assertions in this case. To move on to the general case, we note first of all that the "associated bundle" functor does not depend on the universe (II 3.6) and that the topos of \(\mathcal{V}\)-beams on \(C\) is equivalent to the topos of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) (III 4 and IV 1). It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 10.4.2

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\), \(\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the topos of \(\mathcal{V}\)-beams on \(E\), \(X\) and \(Y\) two objects from \(E\). There is a canonical isomorphism \(\mathscr{H}om_E(X,Y) \approx \mathscr{H}om_{\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}}(X,Y)\).

The functor \(\varepsilon_E : E \to \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) is fully faithful and left-exact. Consequently we have, for everything object \(Z\) of \(E\) an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}}(\varepsilon_E Z, \varepsilon_E \mathscr{H}om_E(X,Y)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}}(\varepsilon_E Z \times \varepsilon_E X, \varepsilon_E Y) \] But every object of \(\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) is an inductive limit of objects coming from \(E\), hence (10.2.1) the assertion.

Let \(v : E \to E'\) be a topos morphism. We propose to define four morphisms bifunctional. \[ \begin{aligned} \Phi_v &: v^* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y) \to \mathscr{H}om(v^*X,v^*Y) , && X \text{ et } Y \text{ objets de } E' ,\\ \Psi_v &: v_* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y) \to \mathscr{H}om(v_*X,v_*Y) , && X \text{ et } Y \text{ objets de } E ,\\ \Xi_v &: \mathscr{H}om(v_!X,Y) \to v_* \mathscr{H}om(X,v^*Y) , && X \in \operatorname{ob} E , \ Y \in \operatorname{ob} E' ,\\ \Lambda_v &: v_!(X \times v^*Y) \to v_!(X) \times Y , && X \in \operatorname{ob} E , \ Y \in \operatorname{ob} E' , \end{aligned} \] where, to define the morphisms \(\Xi_v\) and \(\Lambda_v\) we suppose that the functor \(v^*\) reciprocal image by \(v\) admits a deputy left \(v_!\).
(10.5.1) Definition of \(\Psi_v\).

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\). We have an addition morphism \(v^*v_*X \to X\), hence, for any object \(Z\) of \(E'\), a bifunctional morphism \(v^*(Z \times v_*X) \to (v^*Z) \times X\). We therefore have, for any object \(Y\) of \(E\), a morphism trifunctional \[ \mathrm{Hom}_E((v^*Z) \times X,Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*(Z \times v_*X),Y) . \] We deduce, by addition, a trifunctional morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,v_* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,\mathscr{H}om(v_*X,v_*Y)) ; \] hence the morphism \(\Psi_v\).
(10.5.2) Definition of \(\Lambda_v\).

For any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have an addition morphism \(X \to v^*v_!X\); hence, for any object \(Y\) of \(E\), a bifunctional morphism \(X \times v^*Y \to v^*v_!(X) \times v^*Y \approx v^*(v_!(X) \times Y)\); hence, by addition, the morphism \(\Lambda_v\).

Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \(Z\) and \(Y\) two objects of \(E'\). Morphism \(\Lambda_v : v_!(X \times v^*Z) \to v_!(X) \times Z\) provides a trifunctional morphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(v_!(X) \times Z,Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(v_!(X \times v^*Z),Y)\); hence, by addition, a trifunctional morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,\mathscr{H}om(v_!X,Y)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,v_* \mathscr{H}om(X,v^*Y)) ; \] hence the morphism \(\Xi_v\).
(10.5.4). Definition of \(\Phi_v\).

The trifunctorial morphism of (10.5.3) \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(v_!(Z) \times X,Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(v_!(Z \times v^*X),Y) ; \] where \(Z\) is an object of \(E\) and \(X\), \(Y\) are objects of \(E'\), allows to obtain, by addition, a morphism trifunctional, \[ \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z,v^* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z,\mathscr{H}om(v^*X,v^*Y)) ; \] hence a definition of the morphism \(\Phi_v\). There is a second way to define \(\Phi_v\). Let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be three objects from \(E'\). The functor \(v^* : E' \to E\) provides a trifunctor morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z \times X,Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(v^*(Z) \times v^*(X),v^*y) ; \] hence, by addition, a trifunctional morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,\mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{E'}(Z,v_* \mathscr{H}om(v^*X,v^*Y)) . \] We therefore have a bifunctional morphism \[ \mathscr{H}om(X,Y) \to v_* \mathscr{H}om(v^*X,v^*Y) ; \] hence, by addition, a morphism which we leave to the reader to verify that it is indeed the morphism \(\Phi_v\) defined above.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 10.6

Let \(v : E \to E'\) be a topos morphism.

  1. 1)

    The morphism \(\Psi_v\) (10.5.1) is an isomorphism for any object \(Y\) of \(E\) if and only if the addition morphism \(v^*v_*X \to X\) is an isomorphism. In particular, the morphism \(\Psi_v\) is an isomorphism for any pair of objects \((X,Y)\) if and only if the functor \(v_* : E \to E'\) is fully faithful, i.e. if \(v\) is a topos embedding.

  2. 2)

    The following conditions are equivalent:

    (i) For any pair \((X,Y)\) of objects of \(E'\), the morphism \(\Phi_v\) (10.5.4) is an isomorphism.

    (ii) The functor \(v^*\) admits a left adjoint \(v_!\) and for every object \(X\) of \(E\) and every object \(Y\) of \(E'\), the morphism \(\Xi_v\) (10.5.3) is an isomorphism.

    (iii) The functor \(v^*\) admits a left adjoint \(v_!\) and for any object \(X\) of \(E\) and for any object \(Y\) of \(E'\), the morphism \(\Lambda_v\) (10.5.2) is an isomorphism.

The first assertion follows immediately from (10.5.1). It also follows immediately from (10.5.2.), (10.5.3), (10.5.4) that (iii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ((i) and the functor \(v^*\) admits a left adjoint \(v_!\)). It therefore remains to show that (i) implies that \(v^*\) admits an adjoint on the left, i.e. (IV 1.8) that (i) implies that \(v^*\) switches to small products. Let \(e'\) be the final object of \(E'\) and \(I\) be a small set. Since \(v^*\) is correct at left, \(v^*(e')\) is a final object of \(E\) and like \(v^*\) commutes at the inductive limits \(v^*(\coprod_I e') \approx \coprod_I v^*(e')\). For any object \(Y\) of \(E'\), we have \(\mathscr{H}om(\coprod_I e',Y) \approx \prod_I \mathscr{H}om(e',Y) \approx \prod_I Y\) and \(\mathscr{H}om(\coprod_I v^*(e'),v^*Y) \approx \prod_I \mathscr{H}om(v^*(e'),v^*Y) \approx \prod_I v^*Y\). The functorial morphism \(\Phi_v\) induces therefore an isomorphism \(v^* \prod_I Y \approx \prod_I v^*Y\) which we verify is the canonical isomorphism. c.q.f.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10.7

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(Z\) be an object of \(E\), \(j_Z : E_{/Z} \to E\) the location morphism (IV 5.2), \(X,Y\) two objects from \(E\).

  1. 1)

    There exists a bifunctional isomorphism \[ j_Z^* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y) \approx \mathscr{H}om(j_Z^*X,j_Z^*Y) . \]

  2. 2)

    Let \(X'\) be an object of \(E/Z\). There is a bifunctional isomorphism \[ \mathscr{H}om_E(j_{Z!}X',Y) \approx j_{Z*} \mathscr{H}om_{E_{/Z}}(X',j_Z^*Y) . \]

  3. 3)

    Let \(Y'\) be an object of \(E/Z\). When \(Z\) is an open of \(E\), there is an isomorphism bifunctional \[ j_{Z*} \mathscr{H}om(X',Y') \approx \mathscr{H}om(j_{Z*}X', j_{Z*}Y') . \]

Assertions 1) and 2) are proven by noting that the morphism \(\Lambda_{j_Z}\) is an isomorphism. For assertion 3), it suffices to note that \(j_{Z*}\) is fully faithful, because \(j_{Z!}\) is fully faithful (I 5.7. a)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10.8

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(X\), \(Y\) and \(Z\) three objects of \(E\), \(j_Z : E_{/Z} \to E\) the morphism of location.

  1. 1)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \[ j_{Z*}j_Z^* Y \approx \mathscr{H}om(Z,Y) . \]

  2. 2)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z,\mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/Z}}(j_Z^* X, j_Z^* Y) . \]

Let \(e_Z\) be the final object of \(E_{/Z}\) (i.e. the object \(\mathrm{id}_Z : Z \to Z\)). It follows from (10.2.1) that we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathscr{H}om_{E_{/Z}}(e_Z,j_Z^* Y) \approx j_Z^* Y ; \] hence, according to (10.7.2), an isomorphism \[ j_{Z*}j_Z^* Y \approx \mathscr{H}om(j_{Z!}e_Z,Y) . \] Like \(j_{Z!}e_Z = Z\), we demonstrated 1). Let us demonstrate 2). From (10.7), we derive a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/Z}}(e_Z,j_Z^* \mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/Z}}(j_Z^*X,j_Z^*Y) ; \] hence, by addition on the first member, \[ \mathrm{Hom}_E(Z,\mathscr{H}om(X,Y)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/Z}}(j_Z^*X,j_Z^*Y) . \]

pages.title: 11. Ringed topos, localization in ringed topos

blocks.html: 11. Ringed topos, localization in ringed topos

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. We call \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed topos a pair \((E,A)\) where \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and \(A\) a object with a ring structure. We call \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed site a pair \((C,A)\) where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(A\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ring bundles on \(C\). The universe is not mentioned when the context is not confusing. To a ringed site \((C,A)\) is associated with the ringed topos \((\widetilde{C},A)\). A ringed topos \((E,A)\) is associated with the ringed site constituted by the site \(E\) and the bundle of rings represented by \(A\).

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos. We note \({}_A E\) (resp. \(E_A\)) the category of bundles of \(A\)-modules (we will also write \(A\)-Modules) on the left (resp. right) units. The category \({}_A E\) (resp. \(E_A\)) is a abelian category (II 6.7). Let \(M\) and \(N\) be two bundles of \(A\)-modules on the left (resp. on the right). The group commutative of the morphisms of \(A\)-Modules of \(M\) in \(N\) is denoted \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,N)\).

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be three rings of a topos \(E\), \(M\) a bundle of \(A\)-\(B\) bimodules, \(N\) a bundle of \(A\)-\(C\) bimodules on the left. Let \(e\) be a final object of \(E\) and set \(\mathrm{Hom}(e,B) = \Gamma(B)\), \(\mathrm{Hom}(e,C) = \Gamma(C)\). The structure of \(A\)-\(B\) bimodule of \(M\) provides a homomorphism of rings of \(\Gamma(B)^\circ\) in the ring of endomorphisms of the \(A\)-module \(M\) and similarly, the structure of \(A\)-\(C\) bimodule of \(N\) provides a homomorphism of rings of \(\Gamma(C)^\circ\) in the ring of endomorphisms of \(A\)-module \(N\). We deduce, by functoriality, a structure of \(\Gamma(B)\)-\(\Gamma(C)\) bimodule on the commutative group \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,N)\). In particular, when \(A\) is a bundle of commutative rings, the group \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,N)\) is canonically provided with a structure of \(\Gamma(A)\)-module.

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(A\) and \(B\) two rings of \(E\), \(u : A \to B\) a morphism of sheaves of rings, \(M\) a \(B\)-module (on the left to fix the ideas). The beam \(M\) can be considered as a beam of \(A\)-modules via \(u\): For any object \(X\) of \(E\), \(M(X)\) is provided with the structure of \(A(X)\)-module deduced from its \(B(X)\)-module structure and from the homomorphism \(u(X) : A(X) \to B(X)\) by restriction of scalars. We thus obtain a functor “restriction of scalars by \(u\)” \[ \mathrm{Res}(u) : {}_B E \to {}_A E . \]

In particular when \(A = \mathbf{Z}\) (constant beam \(\mathbf{Z}\) i.e. beam associated with constant pre-beam \(\mathbf{Z}\)) and when \(u : \mathbf{Z} \to B\) is the unique canonical morphism, we obtain a functor of \({}_B E\) in the category \({}_{\mathbf{Z}} E\) which is none other than the category of abelian sheaves denoted \(E_{\mathrm{Ab}}\). This functor is called the sheaf functor underlying abelian.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 11.1.6

The functor restriction of scalars by \(u\) commutes to the inductive limits and projectives. It is conservative.

The proposition is true for the restriction functor of scalars for ordinary modules, i.e. when \(E\) is the point topos. It is therefore true when \(E\) is the topos of the pre-beams on a small site. He then results from the determination of the inductive and projective limits using the associated beam functor (II 6.4) that the proposition is true in the general case.

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \(j_X : E_{/X} \to E\) the location morphism (IV 8). According to III 1.7 or IV 3.1.2, the beam \(j_X^*A\) is canonically equipped with a ring structure. The beam of rings \(j_X^*A\) is most often noted \(A|X\) or even, incorrectly, \(A\). Unless otherwise stated, the topos \(E_{/X}\) will be ringed by \(A|X\). The beam \(j_{X*}j_X^*A\) is canonically equipped with a ring structure. The adjunction morphism \(A \to j_{X*}j_X^*A\) is a morphism of ring bundles.

Let us also \(M\) be a \(A\)-module (on the left to fix the ideas). The beam \(j_X^* M\) is provided with a structure of \(A|X\)-module (III 1.7; or IV 3.1.2); hence a functor: \[ j_X^* : {}_A E \to {}_{A|X} E_{/X} \] called restriction functor to \(E_{/X}\), or even restriction functor to \(X\). The functor \(j_X^*\) commutes to inductive and projective limits (loc. cit.). It is particularly accurate. Now let \(N\) be a \(A|X\)-Module. The bundle \(j_{X*}N\) is a bundle of \(j_{X*}A|X\)-Modules (III 1.7 or IV 3.1.2); hence, by restriction of the scalars by the addition morphism \(A \to j_{X*}(A|X)\) (11.1.4), a \(A\)-Module also denoted \(j_{X*}N\). We therefore defined a functor \[ j_{X*} : {}_{A|X} E_{/X} \to {}_A E \] which commutes to projective limits (11.1.6 and III 1.7). For any \(A\)-Module \(M\), the addition morphism \(M \to j_{X*}j_X^*M\) is a morphism of \(A\)-Modules. For any \(A|X\)-Module \(N\) and any \(A\)-Module \(M\), the morphism addition \(M \to j_{X*}j_X^*M\) defines a bifunctional morphism in \(M\) and \(N\) \[ (11.2.2.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^* M,N) \to \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}N) . \] This last morphism is an isomorphism i.e. the functors \(j_X^*\) and \(j_{X*}\) for the \(A\)-Modules, are adjoint (III 1.7).

The functors \(j_X^*\) and \(j_{X*}\) for Modules, commute to the "underlying set" functor (III 1.7 4)). They therefore commute to the restriction functors of scalars and in particular to the sheaf functor underlying abelian.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 11.3.1

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\). The functor \[ j_X^* : {}_A E \to {}_{A|X} E_{/X} \] admits a left adjoint denoted \(j_{X!} : {}_{A|X} E_{/X} \to {}_A E\) and called the extension by zero. The functor extension by zero is exact and faithful and commutes at the inductive limits. The functors \(j_{X!}\) for the Modules commute to functors restriction of scalars and, in particular, they commute to functor underlying abelian bundle.

The existence of the functor \(j_{X!}\) results from III 1.7. As \(j_{X!}\) is a left adjoint, it switches to inductive limits (I 2). To prove the other assertions, let us first assume that \(E\) is the topos of set pre-bundles on a small category \(C\) containing the object \(X\). We then know that \(E_{/X}\) is equivalent to the category of presheaves on \(C_{/X}\), and that, modulo this equivalence, the functor \(j_X^* : E \to E_{/X}\) is none other than composition with the forgetting functor \(C_{/X} \to C\) (I 5.11). It then immediately results from the construction explicit of \(j_{X!}\) (I 5.1) that for every \(A|X\)-Module \(N\) and for every object \(Y\) of \(C\), we have \[ j_{X!}N(Y) = \bigoplus_{u \in \mathrm{Hom}_C(Y,X)} N(u) ; \] hence the exactness of \(j_{X!}\) and the fact that the extension by zero commutes to the restriction functors of the scalars in this case. In the general case, we can assume that \(E\) is the topos of the beams on a small site \(C\) and that \(X\) an object of \(C\) ((IV 1). The topos \(E_{/X}\) is then equivalent to the topos of the sheaves on \(C_{/X}\) (equipped with the topology induced) (III 5.4) and the topos morphism \(j_X : E_{/X} \to E\) comes from the forgetting functor \(C_{/X} \to C\) which is continuous and cocontinuous (III 5.2). It then follows from III 1.711) that the extension by zero for the sheaves is obtained by composing the extension by zero for the pre-sheaves with the associated beam functor; hence the assertion accuracy and switching to scalar restrictions.

To demonstrate fidelity, it amounts to the same thing to show that for \(A|X\)-Module \(N\), the morphism addition \[ \mathrm{ad}_N : N \to j_X^*j_{X!} N \] is a monomorphism. However, by noting \(j_{X!}^{\wedge}\) the functor extension by zero for the presheaves and \(a\) the "associated beam" functor, we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ N \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{ad}_N} \ar[dr]_-{\mathrm{ad}_N^{\wedge}} & j_X^* a\, j_{X!}^{\wedge} N \ar[d] \\ & a\, j_X^* j_{X!}^{\wedge} N } \] where \(\mathrm{ad}_N^{\wedge}\) is the addition morphism for the presheaves. The morphism \(\mathrm{ad}_N^{\wedge}\) is a monomorphism from the above, therefore \(a(\mathrm{ad}_N^{\wedge})\) is a monomorphism. Moreover, since the forgetting functor \(C_{/X} \to C\) is continuous and cocontinuous, the canonical morphism \(a\, j_X^* \to j_X^* a\) is an isomorphism (III 2.3). As a result \(\mathrm{ad}_N\) is a monomorphism.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 11.3.2

Let \(N\) be a \(A|X\)-Module. The overall bundle underlying \(j_{X!}N\) is not, in fact, general, isomorphic to the beam obtained by extending the overall beam through a vacuum (III 5.3 and IV 5.2) underlying \(N\). We will therefore be careful not to confuse the functor extension by zero noted \(j_{X!} : {}_{A|X} E_{/X} \to {}_A E\) in 11.3.1, and the vacuum extension functor noted again \(j_{X!} : E_{/X} \to E\) in III 5.3 and IV 5.2. In most cases encountered in practice, the abuse of notation reported above does not lead to confusion. When confusion is nevertheless possible, we will use the notations \(j_{X!}^{\mathrm{ab}}\) and \(j_{X!}^{\mathrm{ens}}\) to denote respectively the functors extension by zero and extension by the empty.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 11.3.3

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos and \(X\) be an object of \(E\). The \(A\)-Module \(j_{X!}(A|X)\), most often noted \(A_X\) or \(A_{X,E}\), is the free \(A\)-Module generated by \(X\) (II 6.5) i.e. for all \(A\)-Module \(M\), we have a canonical, functorial isomorphism in \(M\): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,M) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_A(A_X,M) \] Let \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a topologically generating family of \(E\) (II 3.0.1). The \((A_{X_i})_{i \in I}\) family is a family generator of the category of \(A\)-Modules.

Let \(e_X\) be the final object of the topos \(E_{/X}\) (\(e_X = X \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id}\ } X\)). We have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(A|X,j_X^* M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{E_{/X}}(e_X,j_X^* M) ; \] deduced from the unit section \(e_X \to A/X\); hence, by addition, an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}^{\mathrm{ab}}(A|X),M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_E(j_{X!}^{\mathrm{ens}}(e_X),M) . \] Like \(j_{X!}^{\mathrm{ens}}(e_X) = X\), we obtain the announced isomorphism. The last assertion follows from II 6.6.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 11.3.4

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be two sheaves of rings on a topos \(E\), \(X\) an object of \(E\) and \(N\) a \(A|X\)-\(B|X\)-bi Module. The abelian beam obtained by extending \(N\) by zero is canonically provided with a structure of \(A\)-\(B\)-biModule as follows immediately from its explicit description (11.3.1). In particular The spawned \(A\)-free module \(A_X\) is a \(A\)-biModule.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 11.3.5

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \(x : P \to E\) be a point of \(E\) (IV 6.1), \((x_i)_{i \in I}\) the family of points of \(E_{/X}\) above \(x\) (in one-to-one correspondence with the fiber \(X_x\) IV 6.7.2). Show that for any abelian beam \(M\) on \(E_{/X}\), the fiber \((j_{X!}M)_x\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\bigoplus_i M_{x_i}\).
12. Operation on modules

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.1

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-Modules on the left (resp. on the right). The functor on \(E\) which has any object \(X\) of \(E\) associates the commutative group \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(X,N))\) is representable by an abelian beam denoted \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)\) (or sometimes \(\mathscr{H}om(M,N)\) when no confusion arises results). For any object \(X\) of \(E\) we have a canonical isomorphism \[ (12.1.1) \qquad \mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)(X) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N). \]

We have a canonical isomorphism \(\mathscr{H}om_E(X,N) = j_{X*}j_X^* N\) (10.8) and consequently \(\mathscr{H}om_E(X,N)\) is equipped functionally in \(X\) of a \(A\)-Module structure on the left (resp. on the right). The functor \(X \to \mathscr{H}om_E(X,N)\) transforms the inductive limits of the argument \(X\) into projective limits of \(A\)-Modules (10.2 and 11.2.3) and by following the functor \(X \to \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(X,N))\) transforms the inductive limits of the argument \(X\) into limits projectives of commutative groups, therefore in projective limits of the underlying sets. It is therefore representable (IV 1.4 and 1.2) and the object which represents it is provided with an abelian sheaf structure. We have, by definition, for any object \(X\) of \(E\), a canonical isomorphism \[ (12.1.2) \qquad \mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)(X) = \mathrm{Hom}(X,\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om(X,N)) \] and the isomorphism (12.1.1) results from (12.1.2), the isomorphism \(\mathscr{H}om(X,N) \approx j_{X*}j_X^* N\) (10.8) and the addition formulas of 10.

The abelian sheaf \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)\) is called the sheaf of morphisms of \(A\)-Modules of \(M\) in \(N\). It is a bifunctor in \(M\) and \(N\). It follows from its definition and from (10.2) that it transforms the limits projective limits of the argument \(N\) (resp. the inductive limits of \(M\)) in projective limits of abelian sheaves. In in particular it is exact on the left in its two arguments.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.3

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-Modules on the right (resp. on the left), \(X\) an object of \(E\):

  1. a)

    We have canonical isomorphisms: \[ \mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)(X) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M, j_{X*}j_X^*N) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}j_X^*M,N) \]

  2. b)

    We have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \Phi_X : j_X^* \mathscr{H}om_A(M,N) \approx \mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N) \]

Let there be in addition \(P\) a \(A|X\)-Module on the right (resp. on the left):

  1. c)

    We have a canonical isomorphism: \[ j_{X*} \mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,P) \approx \mathscr{H}om_A(M,j_{X*}P) \]

  2. d)

    We have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \Xi_X : \mathscr{H}om_A(j_{X!}P,N) \approx j_{X*} \mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(P,j_X^*N) \]
The isomorphisms of a) result from (12.1.2), from the isomorphism \(\mathscr{H}om_E(X,N) \approx j_{X*}j_X^* N\) (10.8) and addition formulas of 10.

Let’s demonstrate b). For any object \(Y\) of \(E_{/X}\) we have the sequence of isomorphisms: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,j_X^* \mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(j_{X!}Y,\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)) & \text{(adjonction pour les faisceaux d'ensembles)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}(j_{X!}Y,\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(j_{X!}Y,N)) & (12.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(j_{X!}Y,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(Y,j_X^*N)) & (10.7)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(Y,j_X^*N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(Y,j_X^*N)) & (11.2.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(Y,j_X^*N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(Y,\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N)) & (12.2.1) \end{aligned} \]

Let's demonstrate c). For any object \(Y\) of \(E\), we have the sequence of isomorphisms: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,P)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(j_X^*Y,\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,P)) & \text{(adjonction)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}(j_X^*Y,\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(j_X^*M,P)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,P)) & (12.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,P)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,P)) & (11.2.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,P)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,j_{X*}P)) & (10.3)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,j_{X*}P)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(Y,\mathscr{H}om_A(M,j_{X*}P)) & (12.2.1) \end{aligned} \]

Let's demonstrate d). For any object \(Y\) of \(E\), we have the sequence of isomorphisms: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,\mathscr{H}om_A(j_{X!}P,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}P,\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,N)) & (12.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}P,\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,j_X^*\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,N)) & (11.3.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,j_X^*\mathscr{H}om_E(Y,N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,j_X^*N)) & (10.7)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,\mathscr{H}om_{E_{/X}}(j_X^*Y,j_X^*N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(j_X^*Y,\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(P,j_X^*N)) & (12.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}(j_X^*Y,\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(P,j_X^*N)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(Y,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{A|X}(P,j_X^*N)) & \text{(adjonction)} \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(A'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundle of rings on \(C\), \(M'\) be \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundle of \(A'\)-modules (on the left to fix the ideas), \(N\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundle of \(A'\)-modules (on the left). Let us denote by \(A\) the beam associated with \(A'\), so that the beams \(N\) and \(aM\) (beam associated with \(M\)) are \(A\)-Modules. The prebundle \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{A'|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N)\) is a canonically isomorphic \(\mathcal{U}\)-Abelian bundle to \(\mathscr{H}om_A(aM,N)\).

In fact it results from III 5.5. and III 2.3. that the location functor at \(C_{/X}\) commutes with the associated sheaf functors (for \(C\) and \(C_{/X}\)). As a result, we have functorial isomorphisms in the variable object \(X\) from \(C\): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{A'|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(a j_X^*M,j_X^*N) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^* aM,j_X^*N) \approx \mathscr{H}om_A(aM,N)(X) . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12.5

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(A\), \(B\), \(C\) three sheaves of rings on \(E\), \(M\) a \(A\)-\(B\) biModule, \(N\) a \(A\)-\(C\) biModule. The abelian beam \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)\) is canonically equipped with a structure of \(B\)-\(C\) biModule. In particular when \(A\) is a commutative ring bundle and when \(M\) and \(N\) are \(A\)-Modules, \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)\) is canonically provided with a structure of \(A\)-Module. Canonical isomorphisms b) , c), d) of 12.3 are isomorphisms of bi-Modules.

We will limit ourselves to giving indications. For any object \(X\) of \(E\), we have \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)(X) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,j_X^*N)\) (12.3). Consequently, the commutative group \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)(X)\) is canonically provided with a structure of \(B(X)\)-\(C(X)\) bimodule (11.1.3) which we verify that it is functorial in \(X\). Other assertions are left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12.6

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(X\) be an object of \(A\), \(N\) be a \(A\)-Module (left to fix ideas). We have canonical isomorphisms of \(A\)-Modules: \[ \mathscr{H}om_E(X,N) \approx j_{X*}j_X^*N \approx \mathscr{H}om_A(A_X,N) ; \] the structure of \(A\)-Module on \(\mathscr{H}om_A(A_X,N)\) coming from the structure of biModule on \(A_X\) (11.3.4).
The first isomorphism results from 10.7, the second from the isomorphism of \(A\)-Modules \(N \approx \mathscr{H}om_A(A,N)\) and 12.3.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.7

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module on the right and \(N\) be a \(A\)-Module on the left. The functor which to any abelian sheaf \(P\) associates the commutative group \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,P))\) is representable by an abelian sheaf denoted \(M \otimes_A N\) and called the tensor product on \(A\) of \(M\) and \(N\).

We have a canonical injection of \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,P))\) in the whole \(\mathrm{Hom}(M,\mathscr{H}om(N,P)) \approx \mathrm{Hom}(M \times N,P)\); We immediately see, returning to the definitions, that a morphism \(f\) of bundles of sets of \(M \times N\) in \(P\) comes from an element of \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,P))\) if and only if for every object \(X\) of \(E\), \(f(X) : M(X) \times N(X) \to P(X)\) is a \(A(X)\)-bilinear map of \(M(X) \times N(X)\) in \(P(X)\). Let us call morphism \(A\)-bilinear the morphisms of sheaves of sets of \(M \times N\) in \(P\) that have this property. It is therefore a question of representing the functor of the morphisms \(A\)-bilinears from \(M \times N\) in \(P\). To do this we proceed as in the ordinary case i.e. as in the case where \(E\) is the punctual topos. Consider the eight morphisms: \[ \begin{aligned} (i) &: M \times M \times N \to M \times N & 1 \le i \le 3\\ (i) &: M \times N \times N \to M \times N & 4 \le i \le 6\\ (i) &: M \times A \times N \to M \times N & 7 \le i \le 8 \end{aligned} \] defined by the formulas: \[ \begin{aligned} (1) \quad & (m_1,m_2,n) \mapsto (m_1+m_2,n)\\ (2) \quad & (m_1,m_2,n) \mapsto (m_1,n)\\ (3) \quad & (m_1,m_2,n) \mapsto (m_2,n)\\ (4) \quad & (m,n_1,n_2) \mapsto (m,n_1+n_2)\\ (5) \quad & (m,n_1,n_2) \mapsto (m,n_1)\\ (6) \quad & (m,n_1,n_2) \mapsto (m,n_2)\\ (7) \quad & (m,a,n) \mapsto (ma,n)\\ (8) \quad & (m,a,n) \mapsto (m,an) ; \end{aligned} \] where the formula \((i)\) describes the application \((i)(X)\) for the variable objects \(X\) of \(E\). Let us denote \(L\) the functor "sheaf free-begotten abelian". It is clear that the largest quotient (in the sense of the abelian sheaves of \(L(M \times N)\)) which equalizes the morphism \(L(1)\) to \(L(2)+L(3)\), \(L(4)\) to \(L(5)+L(6)\) and \(L(7)\) to \(L(8)\) represents the functor of \(A\)-bilinear morphisms from \(M \times N\) into \(P\).

We note that the functor \(P \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_A(N,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(M,P))\) is also canonically isomorphic to functor of \(A\)-bilinear maps of \(M \times N\) into \(P\). Consequently the tensor product \(M \otimes_A N\) represents also the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(N,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(M,P))\). We therefore have isomorphisms, functorial in all arguments: \[ (12.8.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(M \otimes_A N,P) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,P)) , \] \[ (12.8.2) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(M \otimes_A N,P) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(N,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(M,P)) . \]
It follows from (12.8.1), or from (12.8.2) and (12.2) that the functor \(\otimes_A\) commutes at the limits inductive in its two arguments.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.10

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(A'\) be a prebundle of rings on \(C\), \(M'\) (resp. \(N'\)) a \(A'\)-module on the right (resp. on the left), \(A,M,N\) the beams associated with \(A'\), \(M'\) and \(N'\) respectively. The beam associated with the prebundle \(X \mapsto M'(X) \otimes_{A'(X)} N'(X)\) (\(X \operatorname{ob} C\)) is canonically isomorphic to the bundle \(M \otimes_A N\).

The pre-bundle \(X \mapsto M'(X) \otimes_{A'(X)} N'(X)\) can be built from the pre-bundles \(M'\), \(N'\) and \(A'\) by the operations indicated in the demonstration of 12.7. As the “associated beam” functor commutes to finite projective and inductive limits and the functors "free generated abelian object", the formation of the product tensor commutates to the "associated bundle" functor.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.11

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module on the right, \(N\) be a \(A\)-Module on the left, \(X\) be an object of \(E\).

  1. a)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \[ j_X^*(M \otimes_A N) \approx (j_X^*M) \otimes_{(j_X^*A)} (j_X^*N) . \]

Let there also be \(P\) a \(A/X\)-Module on the right and \(Q\) a \(A/X\) module on the left.

  1. b)

    We have canonical isomorphisms (projection formulas): \[ j_{X!}(P \otimes_{A|X} j_X^*N) \approx (j_{X!}P) \otimes_A N \] \[ j_{X!}(j_X^*M \otimes_{A|X} Q) \approx M \otimes_A (j_{X!}Q) . \]

Let’s demonstrate a). For any abelian sheaf \(R\) on \(E_{/X}\), we have the sequence of functorial isomorphisms in \(R\): \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*(M \otimes_A N),R) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(M \otimes_A N,j_{X*}R) & (11.2.2.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(M \otimes_A N,j_{X*}R) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,j_{X*}R)) & (12.8.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,j_{X*}R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,R)) & (12.3)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,j_{X*}\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,R)) & (11.2.2.)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(j_X^*M,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*M \otimes_A j_X^*N,R) & (12.8.1) \end{aligned} \] Let us show the first isomorphism of b). For any abelian sheaf \(R\), we have a sequence of isomorphisms functorials in \(R\): \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_{X!}(P \otimes_{A|X} j_X^*N),R) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(P \otimes_{A|X} j_X^*N,j_X^*R) & (11.3.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(P \otimes_{A|X} j_X^*N,j_X^*R) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,j_X^*R)) & (12.8.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_X^*N,j_X^*R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,j_X^*\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,R)) & (12.3)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A|X}(P,j_X^*\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}P,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,R)) & (11.3.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(j_{X!}P,\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,R)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Z}}(j_{X!}P \otimes_A N,R) & (12.8.1) \end{aligned} \] The second isomorphism is obtained in an analogous way using (12.8.2).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12.12

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(A\), \(B\), \(C\) three sheaves of rings on \(E\), \(M\) a \(B\)-\(A\) biModule, \(N\) a \(A\)-\(C\) biModule. The abelian beam \(M \otimes_A N\) is canonically equipped with a structure of \(B\)-\(C\) biModule. In particular, when \(A\) is a commutative ring bundle and when \(M\) and \(N\) are \(A\)-Modules, \(M \otimes_A N\) is canonically provided with a structure of \(A\)-Module. The isomorphisms of 12.9 are isomorphisms of biModules.

For any object \(X\) of \(E\), \(j_X^*M\) is a \(A|X\)-Module on the right on which operates, on the left, the ring \(B(X)\) and similarly, \(j_X^*N\) is a \(A/X\)-Module on the left on which \(C(X)\) operates, on the right. By functoriality, the beam abelian \(j_X^*M \otimes_{A/X} j_X^*N \approx j_X^*(M \otimes_A N)\) is provided with a structure of \(B(X)\)-\(C(X)\) "object", structure which varies functionally in \(X\). As a result \(M \otimes_A N\) is provided with a structure of \(B\)-\(C\) biModule. This structure of \(B(X)\)-\(C(X)\) object on \(j_X^*(M \otimes_A N)\) is obviously reflected on the "morphism \(A\)-bilinear" functor. We then notes that, when \(A\) is commutative and when \(M\) and \(N\) are \(A\)-Modules, the structures of \(A\)-Modules on the right and on the left that we obtain are "equal" and therefore \(M \otimes_A N\) is in this case a \(A\)-Module. The last assertion is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12.13

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module on the left (resp. on the right), \(X\) be object of \(E\). We have (with the notation \(A_X\) of 11.3.3) a canonical isomorphism \[ A_X \otimes_A M \approx j_{X!}j_X^*M \quad (\text{resp. } M \otimes_A A_X \approx j_{X!}j_X^*M) . \]
results from the projection formulas (12.11).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12.14

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(A\) and \(B\) two sheaves of rings on \(E\), \(M\) a \(A\)-Module with right, \(N\) a \(A\)-\(B\) biModule, \(P\) a \(B\)-Module on the right. We have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_B(M \otimes_A N,P) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(M, \mathscr{H}om_B(N,P)) . \]

From (12.8.1) we derive a canonical \(A\)-bilinear morphism \(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathbf{Z}}(N,P) \times N \to P\); hence, restricting to \(\mathscr{H}om_B(N,P) \times N\) (which is a subsheaf), a \(A\)-bilinear morphism \(\mathscr{H}om_B(N,P) \times N \to P\) which we immediately verify that it is \(B\)-linear on the second factor. We therefore have a canonical morphism of \(B\)-Modules \(\mathscr{H}om_B(N,P) \otimes_A N \to P\) ; hence a canonical, functorial application in \(M\): \[ (12.14.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_A(M, \mathscr{H}om_B(N,P)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_B(M \otimes_A N,P) . \] Let us show that this morphism of functors is an isomorphism. As the two members transform the inductive limits of \(M\) in projective limits, it suffices to show that (12.14.1) is an isomorphism, when \(M\) browses a generating family of the category \(E_A\). It is therefore sufficient to show that (12.14.1) is an isomorphism when \(M = A_X\) where \(X\) is an object of \(E\). Verification is then immediate.
13. Morphism of ringed topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 13.1

Let \((E,A)\) and \((E',A')\) be two ringed topos. A ringed topos morphism \(u : (E,A) \to (E',A')\) is a pair \((m,\theta)\) where \(m : E \to E'\) is a topos morphism (IV.3.1) and \(\theta : m^*A' \to A\) is a morphism of Rings.

As the functor \(m^* : E' \to E\) is adjoint on the left to the functor \(m_* : E \to E'\), give ourselves a morphism of ringed topos \((m,\theta) : (E,A) \to (E',A')\) amounts to giving ourselves a morphism of topos \(m : E \to E'\) and a ring sheaf morphism \(\theta : A' \to m_*A\).
To a ringed topos morphism \(u = (m,\theta) : (E,A) \to (E',A')\), we associate two functors remarkable between the categories of Modules:
13.2.1 The direct image functor for Modules

Let \(M\) be a \(A\)-Module on the left (resp. on the right). The object \(m_*M\) has a structure of \(m_*A\)-Module; hence, by restriction of the scalars by the canonical morphism \(\theta' : A' \to m_*A\), a \(A'\)-Module noted \(u_*(M)\) and called the direct image of \(M\) by the morphism \(u\).
13.2.2 The reciprocal image functor for Modules

Let \(N\) be a \(A'\)-Module on the left (resp. on the right). The object \(m^*N\) of \(E\) has a structure of \(m^*A'\)-Module on the left (resp. right). The \(A\)-Module on the left \(A \otimes_{m^*A'} m^*N\) (resp. on the right \(m^*N \otimes_{m^*A'} A\)) where \(A\) is provided with the structure of \(m^*A'\)-Module defined by \(\theta : m^*A' \to A\), is denoted \(u^*N\) and is called the reciprocal image of Module \(N\) by the ringed topos morphism \(u\).

Note that for all \(A\)-Module \(M\), the sheaf of sets and the abelian sheaf underlying \(u_*M\) is beam \(m_*M\). We therefore most often use the notation \(u_*\): direct image for the beams of sets. On the other hand for a \(A'\)-Module \(N\), the bundle of sets or the abelian bundle underlying \(u^*N\) is neither equal to nor isomorphic in general to \(m^*N\) (except when \(\theta\) is an isomorphism). So there is place to distinguish between the reciprocal image for Modules and the reciprocal image for bundles of sets or the abelian fascicles. We most often use the notation \(u^{-1} : E' \to E\) to designate the functor \(m^*\) inverse image for set bundles. The functor \(u^{-1}\) is called the reciprocal image functor "setist" by the ringed topos morphism \(u\), as opposed to the reciprocal image "in the modules sense" \(u^*\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 13.3

Let \((C,A)\) and \((C',A')\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed sites. A morphism of ringed sites \(u : (C,A) \to (C',A')\) is a pair \((m,\theta)\) where \(m\) is a morphism of the site \(C\) into the site \(C'\) (IV 4.9) and \(\theta : m^*A' \to A\) a morphism of Rings.
A ringed topos morphism is a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed sites. A morphism of ringed sites gives rise to a morphism between the corresponding ringed topos (11.1.1 and IV 4.9.1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 13.4

Let \(u : (E,A) \to (E',A')\) be a morphism of ringed topos.

  1. a)

    For a variable \(A\)-Module on the left (resp. right) \(M\), and for a \(A'\)-Module on left (resp. right) variable \(N\), we have canonical bifunctional isomorphisms (called isomorphisms addition) \[ (13.4.1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_A(u^*N,M) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(N,u_*M) , \] \[ (13.4.2) \qquad u_* \mathscr{H}om_A(u^*N,M) \approx \mathscr{H}om_{A'}(N,u_*M) . \]

  2. b)

    For a variable object \(X\) of \(E'\), we have a canonical, functorial isomorphism in \(X\): \[ (13.4.3) \qquad u^*A'_X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } A_{u^{-1}(X)} , \] where \(A'_X\) and \(A_{u^{-1}(X)}\) designate the free Modules generated (11.3.3).

  3. c)

    When \(A'\) is commutative and when the canonical morphism \(u^{-1}A' \to A\) is central (resp. when the canonical morphism \(u^{-1}A' \to A\) is an isomorphism) we have, for a \(A'\)-Module on the right \(M\) variable and a \(A'\)-Module on the left \(N\) variable, a canonical bifunctoral isomorphism: \[ (13.4.4) \qquad u^*M \otimes_A u^*N \approx u^*(M \otimes_{A'} N) \] \[ (\text{resp. } (13.4.5) \qquad u^*M \otimes_A u^*N \approx u^{-1}(M \otimes_{A'} N) ). \]

First of all we have a canonical isomorphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(u^*N,M) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{u^{-1}A'}(u^{-1}N,M)\) (12.12), then an isomorphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_{u^{-1}A'}(u^{-1}N,M) \approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(N,u_*M)\) (III 1.7); hence 13.4.1. Let's show off isomorphism 13.4.2. For any object \(X\) of \(E'\), we have the sequence of functorial isomorphisms: \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,u_*\mathscr{H}om_A(u^*N,M)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}(u^{-1}X, \mathscr{H}om_A(u^*N,M)) & \text{(adjonction)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}(u^{-1}X, \mathscr{H}om_A(u^*N,M)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(u^*N, \mathscr{H}om_E(u^{-1}X,M)) & (12.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(u^*N, \mathscr{H}om_E(u^{-1}X,M)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(N,u_*\mathscr{H}om_E(u^{-1}X,M)) & (13.4.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_A(N,u_*\mathscr{H}om_E(u^{-1}X,M)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(N, \mathscr{H}om_E(X,u_*M)) & (10.3.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(N, \mathscr{H}om_E(X,u_*M)) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(X, \mathscr{H}om_{A'}(N,u_*M)) & (12.1) \end{aligned} \] Formula 13.4.5 is then obtained from formula 13.3.2. by addition (definition of the tensor product (12.7)). Formula 13.4.4 is deduced from 13.4.5 using the commutativity of the tensor product when the ring base is commutative (12.8). Finally, to prove 13.4.3, we consider the sequence of isomorphisms \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_A(u^*A'_X,M) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(A'_X,u_*M) & (13.4.1)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(A'_X,u_*M) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,u_*M) & (11.3.3)\\ \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,u_*M) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_E(u^{-1}X,M) & \text{(adjonction)}\\ \mathrm{Hom}_E(u^{-1}X,M) &\approx \mathrm{Hom}_A(A_{u^{-1}X},M) & (11.3.3) \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 13.5

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(x : P \to E\) a point of \(E\), \(F \mapsto F_x\) the functor associated fiber (IV 6.1). The fiber functor in \(x\) transforms the tensor product of \(A\)-Modules into a tensor product (ordinary) of \(A_x\)-modules.
