The tensor product in \(P\) is the ordinary tensor product of the modules (\(P\) the point topos is the category of sets). The assertion therefore follows from (13.4.4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 13.6

Let \(u : (E,A) \to (E',A')\) be a morphism of ringed topos. The functor \(u_*\) image direct for Modules on the right or on the left commutes to projective limits and in particular is exact at left. The functor \(u^*\) reciprocal image for right or left modules switches at inductive limits and in particular is exact on the right.
This results from formula 13.4.1 (I 2.11).

Note that the functor \(u^*\), reciprocal image for Modules, is not, in general, exact, so that the functor \(u^{-1}\), a set reciprocal image, is exact. However, we can affirm the accuracy of \(u^*\) when the canonical morphism \(u^{-1}A' \to A\) is flat (right or left) (V 1.8). This is particularly the case when the canonical morphism \(u^{-1}A' \to A\) is an isomorphism.

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(A'\) a pre-bundle of rings on \(C\), and let us denote \(A\) the beam associated with \(A'\). The category of pre-bundles of \(A'\)-modules which are beams is equivalent to the category of \(A\)-Modules. We sometimes uses the notation \(\mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(M,N)\) to refer to the group \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,N)\) (11.1.2). Likewise, and improperly, we use the notations \(\mathscr{H}om_{A'}(M,N)\) and \(M \otimes_{A'} N\) to designate the beams \(\mathscr{H}om_A(M,N)\) and \(M \otimes_A N\). When \(A' = k_E\) is the constant presheaf, defined by an ordinary ring \(k\), we also write \(\mathscr{H}om_k\), \(\otimes_k\) instead of \(\mathscr{H}om_{k_E}\), \(\otimes_{k_E}\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 13.9 (Locally ringed topos)

cf. [9] for more information in the following order of ideas).

Let \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) be a commutatively ringed topos. For \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_E(X)\), let \(X_f\) the most grand sous-objet de \(X\) sur lequel \(f\) soit inversible.

  1. a)

    Show that for \(X' \in \operatorname{ob} E_{/X}\), \(f_{X'}\) is invertible if and only if the morphism structural \(X' \to X\) is factorized by \(X_f\), and that for \(f,g \in \mathcal{O}_E(X)\), we have \[ X_{fg} = X_f \cap X_g . \]

  2. b)

    Show that the following conditions (i) to (iii) are equivalent:

    (i) For \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) and \(f,g \in \mathcal{O}_E(X)\), we have \[ X_{f+g} \subset \mathrm{Sup}(X_f, X_g) \]

    (ii) For \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) and \(f,g \in \mathcal{O}_E(X)\) such that \(f + g\) is invertible, we have \(X = \mathrm{Sup}(X_f, X_g)\).

    (iii) Pour \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E\) et \(f \in \mathcal{O}_E(X)\), on a \[ X = \mathrm{Sup}(X_f, X_{1-f}) . \]

    Show that these conditions imply the following condition (iv), and are equivalent to the latter if \(E\) has enough points:

    (iv) For any point \(p\) of \(E\), the fiber ring \(\mathcal{O}_{E,p}\) is a local ring.

    When the equivalent conditions (i) to (iii) are satisfied, we will say that \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) is a topos locally ringed, and we similarly say that a ringed site is a locally ringed site if the corresponding ringed topos is a topos localement annelé.

  3. c)

    Let \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) and \((E',\mathcal{O}_{E'})\) be two locally ringed topos, and \(f\) be a topos morphism ringed from the first to the second. Show that the following condition (i) implies condition (ii) and it is equivalent if \(E\) has enough points:

    (i) For all \(X \in \operatorname{ob} E'\) and \(s' \in \mathcal{O}_{E'}(X)\), setting \(X = f^{-1}(X)\), \(s = f^*(s')\), we have \[ X'_s = f^{-1}(X_s) . \]

    (ii) For any point \(p\) of \(E\), setting \(p' = f(p)\), the natural homomorphism on the fibers \[ \mathcal{O}_{E',p'} \to \mathcal{O}_{E,p} \] is a local homomorphism of local rings.

    When condition (i) is satisfied, we will say that \(f\) is a morphism of locally ringed topos, or another admissible morphism of locally ringed topos if confusion is to be feared. We designate by \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtoplocan}}(E,E')\) the full subcategory of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtopan}}(E,E')\) of all topos morphisms ringed from \(E\) into \(E'\) defined by the admissible morphisms of \(E\) into \(E'\).

  4. d)

    Suppose that \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) is the ringed topos associated with a ringed topological space \((X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) (IV 2.1). Show that for \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) to be locally ringed, it is necessary and sufficient that \((X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) be localement annelé, i.e. que pour tout \(x \in X\), la fibre \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) soit un anneau local. (Note that in criterion (iv) of b), it suffices to take the point \(p\) in a conservative family of points of \(E\)).

    Let \(f : (X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to (X',\mathcal{O}_{X'})\) be a morphism of ringed spaces, where \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{X'}\) are sheaves of local rings. Show that the corresponding ringed topos morphism \(\mathrm{Top}(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathrm{Top}(X',\mathcal{O}_{X'})\) is admissible si et seulement si il en est de même pour le morphisme \(f\), i.e. si et seulement si pour tout \(x \in X\), \(\mathcal{O}_{X',f(x)} \to \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local homomorphism of local rings.

  5. e)

    Let \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) and \((E',\mathcal{O}_{E'})\) be two locally annealed topos, such that \(E\) has sufficient points and that \((E',\mathcal{O}_{E'})\) is equivalent to the ringed topos defined by a schema \((X',\mathcal{O}_{X'})\); prove that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtoplocan}}(E,E')\) is equivalent to a discrete category, i.e. it is a groupoid (all morphism is a rigid isomorphism (the automorphism groups of objects are the unit groups). (Hint: be reduced to the case where \((E,\mathcal{O}_E)\) is the point topos ringed by a body). We will remember that, on the other hand, \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(E,E')\) is not in general equivalent to a discrete category, even if \(E\) and \(E'\) are defined topos by diagrams (and even if \(E\) is the punctual topos), cf. 4.2.3).

  6. f)

    Let \(E\) be a locally ringed topos, \(P\) the ringed topos defined by the ringed space \(\mathrm{Spec}\, k\), where \(k\) is a body. Show that the admissible morphisms of locally ringed topos \(P \to E\) correspond to the couples \((p,u)\) of a point \(p\) of \(E\), and of an injection of \(k(p)\) in \(k\), where \(k(p)\) is the residual body of the ring local \(\mathcal{O}_{E,p}\). Generalize into a statement exhibiting the admissible morphisms of a locally ringed topos punctual in \(E\) (generalizing EGA I 2.4.4).

    We call geometric point of a locally ringed topos \(E\) any admissible morphism in \(E\) of the locally ringed topos defined by a ringed space of the form \(\mathrm{Spec}(k)\), where \(k\) is a field algebraically closed. Define the category of geometric points of \(E\) (implied: corresponding to bodies \(k \in \mathcal{U}\)), denoted \(\underline{\text{Ptgéom}}(E)\), and a canonical functor \(\underline{\text{Ptgéom}}(E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\). Show that this functor is faithful if and only if \(E\) has no point i.e. \(\underline{\mathrm{Point}}(E)\) is the empty category.

  7. g)

    Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(\mathcal{V}\) a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\). Define a 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Top})_{/E}\) in terms of the object \(E\) of the 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Top})\) (3.3.1), drawing inspiration from 5.14 a). When \(E\) is ringed by a Ring \(A\), similarly define a 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Topan})_{/E}\), and when \(E\) is locally annealed, using the notion of admissible morphism (cf. c)), define a 2-category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-Topanloc})_{/E}\), admitting \(\underline{\text{Ptgéom}}(E)\) (cf. f)) as a full subcategory.

pages.title: 14. Modules on a topos defined by gluing

blocks.html: 14. Modules on a topos defined by gluing

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\mathscr{U}\) be an open subtopos of \(E\), \(\mathscr{F}\) be the closed subtopos complementary, \(j : \mathscr{U} \to E\) and \(i : \mathscr{F} \to E\) the canonical morphisms (9.3). The topos \(\mathscr{U}\) is annealed by \(j^*A\) (11.2.2) and the topos morphism \(i\), completed in an obvious way, becomes a ringed topos morphism. In this number, the topos \(\mathscr{F}\) will be ringed by the bundle \(i^*A\), denoted \(A|F\), and the morphism \(j\), completed in obvious, is then a morphism of ringed topos.

We therefore have two morphisms of ringed topos: \[ j : (\mathscr{U},A|U) \to (E,A) \quad \text{et} \quad i : (\mathscr{F},A|F) \to (E,A) ; \] hence five functors between the corresponding categories of Modules (on the left to fix the ideas): \[ (14.2.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ {{}_{A|U}\mathscr{U}} \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{j_!} \ar@<-1ex>[r]_-{j_*} & {{}_A E} \ar[l]_-{j^*} \ar@<1ex>[r]^-{i^*} & {{}_{A|F}\mathscr{F}} \ar@<1ex>[l]^-{i_*} } \] Each functor in the diagram (14.2.1) is appended on the left to the one below it.

We know that the functor \(X \mapsto (j^*X, i^*X ; i^*X \to i^*j_* j^*X)\) is an equivalence of \(E\) in the topos \((\mathscr{U},\mathscr{F}, i^*j_*)\) (9.5.4). This equivalence induces an equivalence between the categories of Modules corresponding and consequently the functor \(P \mapsto (j^*P,i^*P ; i^*P \to i^*j_* j^*P)\) is an equivalence, denoted \(\varphi\), of the category \({}_A E\) in the category \(({}_{A|U}\mathscr{U}, {}_{A|F}\mathscr{F}, i^*j_*)\). The functors of (14.2.1) composed with \(\varphi\) or \(\varphi^{-1}\) are then the functors: \[ \begin{aligned} \varphi \circ j_* &: M \mapsto (M,i^*j_*M ; i^*j_*M \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id}\ } i^*j_*M) ,\\ j^* \circ \varphi^{-1} &: (M,N ; N \to i^*j_*M) \mapsto M ,\\ \varphi \circ j_! &: M \mapsto (M,0 ; 0 \to i^*j_*M) ,\\ \varphi \circ i_* &: N \mapsto (0,N ; N \to 0) ,\\ i^* \circ \varphi^{-1} &: (M,N ; N \to i^*j_*M) \mapsto N \end{aligned} \] The reader will be able, as an exercise, to explain the different addition morphisms.

Let's note \[ (14.4.1) \qquad i^! : {}_A E \to {}_{A|F}\mathscr{F} \] the functor defined by the formula: \[ (14.4.2) \qquad i^! \circ \varphi^{-1}(M,N ; N \xrightarrow{\ u\ } i^*j_*M) = \mathrm{Ker}(u) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 14.5

The functor \(i^!\) is right adjoint to the functor \(i_*\). The addition morphism \(i_*i^! \to \mathrm{id}\) is a monomorphism. Functors \(i^!\) (for variable rings) commute to functors restriction of scalars.

It is clear that any morphism \[ (0,N ; 0) \to (M,N ; N \xrightarrow{\ u\ } i^*j_* M) \] factors in a unique way by \((0, \mathrm{ker}(u) ; 0)\); which demonstrates the addition property. The others assertions are trivial.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 14.6

For any object \(P\) of \({}_A E\), we have the exact functorial sequences in \(P\): \[ (14.6.1) \qquad 0 \to j_!j^*P \to P \to i_*i^*P \to 0 ; \] \[ (14.6.2) \qquad 0 \to i_*i^!P \to P \to j_*j^*P , \] where the non-trivial arrows are the addition arrows.

Let us first notice that a sequence \[ (M',N';u') \to (M,N ; u) \to (M'',N'' ; u'') \] of \(({}_{A|U}\mathscr{U}, {}_{A|F}\mathscr{F} ; i^*j_*)\) is exact if and only if the corresponding sequences \[ M' \to M \to M'' \qquad N' \to N \to N'' \] are accurate.

The sequences (14.6.1) and (14.6.2) are transformed by the equivalence \(\varphi\) into sequences of the type (14.3): \[ \begin{aligned} &0 \to (M,0 ; 0) \to (M,N ; u) \to (0,N ; 0) \to 0\\ &0 \to (0,\mathrm{ker}(u);0) \to (M,N,u) \to (M,i^*j_*M ; \mathrm{id}) . \end{aligned} \] Verifying the correctness of these sequences is trivial.

The exact sequence (14.6.2) allows us to obtain a new interpretation of the functor \(i_*i^!\). Indeed either \(X\) an object from \(E\). From (14.6.2) we derive the exact sequence of commutative groups: \[ 0 \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,i_*i^!P) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,P) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,j_*j^*P) . \] Let us further note \(U\) the open of \(E\), final object of the open subtopos \(\mathscr{U}\). It results from the addition properties of functors \(j_*\), \(j^*\) and \(j_!^{\mathrm{ens}}\) that the group \(\mathrm{Hom}_E(X,j_*j^*P)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Hom}_E(X \times U,P)\) and that the morphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_E(X,P) \to \mathrm{Hom}_E(X,j_*j^* P)\) is none other than the defined morphism by the monomorphism \(X \times U \to X\) (IV 5). In other words (8.5.2):

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 14.8

The sections of \(i_*i^! P\) on \(E_{/X}\) are the sections of \(P\) on \(E_{/X}\) whose support (9.3.5) contains \(F\) (notation of 5.9.1). In other words, \(i_*i^! P\) is the largest supported sub-bundle of \(P\) contained in \(F\).

By abuse of language, we sometimes say that \(i_*i^! P\) is the sub-Module of \(P\) defined by the sections from \(P\) to support in \(F\). It follows from 8.5.3 that this terminology only extends to general topos a terminology used for sheaf topos on topological spaces.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 14.10
  1. 1)

    The functor \(P \mapsto i_*i^*P\) is isomorphic to the functor \(P \mapsto i_*i^* A \otimes_A P\).

  2. 2)

    The functor \(P \mapsto i^! i^*P\) is isomorphic to the functor \(P \mapsto \mathscr{H}om_A(i_*i^*A,P)\).

The exact sequence (14.6.1) is written, in the particular case where \(P\) is the \(A\)-Module \(A\): \[ (14.10.1) \qquad 0 \to A_U \to A \to i_*i^*A \to 0 \] where \(A_U\) is the \(A\)-Free module generated by the open \(U\) corresponding to the open subtopos \(\mathscr{U}\) (9 and 11.3.3). For all \(A\)-Module \(P\), the \(A\)-Modules \(j_!j^* P\) and \(j_*j^* P\) are canonically isomorphic respectively to \(A_U \otimes_A P\) and \(\mathscr{H}om_A(A_U, P)\) (12.3. and 12.6). Furthermore, the canonical morphisms \(j_!j^*P \to P\) and \(P \to j_*j^* P\) come, modulo these isomorphisms, from the monomorphism \(A_U \to A\). We therefore draw from the following exact 14.10.1, two exact sequences (12.2 and 12.11): \[ \begin{aligned} &j_! j^* P \to P \to i_* i^* A \otimes_A P \to 0 ,\\ &0 \to \mathscr{H}om_A(i_* i^* A,P) \to P \to j_* j^* P ; \end{aligned} \] hence the isomorphisms announced by comparison with (14.6.1) and (14.6.2).
